print(f"P(class 0), P(class 1) for first 3 samples:
{proba.round(3)}")

Key Terms — SVM

TermPlain EnglishExample
HyperplaneThe decision boundary. In 2D = a line. In 3D = a plane. In n-dimensional space = an (n-1)-dimensional surface.A line separating spam from non-spam emails in 2D feature space
MarginThe gap between the decision boundary and the nearest points (support vectors) from each class. SVM maximises this gap.Wider margin = more robust model = less sensitive to individual point positions
Support VectorsThe training points closest to the decision boundary. ONLY these points define where the boundary is. All other points are irrelevant.If you remove a non-support-vector point from training data, the boundary doesn't change
C (regularisation)Controls margin width vs training error. Small C = wider margin, accepts some misclassifications (better generalisation). Large C = narrow margin, tries to classify everything (overfits).C=0.1: wide, forgiving. C=1000: narrow, strict.
KernelA function that computes similarity between data points. Different kernels allow SVM to find different shaped boundaries.RBF kernel: similarity decreases with distance. Enables circular/curved boundaries.
RBF KernelRadial Basis Function — the most common kernel. Measures similarity as a Gaussian function of distance. Enables highly non-linear boundaries.Two points very close = high similarity. Far apart = near-zero similarity.
Kernel TrickComputing similarity in a high-dimensional space without actually computing the high-dimensional coordinates. Makes non-linear SVM computationally feasible.RBF kernel implicitly maps to infinite dimensions but only computes exp(−γ||x−x'||²)
gammaRBF kernel parameter — controls the reach of each training point's influence. Small = far reach (smooth boundary). Large = close reach (complex wiggly boundary).gamma=0.001: point influences wide region. gamma=100: point only influences immediate neighbourhood.
Hard MarginRequires ALL training points to be correctly classified and outside the margin. Only works if data is perfectly linearly separable (no noise).Fails if even one outlier is on the wrong side
Soft MarginAllows some misclassifications in exchange for a wider margin. C controls this trade-off. This is what sklearn SVC uses by default.C=1: allows reasonable misclassifications for a wider margin
LinearSVCSVM with linear kernel, implemented differently (using liblinear). Much faster than SVC(kernel='linear') for large datasets.For text classification with 100K+ samples, always use LinearSVC
📍
K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)
The "Ask Your Neighbours" Algorithm — No Training, Pure Memory

What is KNN? (Plain English First)

Imagine you just moved to a new city and you want to know if a neighbourhood is safe. You don't have statistics — but you look at the 5 nearest houses and see what kind of families live there. If 4 out of 5 are happy families, you conclude the neighbourhood is safe.

That is exactly what KNN does with data. When it sees a new data point, it looks at the K closest points already in the dataset and takes a vote (classification) or averages their values (regression).

The KNN Algorithm — Step by Step

  1. Store — During "training", KNN just memorises all training data. There is NO model built.
  2. Measure Distance — When a new point arrives, calculate the distance between it and every training point.
  3. Sort — Sort all training points from nearest to farthest.
  4. Pick Top K — Take the K nearest ones.
  5. Vote (Classification) — The most common class among those K wins.
  6. Average (Regression) — The mean value of those K points becomes the prediction.

Real-World Analogies

Scenario KNN Equivalent
Recommending a movie based on your past 5 favourite films K=5 nearest movies in feature space → recommend the genre they share
Doctor diagnosing a patient by comparing to 3 similar past patients K=3 nearest patient records → majority diagnosis
Predicting house price from 10 similar nearby houses K=10, regression → average of those 10 prices

Why "Lazy" Learner?

KNN is called a lazy learner because it does zero work during training — it just stores data. All the computation happens at prediction time. This means:

  • Training is instant (just copy data to memory)
  • But prediction is slow — must compare against every training point
  • With 1 million training points, every single prediction requires 1 million distance calculations

Distance Metrics — How "Close" is Measured

The word "nearest" means nothing without defining what "distance" means. Here are the main options:

Metric Formula Best When Avoid When
Euclidean √(Σ(aᵢ−bᵢ)²) Continuous data, similar scales High dimensions (curse of dimensionality)
Manhattan Σ|aᵢ−bᵢ| Grid-like data, when outliers exist When diagonal moves are meaningful
Minkowski (Σ|aᵢ−bᵢ|ᵖ)^(1/p) General; p=1→Manhattan, p=2→Euclidean When you don't know which p to pick
Hamming Count of positions where aᵢ≠bᵢ Categorical / binary features Continuous numeric features

Critical Warning: Feature Scaling is MANDATORY for KNN

If "age" ranges from 20–80 and "salary" ranges from 20,000–200,000, the distance calculation will be completely dominated by salary. A tiny salary difference will matter far more than a huge age difference — even if age is the more important feature.

Always scale your features before using KNN:

  • StandardScaler — subtracts mean, divides by std dev → values centered around 0
  • MinMaxScaler — scales everything to [0, 1] range

Choosing K — The Most Important Decision

K Value Effect Problem
K = 1 Extremely flexible, fits every point Overfitting — memorises noise; bad on new data
K = 5–20 Balanced generalisation Usually the sweet spot — start here
K = N (all data) Always predicts majority class Underfitting — ignores the local neighbourhood

Rule of thumb: Start with K = √(number of training samples). Always use odd K for binary classification (prevents ties).

How KNN Votes to Classify

The yellow question mark is the new point. The dashed circle is the K=5 nearest-neighbor radius. Lines connect to each neighbor. 3 red, 2 blue → predicted class = Red.

Impact of K on Decision Boundary

K=1 creates wiggly, complex boundaries that perfectly fit training data but fail on test data. K=5 creates smooth boundaries that generalise well. K=all always predicts the majority class — it stops looking locally.

Distance Metrics Visualised

Same two points — different distances depending on which metric you use. Euclidean takes the straight-line path. Manhattan forces you to travel along a grid (like city blocks). Your choice of metric changes which points are considered "nearest".

External Reference: KNN Visual Explainer

KNeighborsClassifier — Every Parameter Explained

n_neighbors default=5
What it is: The value of K — how many nearest neighbours to consider when voting.
Think of it as: "How many neighbours do I ask before deciding?"
Impact of changing:
  • Low K (e.g., 1-3): Highly sensitive to individual points. Wiggly decision boundary. Overfits.
  • High K (e.g., 50+): Very smooth boundary. May underfit and miss local patterns.
  • K=1: Perfect training accuracy but terrible test accuracy (memorises noise).
How to tune: Use cross-validation across a range of K values (1 to 30). Plot validation accuracy vs K and pick the elbow.
for k in range(1, 31): model = KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=k)
weights default='uniform'
What it is: How much "say" each neighbour gets in the vote.
Options:
  • 'uniform' — All K neighbours vote equally. A neighbour at distance 0.1 and one at distance 10 have the same weight.
  • 'distance' — Closer neighbours vote louder. Weight = 1/distance. Far-away neighbours barely matter.
  • callable — You can pass your own custom weighting function.
When to use distance weights: When you believe very close neighbours are much more informative than far-away ones.
Example impact: If a new point has 3 very nearby red neighbours (distance 0.1) and 2 far blue neighbours (distance 5.0), uniform voting → possible tie; distance weighting → strongly red.
algorithm default='auto'
What it is: The data structure/algorithm used to find the K nearest neighbours efficiently.
Options:
  • 'auto' — Sklearn picks the best algorithm based on your data. Almost always start here.
  • 'ball_tree' — Organises data in a ball tree. Fast for many dimensions (>20 features). Uses Minkowski distances.
  • 'kd_tree' — Organises data in a KD-tree. Fast for low dimensions (<20 features). Only Euclidean/Manhattan.
  • 'brute' — Compares every training point one by one. Slow but always correct. Use for tiny datasets.
Speed comparison: For 100,000 data points, brute force calculates 100,000 distances per prediction; KD-tree only calculates ~17 (log₂(100000) ≈ 17).
leaf_size default=30
What it is: Only applies when algorithm is 'ball_tree' or 'kd_tree'. Determines when the tree stops splitting and just stores points in a leaf node.
Impact of changing:
  • Smaller leaf_size: Deeper tree → faster queries but more memory and slower build time.
  • Larger leaf_size: Shallower tree → faster build but slower queries (brute-force inside the leaf).
Typical range: 20–50. Rarely needs tuning unless you have very large datasets.
p default=2
What it is: The power parameter for the Minkowski distance metric.
Values:
  • p=1 → Manhattan distance (sum of absolute differences, like walking city blocks)
  • p=2 → Euclidean distance (straight-line distance — the default)
  • p=3+ → Higher-order Minkowski (rarely used in practice)
Only used when: metric='minkowski' (the default metric). If you explicitly set metric='manhattan', p is ignored.
metric default='minkowski'
What it is: The distance function used to measure "closeness".
Common values:
  • 'minkowski' — Default. Combined with p param: p=2 gives Euclidean, p=1 gives Manhattan.
  • 'euclidean' — Straight-line distance. Most common for continuous data.
  • 'manhattan' — City-block distance. Better when data has many outliers.
  • 'cosine' — Angle-based. Great for text data (measures direction, not magnitude).
  • 'hamming' — For binary/categorical features (fraction of positions that differ).
Rule: Use Euclidean for numeric data, cosine for text/NLP, Hamming for binary features.
n_jobs default=None
What it is: Number of CPU cores to use for distance calculations during prediction.
Values: None=1 core, -1=all available cores, 4=use 4 cores.
Impact: Prediction can be parallelised easily. Set n_jobs=-1 for large datasets to dramatically speed up prediction time.

Key Attributes After Fitting

Attribute What It Contains
classes_ Array of class labels the model knows about
effective_metric_ The actual metric being used (may differ from metric param due to auto-selection)
n_samples_fit_ Number of training samples stored (this IS the model)

Annotated Code — Every Line Explained


from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsClassifier   # import the classifier
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler      # MUST scale for KNN
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score   # for finding best K
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline                 # bundle scaler + model
import numpy as np

# ── Step 1: Scale features (CRITICAL for KNN) ────────────────────────────────
# Without scaling, salary (range 0-100k) dominates over age (range 20-80)
# StandardScaler makes every feature have mean=0 and std=1
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_train_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)  # fit on train, transform train
X_test_scaled  = scaler.transform(X_test)        # only transform test (no fit!)

# ── Step 2: Find the best K using cross-validation ───────────────────────────
# Try every K from 1 to 25 and measure cross-validation accuracy
k_scores = []
for k in range(1, 26):
    knn = KNeighborsClassifier(
        n_neighbors=k,      # number of neighbours to vote
        weights='uniform',  # each neighbour votes equally
        algorithm='auto',   # sklearn picks the fastest data structure
        metric='minkowski', # distance formula
        p=2,                # p=2 with minkowski = euclidean distance
    )
    # 5-fold cross-validation — splits data 5 ways, tests each split
    scores = cross_val_score(knn, X_train_scaled, y_train, cv=5)
    k_scores.append(scores.mean())   # average accuracy across 5 folds

best_k = np.argmax(k_scores) + 1    # +1 because range started at 1
print(f"Best K: {best_k}, Accuracy: {k_scores[best_k-1]:.3f}")

# ── Step 3: Train final model with best K ────────────────────────────────────
knn_final = KNeighborsClassifier(
    n_neighbors=best_k,    # use the best K we found above
    weights='distance',    # closer neighbours count more than farther ones
    algorithm='auto',      # auto picks kd_tree/ball_tree/brute appropriately
    leaf_size=30,          # only matters for ball_tree/kd_tree, default is fine
    metric='minkowski',    # euclidean distance (p=2 below)
    p=2,                   # p=2 → euclidean; change to 1 for manhattan
    n_jobs=-1,             # use all CPU cores for faster prediction
)
knn_final.fit(X_train_scaled, y_train)  # "training" = storing all data in tree

# ── Step 4: Predict and inspect ──────────────────────────────────────────────
y_pred = knn_final.predict(X_test_scaled)   # for each test point, find K nearest, vote

# Get probabilities (useful for confidence scores)
y_proba = knn_final.predict_proba(X_test_scaled)   # e.g. [0.33, 0.67] → 67% class 1

# Find actual neighbours for a single point (debugging / explainability)
distances, indices = knn_final.kneighbors(X_test_scaled[:1])
# distances: how far each neighbour is
# indices: row numbers in training data of those K neighbours
print(f"5 nearest neighbours are at distances: {distances[0].round(3)}")

# ── Step 5: Better approach — use Pipeline so scaling + model are bundled ─────
pipe = Pipeline([
    ('scaler', StandardScaler()),               # step 1: scale
    ('knn', KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=best_k, weights='distance')),  # step 2: classify
])
pipe.fit(X_train, y_train)       # automatically scales then trains
pipe.predict(X_test)             # automatically scales X_test before predicting
# Pipeline prevents data leakage (scaler fitted only on training data)

KNN for Regression


from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsRegressor

# Same parameters, but instead of voting → averages the K neighbours' values
knn_reg = KNeighborsRegressor(
    n_neighbors=5,       # average of 5 nearest house prices
    weights='distance',  # closer houses contribute more to the average
    algorithm='auto',
    metric='euclidean',
)
knn_reg.fit(X_train_scaled, y_train)
y_pred = knn_reg.predict(X_test_scaled)   # returns average of K neighbours

When to Use KNN

Use KNN When:

  • Dataset is small to medium (under ~50,000 rows) — prediction speed is acceptable
  • You need a quick baseline model with minimal coding (no hyperparameter complexity)
  • The decision boundary is irregular and complex (KNN adapts automatically)
  • You have very few features (fewer dimensions = distance metrics work better)
  • You want interpretability — you can literally show which K training points influenced the prediction
  • Your data changes frequently — KNN requires no retraining, just add/remove data points

Avoid KNN When:

  • Dataset is large (millions of rows) — prediction becomes impossibly slow
  • You have many features (>20) — curse of dimensionality makes distances meaningless
  • Features are on very different scales and you cannot/have not scaled them
  • You have categorical features with no meaningful distance (use label encoding carefully)
  • You need feature importance — KNN cannot tell you which features matter most
  • Storage is a concern — the entire training set must stay in memory forever

KNN vs Other Algorithms

Property KNN Decision Tree Logistic Reg
Training speed Instant Fast Fast
Prediction speed Slow Very fast Very fast
Needs scaling YES (critical) No Recommended
Interpretable Medium High High
Handles complex boundary Yes Yes No (linear only)

Curse of Dimensionality — Why KNN Breaks in High Dimensions

In 2D space, the "nearest neighbour" of a point is genuinely nearby. But as you add more dimensions:

  • In 100D space, all points are approximately the same distance from each other
  • The concept of "nearest" becomes meaningless — everything is equally far away
  • You would need exponentially more data to maintain the same density as dimensions increase

Solution: Use PCA to reduce dimensions before applying KNN, or switch to a tree-based model for high-dimensional data.

📊
Naive Bayes
Probability-Based Classification — Fast, Simple, Surprisingly Powerful

What is Naive Bayes? (Plain English First)

Imagine you are a detective. Someone tells you: "I received an email with the words FREE, WINNER, and CLAIM." You need to decide: spam or not spam?

A Naive Bayes detective would think: "In the past, 80% of emails containing FREE were spam. 90% with WINNER were spam. 75% with CLAIM were spam. Overall, 30% of all emails are spam." It then multiplies those probabilities together and concludes — this email is very probably spam.

This is Bayes' Theorem applied to classification: combine prior knowledge (base rates) with new evidence (observed features) to update your belief about what category something belongs to.

Bayes' Theorem — The Core Formula

P(Class | Features) = P(Features | Class) × P(Class) / P(Features)
Term Name Plain English
P(Class | Features) Posterior What we WANT: "Given these words, what's the probability it's spam?"
P(Features | Class) Likelihood How common is this evidence if it IS spam? (learned from training data)
P(Class) Prior What percentage of all emails are spam regardless of content?
P(Features) Evidence How common are these words overall? (constant — same for all classes, so ignored)

The "Naive" Assumption Explained

The algorithm is called Naive because it makes a massive, unrealistic assumption: all features are independent of each other given the class label.

In reality, the words "free" and "money" often appear together (correlated). But Naive Bayes pretends they don't — it treats each word as if its presence is completely independent of every other word.

Why is this "naive" assumption okay? Despite being wrong, the model still works remarkably well in practice, especially for text classification. The relative ranking of probabilities (spam vs not-spam) is preserved even when the actual probability values are wrong.

P(spam | "free","money","winner")
= P("free"|spam) × P("money"|spam) × P("winner"|spam) × P(spam)
= 0.80 × 0.70 × 0.90 × 0.30
= 0.151 (unnormalised)

Three Types of Naive Bayes

The three variants differ only in what statistical distribution they assume for P(Features | Class):

Type Feature Type Distribution Assumed Best Use Case
GaussianNB Continuous numbers Normal/bell curve Medical data, sensor readings
MultinomialNB Counts / frequencies Multinomial distribution Text classification (word counts)
BernoulliNB Binary (0/1) Bernoulli distribution Spam detection (word present/absent)

Log Probabilities — Why NB Uses log() Internally

When multiplying many small probabilities together (e.g., 0.001 × 0.003 × 0.0001 × ...), the product becomes astronomically small — smaller than what floating-point numbers can represent. This is called numerical underflow.

Solution: use logarithms! Since log(a × b) = log(a) + log(b), multiplication of probabilities becomes addition of log-probabilities. The class with the highest log-probability still wins, so the final prediction is identical — but without numerical errors.

Bayes' Theorem in Action — Spam Detection

Prior (base rate of spam) × Likelihood (how often spam contains this word) → Posterior (updated probability that this email is spam). The Evidence denominator is a normalising constant — same for all classes, so it doesn't change which class wins.

Three Types of Naive Bayes — Side by Side

Choose based on your data type. For text: use Multinomial (word counts) or Bernoulli (word present/absent). For continuous numbers: use Gaussian.

External Reference: Naive Bayes Explained

GaussianNB Parameters

var_smoothing default=1e-9
What it is: A tiny value (0.000000001 by default) added to the variance of each feature to prevent division by zero.
Why it exists: If a feature has zero variance in one class (e.g., all training patients with diabetes have exactly the same blood sugar), the Gaussian formula would divide by zero. Adding var_smoothing prevents this.
Impact of changing:
  • Larger value (e.g., 1e-3): Stronger smoothing — feature variance is inflated more. Makes model less sensitive to individual features. Acts as regularisation.
  • Smaller value (e.g., 1e-12): Less smoothing — model trusts the exact variance from data. Risk of numerical errors if variance is 0.
When to tune: If you see NaN predictions or warnings about zero variance, increase var_smoothing to 1e-6 or 1e-3.
priors default=None
What it is: The prior probability P(Class) for each class — how common each class is before seeing any features.
Default (None): Sklearn automatically estimates priors from the training data. If 30% of training examples are spam, P(spam)=0.30.
Custom priors example: If you know from domain knowledge that 5% of all real-world emails are spam (but your training set is balanced 50/50), set priors=[0.95, 0.05] to correct for the dataset imbalance.
Impact: Custom priors can dramatically improve real-world performance when your training set doesn't reflect the true class distribution.

MultinomialNB Parameters

alpha default=1.0
What it is: Laplace/Lidstone smoothing parameter for handling unseen words.
The problem it solves: If the word "cryptocurrency" never appeared in training spam emails, then P("cryptocurrency"|spam)=0. Multiplying by 0 makes the entire spam probability = 0, regardless of all other words. This is called the zero-frequency problem.
How alpha fixes it: Instead of 0/total, it becomes (0+alpha)/(total+alpha×vocab_size). Even unseen words get a tiny non-zero probability.
Values:
  • alpha=1.0 — Laplace smoothing (classic). Each unseen word gets probability 1/(total + vocab_size).
  • alpha=0.5 — Lidstone smoothing. Less smoothing than Laplace.
  • alpha=0.0 — No smoothing. Dangerous — any unseen word kills the probability.
Tune with cross-validation: Try values [0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 10.0].
fit_prior default=True
What it is: Whether to learn class prior probabilities from training data.
True (default): Estimate P(Class) from the frequency of each class in training set.
False: Use uniform priors — every class is assumed equally likely regardless of how many training examples each class has. Useful for heavily imbalanced datasets where you want the model to rely purely on feature patterns.

BernoulliNB Parameters

alpha default=1.0
Same as MultinomialNB — Laplace smoothing to handle unseen binary features.
binarize default=0.0
What it is: A threshold for automatically converting continuous features to binary (0/1).
Example: If word count feature value is 3.0 and binarize=0.5, then 3.0 > 0.5 → converted to 1 (word is present).
None: Don't binarize — assume input is already binary. Use this if you've pre-processed your features.

Shared Attributes After Fitting

Attribute Type Meaning
class_log_prior_ array [n_classes] Log of prior probability for each class — learned from training data
feature_log_prob_ array [n_classes, n_features] Log of P(feature|class) for each feature-class combination. This is the model's "knowledge".
class_count_ array [n_classes] Number of training examples per class

Complete Spam Classifier — Annotated


from sklearn.naive_bayes import MultinomialNB, GaussianNB, BernoulliNB
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import CountVectorizer, TfidfVectorizer
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split, GridSearchCV
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report, confusion_matrix

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# EXAMPLE 1: Text Classification with MultinomialNB (most common use)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

emails = [
    "free money winner claim prize",       # spam
    "meeting at 3pm tomorrow office",      # not spam
    "you won a free car click here",       # spam
    "please review the attached report",   # not spam
]
labels = [1, 0, 1, 0]   # 1=spam, 0=not spam

# CountVectorizer converts text → word count matrix
# e.g. "free money" → {free: 1, money: 1, meeting: 0, ...}
vectorizer = CountVectorizer()
X = vectorizer.fit_transform(emails)    # learn vocabulary AND transform
# X is now a sparse matrix: rows=documents, cols=vocab words, values=word counts

# Build Naive Bayes pipeline
pipe = Pipeline([
    ('vec', CountVectorizer()),   # convert text to word counts
    ('nb',  MultinomialNB(
        alpha=1.0,           # Laplace smoothing: unseen words get prob = 1/(total+vocab)
        fit_prior=True,      # learn P(spam) and P(not_spam) from training data
    ))
])

pipe.fit(emails[:3], labels[:3])    # train on first 3 examples
pred = pipe.predict(emails[3:])     # predict last example

# ── Check what the model learned ─────────────────────────────────────────────
nb_model = pipe.named_steps['nb']
vocab = pipe.named_steps['vec'].vocabulary_  # maps words to feature index

# Print learned log-probabilities for each word given spam
# Higher = word more associated with spam
import numpy as np
spam_word_probs = np.exp(nb_model.feature_log_prob_[1])    # class 1 = spam
# np.exp() converts log-probability back to probability

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# EXAMPLE 2: Tune alpha with GridSearchCV
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
param_grid = {'nb__alpha': [0.001, 0.01, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0]}
# 'nb__alpha' means: in pipeline step 'nb', tune parameter 'alpha'

search = GridSearchCV(pipe, param_grid, cv=5, scoring='f1')
search.fit(emails, labels)
print(f"Best alpha: {search.best_params_['nb__alpha']}")

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# EXAMPLE 3: GaussianNB for continuous features
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris

iris = load_iris()
X, y = iris.data, iris.target     # 4 continuous features (petal length, width, etc.)

gnb = GaussianNB(
    var_smoothing=1e-9,    # tiny value added to variance to prevent zero-division
    priors=None,           # learn priors from data (each class has 50 samples → prior=0.33 each)
)
gnb.fit(X, y)

# What did it learn?
print(f"Class priors: {gnb.class_prior_}")           # e.g. [0.33, 0.33, 0.33]
print(f"Mean per class:
{gnb.theta_}")              # mean of each feature for each class
print(f"Variance per class:
{gnb.var_}")            # variance of each feature per class

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# EXAMPLE 4: BernoulliNB for binary features
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
bnb = BernoulliNB(
    alpha=1.0,        # smoothing for unseen feature combinations
    binarize=0.0,     # values > 0.0 → 1 (present), values ≤ 0.0 → 0 (absent)
    fit_prior=True,   # estimate class priors from training data
)
# Use when your features are binary flags:
# e.g. [contains_free=1, contains_winner=1, contains_meeting=0, ...]

When to Use Naive Bayes

Use Naive Bayes When:

  • Text classification — spam detection, sentiment analysis, news categorisation. It's the go-to baseline for NLP.
  • You need a very fast, simple baseline to compare against. NB trains in milliseconds even on millions of documents.
  • Real-time streaming data — NB can update incrementally with partial_fit() without retraining from scratch.
  • You have very small training data — NB needs fewer examples than complex models to produce decent results.
  • Features are genuinely independent (rare, but when true, NB is optimal).

Avoid Naive Bayes When:

  • Features are highly correlated — the independence assumption is badly violated, leading to inaccurate probabilities.
  • You need well-calibrated probability estimates — NB probabilities are often extreme (very near 0 or 1) and not accurate absolute probabilities.
  • Your data has complex non-linear feature interactions — gradient boosting or neural networks will perform much better.
  • Features include negative values for MultinomialNB (it requires non-negative inputs).

Naive Bayes Strengths vs Weaknesses Summary

Strength Weakness
Extremely fast training and prediction Independence assumption rarely holds
Works well with small datasets Probability outputs poorly calibrated
Handles high-dimensional data well Cannot capture feature interactions
No hyperparameter tuning needed usually Sensitive to feature engineering quality
Online learning via partial_fit() Categorical feature encoding can mislead
🔵
K-Means Clustering
Unsupervised Learning — Group Similar Data Points Without Labels

What is K-Means? (Plain English First)

Imagine you have 1,000 customers and you want to group them into 3 types: budget shoppers, mid-range shoppers, and luxury shoppers. But nobody told you which group each customer belongs to. You only have data on their spending patterns.

K-Means does exactly this. It looks at the data and asks: "Can I find K natural groups where points within each group are more similar to each other than to points in other groups?"

The key difference from classification: there are no labels. K-Means is unsupervised — it discovers structure on its own.

The K-Means Algorithm — 4 Simple Steps

  1. Initialise: Randomly place K centroids (center points) in the feature space.
  2. Assign: For each data point, calculate its distance to every centroid. Assign the point to the nearest centroid's cluster.
  3. Update: For each cluster, calculate the mean (average position) of all its points. Move the centroid to that mean.
  4. Repeat: Go back to step 2. Keep repeating until centroids stop moving (or barely move below a tolerance threshold).

What Does "Centroid" Mean?

A centroid is the centre of a cluster — the average of all points assigned to that cluster. It's not a real data point — it's a calculated representative point.

Example: If cluster 1 has 3 customers at positions (2,3), (4,5), (3,7), the centroid is at ((2+4+3)/3, (3+5+7)/3) = (3.0, 5.0).

What is Inertia (WCSS)?

K-Means minimises inertia (also called Within-Cluster Sum of Squares — WCSS): the total sum of squared distances from each point to its cluster's centroid.

Inertia = Σ Σ (point - centroid)²
Sum over all clusters, then sum over all points in each cluster

Lower inertia = tighter clusters = better. But adding more clusters always reduces inertia. The trick is finding the sweet spot with the elbow method.

The Elbow Method — Choosing K

Since we don't know the "right" K upfront, we run K-Means multiple times with K=1,2,3,...10 and plot the inertia each time:

  • At K=1: One giant cluster. Very high inertia.
  • At K=3: Inertia drops dramatically — data has 3 natural groups.
  • At K=10: Inertia barely improves — just splitting real clusters unnecessarily.

Look for the "elbow" in the plot — the K where the curve bends sharply. That's the optimal K.

K-Means++ Initialisation — Why Random Init Fails

If initial centroids are placed randomly, they might all start in the same dense region, leaving other clusters unrepresented. This leads to poor final clusters and is called getting stuck in a local optimum.

K-Means++ fix: Place the first centroid randomly. Then for each subsequent centroid, choose a point with probability proportional to its squared distance from the nearest existing centroid. This spreads centroids out intelligently from the start.

Result: Faster convergence and better clusters. sklearn uses k-means++ by default (init='k-means++').

Limitations of K-Means

  • You must specify K — the algorithm cannot discover the number of clusters on its own.
  • Assumes spherical clusters — works poorly with elongated, crescent-shaped, or nested clusters.
  • Sensitive to outliers — one extreme point can pull a centroid far from the true cluster centre.
  • Feature scaling required — distance-based, so salary (0-100k) dominates over age (0-100).
  • Assumes similar cluster sizes — if one cluster has 1000 points and another has 10, it may split the big one and merge the small ones.

Real-World Use Cases

Industry What Gets Clustered Business Goal
E-commerce Customers (purchase history, frequency, value) Target each segment with different promotions
Healthcare Patient records (symptoms, test results) Discover disease subtypes for personalised treatment
Cybersecurity Network traffic patterns Identify anomalous behaviour clusters (potential attacks)
NLP Document embeddings Topic discovery without predefined categories

K-Means with 3 Clusters

Three clusters found: blue (top-left), green (bottom-centre), orange (right). The large circles with "C1/C2/C3" labels are the centroids — the average position of all points in each cluster. Inertia is minimised when points are tightly packed around their centroid.

The Iterative Process — How K-Means Converges

Step 1: Random initialisation of centroids. Step 2: Assign each point to nearest centroid (colour them). Step 3: Move centroid to the mean of its cluster (dashed = old position). Step 4: Repeat until centroids stop moving.

Elbow Method — Choose the Right K

Plot inertia for K=1 to 10. Find where the curve bends sharply (the "elbow"). That K gives the best balance between cluster tightness and simplicity. Here the elbow is at K=3.

External Reference: K-Means Animation

KMeans — Every Parameter Explained

n_clusters default=8
What it is: The value of K — how many clusters to find.
This is the most critical parameter. There is no universally correct K. Use the elbow method or silhouette score to choose.
Impact:
  • Too small K: Large, impure clusters that mix different types of points.
  • Too large K: Many tiny clusters; each natural group gets artificially split; overfitting.
How to find K: Plot inertia for K=1 to 15, look for the elbow. Or use silhouette score: higher is better (range −1 to 1).
from sklearn.metrics import silhouette_score
init default='k-means++'
What it is: How to initialise the centroids at the start.
Options:
  • 'k-means++' — Smart initialisation. Spreads initial centroids far apart, reducing chance of bad local optima. Almost always use this.
  • 'random' — Place centroids at random data points. Fast but more likely to converge to a poor solution.
  • ndarray — Pass your own starting centroid positions (useful when you have domain knowledge about cluster centres).
Rule: Always use 'k-means++' unless you have a specific reason not to.
n_init default='auto' (10 in older sklearn)
What it is: How many times to run the entire K-Means algorithm with different centroid initialisations.
Why multiple runs? Because of local optima — different starting positions can lead to different final clusters. Running 10 times and picking the best (lowest inertia) reduces this risk dramatically.
Impact:
  • Higher n_init: More reliable results, but proportionally slower.
  • n_init=1: Fast but risky — might get stuck in bad local optimum.
Recommendation: Keep default (10). Increase to 20-50 for important analyses where you can afford the time.
max_iter default=300
What it is: Maximum number of iterations (assign + update cycles) per single run.
Impact: Most practical datasets converge well before 300 iterations. If you see warnings about not converging, either increase max_iter or check if your data needs scaling.
When to increase: Large datasets with many clusters or subtle cluster boundaries may need 500+ iterations.
tol default=1e-4
What it is: Convergence tolerance — if centroid movement between iterations is less than tol, stop early.
Impact: Smaller tol means run until almost exactly converged (slower but more precise). Larger tol stops earlier (faster, slightly less accurate centroids).
Formula: Convergence check: max displacement of any centroid < tol × inertia.
random_state default=None
What it is: Seed for the random number generator used in centroid initialisation.
Always set this to any integer (e.g., 42) so your results are reproducible. Without it, every run produces different clusters.
algorithm default='lloyd'
What it is: The algorithm variant to use.
Options:
  • 'lloyd' — Classic K-Means. Works well for most cases.
  • 'elkan' — Uses triangle inequality to skip some distance calculations. Faster on dense, well-separated clusters. Memory-intensive.
Practical advice: Start with 'lloyd'. Try 'elkan' if you have well-separated clusters and want speed.

Key Attributes After Fitting

Attribute Shape Meaning
cluster_centers_ [n_clusters, n_features] Coordinates of each centroid in feature space
labels_ [n_samples] Cluster assignment (0, 1, 2...) for each training point
inertia_ scalar Final WCSS value — sum of squared distances of each point to its centroid
n_iter_ int Number of iterations the best run took to converge

Complete K-Means Workflow — Annotated


from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.metrics import silhouette_score
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# ── Step 1: Scale features (critical for distance-based algorithms) ───────────
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)   # mean=0, std=1 for every feature

# ── Step 2: Find optimal K using elbow method ─────────────────────────────────
inertias = []           # will store WCSS for each K
silhouette_scores = []  # optional: another metric for cluster quality

K_range = range(2, 12)   # test K=2 through K=11
for k in K_range:
    km = KMeans(
        n_clusters=k,        # try each K value
        init='k-means++',    # smart initialisation — reduces bad local optima
        n_init=10,           # run 10 times, keep the best result
        max_iter=300,        # max assign/update cycles per run
        random_state=42,     # reproducibility
    )
    km.fit(X_scaled)                 # run K-Means
    inertias.append(km.inertia_)     # store WCSS
    # Silhouette: measures how well-separated clusters are
    # Range: -1 (bad) to 1 (perfect); higher = better
    score = silhouette_score(X_scaled, km.labels_)
    silhouette_scores.append(score)

# Plot elbow
plt.figure(figsize=(12,4))
plt.subplot(1,2,1)
plt.plot(K_range, inertias, 'bo-')
plt.xlabel('K'); plt.ylabel('Inertia (WCSS)')
plt.title('Elbow Method')

plt.subplot(1,2,2)
plt.plot(K_range, silhouette_scores, 'ro-')
plt.xlabel('K'); plt.ylabel('Silhouette Score')
plt.title('Silhouette Scores — Pick K with highest score')
plt.show()

# ── Step 3: Train final model with chosen K ────────────────────────────────────
best_k = 3   # chosen from elbow/silhouette analysis

kmeans = KMeans(
    n_clusters=best_k,    # K=3 clusters
    init='k-means++',     # smart start
    n_init=10,            # repeat 10 times for reliability
    max_iter=300,         # converge within 300 iterations
    tol=1e-4,             # stop if centroid moves less than this
    algorithm='lloyd',    # classic K-Means (or 'elkan' for dense data)
    random_state=42,
)
kmeans.fit(X_scaled)   # run K-Means clustering

# ── Step 4: Examine results ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
print(f"Cluster assignments (first 10): {kmeans.labels_[:10]}")
# e.g. [0, 2, 1, 0, 0, 2, 1, 1, 2, 0] — each point belongs to cluster 0, 1, or 2

print(f"Centroid positions:
{kmeans.cluster_centers_}")
# Shape: [3, n_features] — coordinates of each cluster center in feature space

print(f"Inertia: {kmeans.inertia_:.2f}")
# Lower is better, but must compare across K values

print(f"Converged in {kmeans.n_iter_} iterations")

# ── Step 5: Assign new data points to clusters ────────────────────────────────
new_data = [[35, 75000]]   # new customer: age=35, salary=75000
new_data_scaled = scaler.transform(new_data)    # MUST scale with same scaler
cluster_id = kmeans.predict(new_data_scaled)    # which cluster does this point belong to?
print(f"New customer belongs to cluster: {cluster_id[0]}")

# Distance to each centroid
distances = kmeans.transform(new_data_scaled)   # shape: [1, n_clusters]
print(f"Distances to each centroid: {distances[0].round(3)}")
# Closest centroid = assigned cluster

# ── Step 6: Interpret clusters ────────────────────────────────────────────────
import pandas as pd
df_original = pd.DataFrame(X, columns=['age', 'salary', 'purchase_freq'])
df_original['cluster'] = kmeans.labels_

# Describe each cluster in original (unscaled) terms
print(df_original.groupby('cluster').mean())
# This tells you: "Cluster 0 has average age 25, salary 30k, purchase_freq 5/mo → young budget shoppers"

When to Use K-Means

Use K-Means When:

  • You have no labels and want to discover natural groupings in data
  • Clusters are roughly spherical and similarly sized
  • You need a fast, scalable algorithm for large datasets (K-Means is O(n))
  • Customer segmentation, document clustering, image compression
  • As a preprocessing step before classification (use cluster IDs as features)

Avoid K-Means When:

  • Clusters have non-spherical shapes (use DBSCAN or Gaussian Mixture Models)
  • You have very different cluster sizes or densities
  • Data has many outliers (use DBSCAN which can mark outliers as noise)
  • You truly don't know K and the elbow plot is ambiguous (try silhouette score or DBSCAN)

Alternatives for Different Cluster Shapes

Algorithm Best For Handles Outliers
K-Means Spherical, equal-size clusters No
DBSCAN Arbitrary shapes, noisy data Yes (marks as noise)
GMM (Gaussian Mixture) Soft cluster assignments, elliptical shapes Partial
Hierarchical When you want a tree of clusters at multiple scales Partial
📉
Principal Component Analysis (PCA)
Dimensionality Reduction — Compress Features While Preserving Maximum Information

What is PCA? (Plain English First)

Imagine your dataset has 100 features (columns). Most of them contain overlapping, redundant information. PCA's job is to compress those 100 features into, say, 5 "super-features" that capture most of the important patterns — so you lose very little information but gain massive computational savings.

Think of it like taking a 3D sculpture and finding the best angle to photograph it that captures the most detail in 2D. Some angles show more of the structure than others — PCA finds those angles mathematically.

The Core Idea: Variance = Information

PCA operates on a key insight: features that vary a lot carry more information than features that barely change. A feature where every customer has nearly the same value is useless for distinguishing customers.

PCA finds directions in the feature space where the data spreads out (varies) the most. These directions are called Principal Components (PCs).

  • PC1 — The single direction that captures the most variance in the data
  • PC2 — The direction perpendicular to PC1 that captures the second-most variance
  • PC3 — Perpendicular to both PC1 and PC2, capturing the third-most variance
  • And so on... Each PC is orthogonal (perpendicular) to all others

PCA Step by Step

  1. Standardise — Centre the data (subtract mean) so all features are on equal footing.
  2. Covariance Matrix — Calculate how much each feature varies with every other feature. This reveals which features are correlated.
  3. Eigenvectors & Eigenvalues — Find the "principal axes" (eigenvectors) and how much variance each axis explains (eigenvalues). Eigenvectors define the direction; eigenvalues define the magnitude.
  4. Sort — Sort eigenvectors by their eigenvalues, largest first. PC1 = eigenvector with largest eigenvalue.
  5. Project — Multiply the original data by the top K eigenvectors to get K-dimensional representation.

Explained Variance Ratio — How Much Information Is Preserved?

Each PC explains a fraction of the total variance. explained_variance_ratio_[0] might be 0.40, meaning PC1 alone captures 40% of all information in the dataset.

Cumulative explained variance is what you use to choose how many PCs to keep:

  • If PC1+PC2+PC3 together explain 95% of variance → keep 3 PCs, discard the rest
  • You're retaining 95% of the information using only 3 numbers instead of 100 features

Important: PCA Components Are Not Interpretable

Original features have meaning: "age", "salary", "purchase_frequency". But principal components are linear combinations of all features — they have no direct real-world meaning.

PC1 might be something like: 0.45×age + 0.72×salary − 0.31×purchase_freq + ... This cannot be labelled as "age" or any other single thing.

Trade-off: You gain computational efficiency and noise reduction but lose the ability to say "this feature drove the prediction."

When Scaling Is Mandatory Before PCA

PCA finds directions of maximum variance. If "salary" has a range of 0–200,000 and "age" has a range of 0–100, salary will completely dominate the first PC — not because it's more important, but just because it has bigger numbers.

Always apply StandardScaler before PCA unless all features are already on the same scale.

PCA: Projecting 2D Data onto the First Principal Component

Left: original 2D data. The orange arrow (PC1) points in the direction of maximum variance — the "widest spread" of data. The purple arrow (PC2) is perpendicular — it captures the remaining variance. Right: data projected onto PC1. 80% of the original information is preserved in this 1D representation.

Scree Plot — How Many Components to Keep

Each bar shows how much variance one principal component explains. The orange dashed line shows cumulative variance. Choose the number of components where cumulative variance crosses your threshold (usually 95%). Here: keep PC1+PC2+PC3+PC4 to get 90%.

External Reference: PCA Visualised

PCA — Every Parameter Explained

n_components default=None
What it is: How many principal components to keep.
Options (3 ways to set this):
  • n_components=5 — Keep exactly 5 PCs regardless of variance explained.
  • n_components=0.95 — Keep however many PCs are needed to explain 95% of variance. (Float between 0 and 1 triggers this mode.)
  • n_components=None — Keep all components = no reduction. Still useful for getting explained_variance_ratio_ to decide how many to use.
  • n_components='mle' — Use Maximum Likelihood Estimation to automatically select the optimal number of components.
Best practice: First run PCA with None or a large number, examine explained_variance_ratio_, then re-run with n_components=0.95 or a specific int.
svd_solver default='auto'
What it is: The mathematical algorithm used to compute principal components.
Options:
  • 'auto' — sklearn picks the best solver based on data shape.
  • 'full' — Full Singular Value Decomposition. Most accurate. Slow for very large datasets. Use when n_components is large.
  • 'arpack' — ARPACK algorithm. Efficient when n_components is small relative to n_features. Good for sparse data.
  • 'randomized' — Randomised SVD. Very fast for large n and small n_components. Slight accuracy trade-off but usually negligible.
  • 'covariance_eigh' — For small datasets. Uses eigendecomposition of covariance matrix directly.
Rule: Use 'randomized' for speed when you have many features (>100) and need few components.
whiten default=False
What it is: Whether to divide each component by its standard deviation so all components have unit variance.
Effect: Without whitening, PC1 has much larger variance than PC2, PC3, etc. With whitening, all components are rescaled to have equal variance.
When to use True: When feeding PCA output into algorithms sensitive to feature scale (SVMs, neural networks, KNN). Whitening ensures downstream algorithms don't over-weight PC1.
When to keep False: When you want to preserve the original variance structure (e.g., for visualisation or when downstream algorithm is scale-invariant like Random Forest).
random_state default=None
What it is: Random seed for 'randomized' and 'arpack' solvers.
Set to any integer for reproducibility. No effect when svd_solver='full'.

Key Attributes After Fitting

Attribute Shape Meaning
explained_variance_ratio_ [n_components] Fraction of variance explained by each PC. Sum should be ≤1.0. Most important attribute.
components_ [n_components, n_features] The eigenvectors (principal axes). Each row is a PC direction in the original feature space.
explained_variance_ [n_components] Eigenvalues — the actual variance amount explained by each PC (not fraction)
singular_values_ [n_components] The singular values corresponding to each selected component
mean_ [n_features] Per-feature mean used to centre the data. Stored so transform() on new data uses the same mean.

Complete PCA Workflow — Annotated


from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# ── Step 1: Always scale before PCA ───────────────────────────────────────────
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)   # zero mean, unit variance per feature

# ── Step 2: Run PCA with all components to analyse variance ───────────────────
pca_full = PCA(
    n_components=None,    # keep all components — for analysis only
    svd_solver='auto',    # sklearn picks best solver for data shape
    random_state=42,
)
pca_full.fit(X_scaled)

# Check how much variance each PC explains
print("Explained variance ratio per PC:")
print(pca_full.explained_variance_ratio_.round(3))
# e.g. [0.40, 0.25, 0.15, 0.10, 0.07, ...]

# Cumulative variance — find how many PCs needed for 95%
cumulative = np.cumsum(pca_full.explained_variance_ratio_)
print(f"
Cumulative variance:")
for i, cv in enumerate(cumulative):
    print(f"  {i+1} components: {cv:.3f} ({cv*100:.1f}%)")
    if cv >= 0.95:
        print(f"  → Keep {i+1} components for 95% variance")
        break

# Scree plot
plt.bar(range(1, len(pca_full.explained_variance_ratio_)+1),
        pca_full.explained_variance_ratio_)
plt.xlabel('Principal Component')
plt.ylabel('Explained Variance Ratio')
plt.title('Scree Plot')
plt.show()

# ── Step 3: Apply PCA with chosen number of components ────────────────────────
pca = PCA(
    n_components=0.95,     # keep however many PCs explain 95% of variance
    # OR: n_components=5   — explicitly keep 5 PCs
    svd_solver='randomized',  # fast for large datasets with few components
    whiten=False,          # True: rescale PCs to unit variance (useful for SVM/KNN downstream)
    random_state=42,
)
X_reduced = pca.fit_transform(X_scaled)
# X_scaled: shape [n_samples, n_features] (e.g., 1000 × 100)
# X_reduced: shape [n_samples, n_components] (e.g., 1000 × 8)
# We just went from 100 features to 8!

print(f"Original features: {X_scaled.shape[1]}")
print(f"Reduced features: {X_reduced.shape[1]}")
print(f"Variance preserved: {pca.explained_variance_ratio_.sum():.3f}")

# ── Step 4: Examine what each PC is made of (loadings) ────────────────────────
# pca.components_[i] is PC i+1 — a vector of length n_features
# Large absolute values = that original feature contributes a lot to this PC
pc1_loadings = pca.components_[0]   # first principal component
feature_names = ['age', 'salary', 'spend', 'visits', 'items']   # example
for name, loading in zip(feature_names, pc1_loadings):
    print(f"  {name}: {loading:.3f}")
# e.g. salary: 0.72, spend: 0.65, age: 0.21 → PC1 is mostly about financial capacity

# ── Step 5: Use in a ML pipeline (best practice) ──────────────────────────────
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression

pipe = Pipeline([
    ('scaler', StandardScaler()),     # 1. scale features
    ('pca',    PCA(n_components=0.95, random_state=42)),  # 2. reduce dimensions
    ('model',  LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000)),         # 3. classify
])
pipe.fit(X_train, y_train)
y_pred = pipe.predict(X_test)

# ── Step 6: Inverse transform — reconstruct approximate original data ──────────
X_reconstructed = pca.inverse_transform(X_reduced)
# This is not exactly X_scaled — some information was lost in reduction
# Useful for: image compression, anomaly detection (high reconstruction error = anomaly)

reconstruction_error = np.mean((X_scaled - X_reconstructed) ** 2)
print(f"Mean Squared Reconstruction Error: {reconstruction_error:.4f}")
# 0.0 = perfect reconstruction. Higher = more information lost.

When to Use PCA

Use PCA When:

  • You have many features (>50) and suspect many are correlated
  • Model is slow to train due to high dimensionality
  • You want to visualise high-dimensional data in 2D or 3D (use n_components=2 or 3)
  • Noise reduction — lower PCs often capture noise; discarding them can improve model performance
  • Upstream of distance-based algorithms (KNN, SVM with RBF kernel) to reduce the curse of dimensionality
  • Image data — faces, scans. PCA on pixel values is a classic application.

Avoid PCA When:

  • You need feature interpretability — PCA components are not explainable
  • You have categorical features — PCA assumes continuous numeric data
  • Your features are already few and uncorrelated — PCA adds complexity without benefit
  • You're using tree-based models (Random Forest, XGBoost) — they handle high dimensionality natively and are not distance-based

PCA vs Other Dimensionality Reduction Techniques

Method Type Best For Captures
PCA Linear Preprocessing, noise reduction Global linear structure
t-SNE Non-linear 2D/3D visualisation Local cluster structure
UMAP Non-linear Fast visualisation + preprocessing Both local and global
LDA Linear (supervised) Before classification Class-discriminating directions

PCA for Anomaly Detection

An elegant use of PCA: train PCA on normal data. For new data points, project to PCA space and reconstruct. Normal points reconstruct accurately. Anomalies reconstruct poorly (high reconstruction error) because they don't fit the patterns learned from normal data.

recon_error = np.mean((X_scaled - pca.inverse_transform(pca.transform(X_scaled)))**2, axis=1)
anomalies = X[recon_error > threshold]
11
Neural Networks (ANN)
Every layer, every parameter, every concept — explained from zero for non-technical learners

What Is a Neural Network? (Plain English, Zero Jargon)

Imagine you're teaching a child to recognise cats. You show them 1,000 pictures — some cats, some dogs. After enough examples they start to "get it." They've learned patterns: pointy ears, whiskers, certain face shapes. A neural network does the exact same thing — but with numbers instead of eyes, and math instead of a brain.

At its core, a neural network is just a big mathematical function that takes in a bunch of numbers (your data — age, price, pixel values, whatever) and outputs a number (a prediction). It learns this function automatically by looking at thousands of examples.

The "neural" part comes from a loose inspiration from biology — real neurons in the brain receive signals, process them, and pass the result on. Artificial neurons do the same thing with math. The "network" part is because thousands of these artificial neurons are connected together in layers.

One-line summary: A neural network is a layered system of simple math operations that, when stacked and trained on data, can learn to recognise incredibly complex patterns — faces, speech, fraud, disease, anything that has patterns.

Each circle = one neuron. Each line = one weight (connection strength). Data flows left to right.

The Three Layer Types

LayerWhat it doesRule of thumb
Input Layer Simply receives your features and passes them forward. Does NO computation. One node per feature. 20 features → input_shape=(20,). Never add activation here.
Hidden Layers Where ALL the learning happens. Each layer learns progressively more abstract patterns. Layer 1 might learn edges; layer 5 might learn "face shape". Start with 1-3 hidden layers. Deeper = more powerful but needs more data.
Output Layer The final prediction. Its structure depends on your problem. Binary classification → 1 node + sigmoid. Multi-class (5 classes) → 5 nodes + softmax. Regression → 1 node, no activation.

Types of Neural Networks (Overview)

TypeBest ForKey Layers
ANN / MLP (what this section covers)Tabular data, structured featuresDense, Dropout, BatchNorm
CNN (Convolutional)Images, spatial dataConv2D, MaxPooling, Flatten
RNN / LSTMSequences: time series, textLSTM, GRU, Embedding
TransformerText, language, attentionMultiHeadAttention, LayerNorm
AutoencoderAnomaly detection, compressionEncoder + Decoder Dense blocks

This guide section covers ANN/MLP — the foundation that every other type builds on. Master this first.

🏭 Real-world analogy — Factory Quality Inspector

Imagine a factory that inspects products. Worker 1 (layer 1) checks colour. Worker 2 (layer 2) checks shape using Worker 1's output. Worker 3 (layer 3) checks texture. Final inspector (output layer) makes the pass/fail decision using all previous workers' findings. Each worker specialises in one aspect. Together, they catch defects no single worker could.

Step 1 — The Building Block: A Single Neuron

Every layer is made of individual neurons. Here's exactly what ONE neuron does:

  1. Receives several input numbers (e.g., x₁=0.8, x₂=0.3, x₃=1.2)
  2. Multiplies each input by a weight (e.g., w₁=0.5, w₂=−0.3, w₃=0.9)
  3. Adds all those products together: (0.8×0.5) + (0.3×−0.3) + (1.2×0.9) = 0.4 − 0.09 + 1.08 = 1.39
  4. Adds a bias (a learnable offset): 1.39 + 0.1 = 1.49
  5. Passes the result through an activation function (e.g., ReLU): max(0, 1.49) = 1.49
  6. Outputs 1.49 to every neuron in the next layer

Formula: output = activation( w₁x₁ + w₂x₂ + ... + wₙxₙ + b )
This is it. This is the entire computation of one neuron.

Dense Layer — The Most Common Layer (Fully Connected)

"Dense" means every input connects to every output neuron. A Dense(64) layer has 64 neurons, each connected to every input.

Dense(units, activation, use_bias, kernel_initializer, kernel_regularizer)

ParameterWhat it meansDefaultImpact if changed
units Number of neurons in this layer. More neurons = more learning capacity. required 64→128: doubles parameters, slower but can learn more complex patterns. Too many: overfits, wastes compute. Too few: underfits, misses patterns.
activation The activation function applied to each neuron's output. Without this, stacking Dense layers is mathematically identical to one layer. None (linear) 'relu': best default for hidden layers. 'sigmoid': use only on binary output. 'softmax': use only on multi-class output. None / 'linear': use on regression output.
use_bias Whether to add a learnable bias term (the "+b" in the formula). Almost always True. True Setting False removes the bias. Only do this if you have a specific mathematical reason. Default True is almost always correct.
kernel_initializer How to set the starting values of the weights BEFORE training. Wrong initialisation = very slow training or total failure. 'glorot_uniform' 'glorot_uniform': best for sigmoid/tanh. 'he_uniform': better for ReLU. 'zeros': NEVER use — all neurons learn the same thing. 'random_normal': risky with deep networks.
kernel_regularizer Adds a penalty to the loss for large weights. Forces the model to keep weights small, which reduces overfitting. None l2(0.01): mild regularisation, good starting point. l2(0.1): strong — may underfit. l1(0.01): promotes sparse weights (some become exactly zero). Use when model overfits.
bias_regularizer Same as kernel_regularizer but applies to the bias values. None Rarely needed. Only add if you suspect bias overfitting, which is uncommon.

Simple rule for units: Start with powers of 2 (32, 64, 128, 256). Decrease toward the output. Example: Dense(128) → Dense(64) → Dense(32) → Dense(1)

Dropout Layer — Preventing Memorisation (Overfitting)

The problem it solves: Without Dropout, a neural network can memorise the training data — like a student who memorises every past exam question but can't handle new questions. This is called overfitting. The model performs great on training data but terrible on new data.

What Dropout does: During each training step, it randomly picks some neurons and turns them off (sets their output to zero). This forces the network to NOT rely on any single path. Every neuron must learn something useful on its own, because its neighbours might be gone next step.

At test time, ALL neurons are active. Dropout is a training-only trick.

Training: Dropout(0.4) — 2 of 5 dropped

Testing: All neurons active

Dropout(rate, noise_shape, seed)

ParameterWhat it meansDefaultImpact if changed
rate Fraction of neurons to randomly DROP each training step. rate=0.3 means 30% of neurons are zeroed out each step. required 0.1–0.2: mild regularisation, barely noticeable. 0.2–0.5: standard range, use this. 0.5: classic "50% dropout", strong regularisation. 0.6+: too aggressive — model learns too little. 0.0: no dropout at all.
noise_shape Shape of the random dropout mask. Usually not set. Only needed for advanced use cases like dropping entire time steps in sequences. None Leave as None unless you know what you're doing.
seed Random seed for reproducibility. Set to a fixed integer (e.g., seed=42) if you want the same dropout pattern across runs. None Setting seed=42 makes runs reproducible. Leave as None in production.

Where to put Dropout: After Dense layers in hidden layers. NOT after the output layer. Typical pattern: Dense → Dropout → Dense → Dropout → Output.

Rate guide: Small dataset or overfitting badly → use 0.4–0.5. Large dataset → use 0.2–0.3. No overfitting → don't add Dropout at all.

BatchNormalization — Stable and Fast Training

The problem it solves: As data passes through layers, the numbers can become very large or very small. This makes training unstable — gradients explode or vanish. BatchNorm "normalises" each layer's output: zero mean, unit variance. This makes every layer receive consistently scaled inputs.

Think of it like: Standardising exam scores. Instead of scores ranging from 0–10,000, you transform them to mean=0, std=1. Every subsequent calculation becomes more stable.

Bonus effect: Acts as a mild regulariser. Often allows you to use a higher learning rate and train faster.

BatchNormalization(axis, momentum, epsilon, center, scale)

ParameterWhat it meansDefaultImpact if changed
axis Which axis to normalise over. For Dense layers: -1 (last axis, which is the features axis). For Conv layers: 1 (channels axis). -1 Rarely need to change for standard networks. Set to 1 for Conv2D layers.
momentum Controls how the running mean and variance are updated during training. Higher value = updates use more of the old estimate. 0.99 0.99: stable, slow updates. 0.9: faster adaptation. Lower for small batches. Rarely needs changing.
epsilon Small number added to variance to prevent division by zero. 0.001 Increase slightly (e.g., 0.01) if you see NaN values during training. Otherwise leave alone.
center If True, learns a beta (shift) parameter to shift the normalised output. True Almost always True. Setting False removes the learnable shift.
scale If True, learns a gamma (scale) parameter to scale the normalised output. True Almost always True. Setting False removes the learnable scale.

Where to put BatchNorm: After Dense, BEFORE or AFTER activation. Common pattern: Dense → BatchNorm → Activation (ReLU) → Dropout. In practice, BN before activation is most common.

Activation Functions — The Entire Picture

Without activation functions, stacking 10 Dense layers is mathematically identical to 1 Dense layer. You can prove this with algebra. Activation functions break this linearity and allow the network to learn curves, complex boundaries, and non-linear patterns.

ReLU — max(0, x)

Use in: hidden layers
Fast, simple, prevents vanishing gradient

Sigmoid — 1/(1+e⁻ˣ)

Use in: binary output layer
Squashes to 0–1 (probability)

Tanh

Use in: hidden layers (sometimes)
Outputs −1 to +1, zero-centered

Softmax (multi-class)

Use in: multi-class output layer
Converts scores to probabilities summing to 1

ActivationFormulaOutput rangeUse inProblem
ReLU max(0, x) 0 to ∞ Hidden layers — almost always the default Dying ReLU: neurons can get stuck at 0 if too many inputs are negative. Fix: use LeakyReLU or He initialiser.
LeakyReLU x if x>0, else 0.01x −∞ to ∞ (small negatives) Hidden layers when you suspect dying ReLU Small negatives can occasionally cause instability.
Sigmoid 1 / (1 + e⁻ˣ) 0 to 1 Output layer for binary classification Vanishing gradient: saturates at 0 and 1, gradient ≈ 0. Never use in hidden layers.
Softmax eˣⁱ / Σeˣʲ 0 to 1 (sums to 1) Output layer for multi-class classification Same vanishing gradient issue as sigmoid. Only for output.
Tanh (eˣ−e⁻ˣ) / (eˣ+e⁻ˣ) −1 to 1 Hidden layers (zero-centered, better than sigmoid for hidden) Still has vanishing gradient, just slower than sigmoid. ReLU usually better.
Linear / None f(x) = x −∞ to ∞ Regression output layer (predicting a continuous number) No non-linearity, so NEVER use in hidden layers.

Loss Functions — Measuring How Wrong the Model Is

The loss function calculates a single number representing "how wrong" the current prediction is. Training tries to minimise this number.

Loss FunctionWhen to useWhat it measuresExample output range
binary_crossentropy Binary classification (output: sigmoid, 1 class) How far the predicted probability is from the true label (0 or 1) 0.0 (perfect) to ∞ (terrible)
categorical_crossentropy Multi-class classification with one-hot encoded labels (e.g., [0,1,0,0]) How far the predicted class probabilities are from the true class 0.0 to ∞
sparse_categorical_crossentropy Same as categorical_crossentropy but labels are integers (e.g., 2) not one-hot Same as above — just different label format 0.0 to ∞
mse (Mean Squared Error) Regression (predicting a number) Average squared difference between predicted and actual values. Penalises large errors heavily. 0.0 to ∞
mae (Mean Absolute Error) Regression — when you have outliers and don't want to penalise them too hard Average absolute difference. More robust to outliers than MSE. 0.0 to ∞

Optimizers — How Weights Are Updated

After backpropagation computes the gradient (direction of steepest error), the optimizer decides HOW MUCH and in WHAT WAY to adjust each weight.

Adam — Adaptive Moment Estimation (default choice)

ParameterWhat it meansDefaultWhen to change
learning_rateHow big each weight update step is. THE most important hyperparameter.0.001Too high (e.g. 0.1): training oscillates, never converges. Too low (e.g. 1e-6): training takes forever. Start at 0.001, halve if unstable, double if too slow.
beta_1Decay rate for the 1st moment (running average of gradients). Controls momentum.0.9Almost never change. 0.9 is the universal standard.
beta_2Decay rate for the 2nd moment (running average of squared gradients). Controls adaptive scaling.0.999Almost never change. Lower (e.g. 0.99) for noisy gradients.
epsilonSmall number to prevent division by zero in the update formula.1e-7Increase to 1e-4 if you see NaN losses.
amsgradUses the maximum of past squared gradients instead of the running average. More stable convergence.FalseSet True if Adam seems to diverge late in training.

SGD — Stochastic Gradient Descent

ParameterWhat it meansDefaultWhen to change
learning_rateStep size per update. Needs to be much lower than Adam (0.01 is typical).0.01Start at 0.01. Use learning rate schedulers to decay over time.
momentumFraction of the previous update direction to keep. Helps escape local minima and smooth oscillations.0.0Use 0.9 almost always when using SGD. Without momentum, SGD is very slow.
nesterovLookahead variant of momentum — looks ahead before computing gradient. Usually slightly better than standard momentum.FalseSet True whenever momentum > 0.

Which optimizer to use? Default: Adam(learning_rate=0.001). If Adam doesn't converge: try SGD(lr=0.01, momentum=0.9). Adam is the safe default for 90% of problems.

The Training Loop: What Actually Happens

Training a neural network is a cycle. Here's what happens every time you call model.fit():

  1. Forward Pass: Input data flows through every layer. Each neuron computes its output using current weights. A prediction comes out of the output layer.
  2. Compute Loss: The loss function compares the prediction to the true label. Produces a single number (e.g., loss = 0.43).
  3. Backpropagation: Calculates how much each weight CONTRIBUTED to the error, using the chain rule of calculus. This gives each weight a "gradient" (how to change it to reduce loss).
  4. Weight Update: The optimizer adjusts every weight in the direction that reduces loss: new_weight = old_weight − (learning_rate × gradient)
  5. Repeat: Steps 1–4 happen for every batch. After all batches = 1 epoch done. This continues for all epochs.

Key Training Parameters Explained

ParameterWhat it meansToo low effectToo high effectSweet spot
epochs How many times the model sees the entire training dataset Underfits — hasn't learned enough Overfits — memorises training data Use EarlyStopping instead of guessing a fixed number
batch_size How many samples to process before updating weights 1 (online): very noisy updates, slow. But can escape local minima. Full dataset: smooth but poor generalisation, needs lots of RAM 32–256. Start with 32. Try 64 or 128 if training is slow.
validation_split Fraction of training data to hold out for validation (measuring generalisation) 0.0: no way to detect overfitting during training 0.4+: too little data for training 0.1–0.2 (10–20% of training data)
learning_rate Step size for weight updates — the single most impactful hyperparameter 1e-6: training is glacially slow 0.1+: loss oscillates or explodes to NaN 0.001 for Adam. 0.01 for SGD with momentum.

When Neural Networks Shine

Neural networks are overkill for simple problems and essential for complex ones. The deciding factors:

  • Large data: NNs need thousands of examples to learn well. Less than ~1000 rows → try Random Forest or XGBoost first.
  • Non-linear patterns: If the relationship between input and output is complex, curvy, or involves interactions, NNs excel.
  • Unstructured data: Images, text, audio — NNs (specifically CNNs, RNNs, Transformers) are the only practical choice.
  • Black box is acceptable: NNs are hard to interpret. If you need to explain every prediction (banking, medical), prefer decision trees.

Industry Applications (Real Examples)

IndustryUse CaseInput FeaturesOutput
E-CommerceCustomer Lifetime Value predictionPurchase history, browsing behaviour, demographicsPredicted spend over 12 months (regression)
BankingCredit risk / fraud detectionTransaction amount, time, location, merchant category, account historyFraud probability 0–1 (binary classification)
HealthcareDisease risk scoringLab results, vitals, age, medical historyRisk score for disease (regression or classification)
RetailDemand forecastingHistorical sales, promotions, holidays, weather, pricePredicted units sold next week (regression)
HR / RecruitmentAttrition predictionTenure, salary, job level, engagement scores, manager ratingProbability employee will leave (binary classification)
MarketingCampaign response predictionDemographics, purchase history, email engagement, RFM scoresProbability of responding to campaign (binary)
ManufacturingPredictive maintenanceSensor readings (vibration, temperature, pressure) over timeProbability of machine failure in next 7 days

When NOT to Use Neural Networks

SituationBetter AlternativeWhy
Less than ~500 rows of dataLogistic Regression, Random ForestNNs overfit badly on tiny datasets. Simpler models generalise better.
Need to explain every predictionDecision Tree, Logistic RegressionNNs are black boxes. Can't say "why" it predicted this.
Very quick prototype / baselineXGBoost or Random ForestNNs take more time to tune. Tree models are faster to get running well.
Simple linear relationshipLinear/Logistic RegressionIf data is linearly separable, a regression is faster and just as accurate.
Very limited compute / deployment constraintsLightweight modelsNNs can be large and slow at inference. Simpler models may be necessary.

Complete Annotated Code — ANN for Binary Classification

Every single line below is explained. Read it like a recipe.

import numpy as np
from tensorflow.keras.models     import Sequential
from tensorflow.keras.layers     import Dense, Dropout, BatchNormalization
from tensorflow.keras.callbacks  import EarlyStopping, ReduceLROnPlateau
from tensorflow.keras.optimizers import Adam
from tensorflow.keras.regularizers import l2
from sklearn.preprocessing       import StandardScaler
from sklearn.model_selection      import train_test_split
from sklearn.metrics              import classification_report

# ── 1. DATA PREP ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
np.random.seed(42)             # makes results reproducible — same random numbers every run
n = 5000                       # 5000 training examples
X = np.random.randn(n, 20)    # 20 input features, normally distributed
# Create target: 1 if a combination of features exceeds threshold
y = (X[:, 0] + X[:, 1]**2 + np.sin(X[:, 2]) > 1).astype(float)

# Split: 80% for training, 20% for final testing (model never sees test during training)
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y,
    test_size=0.2,       # 20% held out as test set
    random_state=42      # fixed seed so split is same every run
)

# Standardise: subtract mean, divide by std → all features have mean=0, std=1
# WHY: if one feature is 0-1 and another is 0-100000, the big one dominates
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_train = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)  # LEARN mean/std from training data AND transform
X_test  = scaler.transform(X_test)       # ONLY transform — use same mean/std from training
                                          # NEVER fit on test data — that's data leakage!

# ── 2. BUILD THE MODEL ──────────────────────────────────────────────
model = Sequential([

    # ── LAYER 1: First hidden layer ──────────────────────────────────
    Dense(
        units=128,                          # 128 neurons
        activation='relu',                  # ReLU activation — standard for hidden layers
        input_shape=(20,),                  # tells Keras: 20 input features
        kernel_initializer='he_uniform',    # He init: better than glorot for ReLU
        kernel_regularizer=l2(0.001)        # L2 penalty: mild regularisation to prevent overfit
    ),
    BatchNormalization(),  # normalise layer output → stable training, acts as regulariser
    Dropout(0.3),         # randomly drop 30% of neurons each training step

    # ── LAYER 2: Second hidden layer (fewer neurons — funnel shape) ──
    Dense(
        units=64,                           # 64 neurons (decreasing from 128 is common)
        activation='relu',
        kernel_initializer='he_uniform',
        kernel_regularizer=l2(0.001)
    ),
    BatchNormalization(),
    Dropout(0.2),         # less dropout here — smaller layer, less risk of overfit

    # ── LAYER 3: Third hidden layer ──────────────────────────────────
    Dense(32, activation='relu', kernel_initializer='he_uniform'),

    # ── OUTPUT LAYER ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
    Dense(
        units=1,              # 1 neuron: outputs a single value
        activation='sigmoid'  # sigmoid: squashes output to 0–1 → interpret as probability
                              # For multi-class: units=num_classes, activation='softmax'
                              # For regression: units=1, no activation (or 'linear')
    )
])

# ── 3. COMPILE ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
model.compile(
    optimizer=Adam(learning_rate=0.001), # Adam: adaptive optimizer, best default
                                           # learning_rate=0.001 is the standard starting point
    loss='binary_crossentropy',            # loss for binary classification
                                           # multi-class: 'categorical_crossentropy'
                                           # regression:  'mse' or 'mae'
    metrics=['accuracy']                   # what to DISPLAY during training (not optimised directly)
                                           # can add: ['accuracy', AUC(), Precision()]
)
model.summary()   # prints layer shapes and parameter counts — always run this first

# ── 4. CALLBACKS ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
early_stop = EarlyStopping(
    monitor='val_loss',        # watch validation loss (NOT training loss)
    patience=10,               # stop if no improvement for 10 consecutive epochs
    restore_best_weights=True, # after stopping, reload the best weights seen
    min_delta=0.0001           # minimum change to qualify as improvement
)

reduce_lr = ReduceLROnPlateau(
    monitor='val_loss',   # watch validation loss
    factor=0.5,           # multiply learning rate by 0.5 when plateau detected
    patience=5,           # wait 5 epochs before reducing
    min_lr=1e-6           # never go below this learning rate
)
# ModelCheckpoint would go here to save best model to disk:
# from tensorflow.keras.callbacks import ModelCheckpoint
# checkpoint = ModelCheckpoint('best_model.h5', save_best_only=True, monitor='val_loss')

# ── 5. TRAIN ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
history = model.fit(
    X_train, y_train,          # training features and labels
    validation_split=0.2,      # use 20% of X_train as validation set (auto-split)
    epochs=200,                # max epochs — EarlyStopping will stop before this
    batch_size=32,             # process 32 samples → update weights → repeat
                               # larger batch (128, 256) = faster but may generalise worse
    callbacks=[early_stop, reduce_lr],  # apply both callbacks
    verbose=1                  # 0=silent, 1=progress bar, 2=one line per epoch
)

# ── 6. EVALUATE ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
test_loss, test_acc = model.evaluate(X_test, y_test, verbose=0)
print(f"Test Loss: {test_loss:.4f}  |  Test Accuracy: {test_acc:.4f}")
# evaluate() returns the same metrics you specified in compile()

# ── 7. PREDICT ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
y_pred_prob = model.predict(X_test).flatten()   # outputs probabilities (0.0–1.0)
y_pred_class = (y_pred_prob > 0.5).astype(int)  # convert probabilities to class labels
                                                  # threshold=0.5 is default; adjust for imbalanced data
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred_class))

# ── 8. INSPECT TRAINING HISTORY ─────────────────────────────────────
# history.history is a dict: {'loss': [...], 'val_loss': [...], 'accuracy': [...], ...}
# Plot training vs validation loss to diagnose overfitting:
# import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
# plt.plot(history.history['loss'],     label='train loss')
# plt.plot(history.history['val_loss'], label='val loss')
# plt.legend(); plt.show()
# If val_loss goes UP while train loss goes DOWN → overfitting → add Dropout or reduce model size

Diagnosing Your Model from Training Curves

What you seeDiagnosisFix
val_loss decreasing, train_loss decreasing (both going down together)Healthy trainingKeep going until EarlyStopping triggers
val_loss going UP, train_loss still going DOWNOverfitting — model memorising training dataAdd Dropout (0.3–0.5), add L2 regularisation, reduce model size, get more data
Both losses high and barely movingUnderfitting — model too small or learning rate too lowIncrease model size (more units, more layers), increase learning rate, train longer
