np.unique(arr, return_counts=True)
np.percentile(arr, [25, 50, 75])
np.corrcoef(arr1, arr2)
np.argmax(arr), np.argmin(arr)
np.where(arr > 0, 1, 0)                                   -- keep only rows that meet this condition
np.concatenate([a, b], axis=0)

Pandas — Core Operations

import pandas as pd                                       -- give this column or table a nickname

# Read / Inspect
df = pd.read_csv('data.csv', parse_dates=['date'], low_memory=False) # ← # load CSV file into a pandas DataFrame
df.info(); df.describe(); df.head(); df.sample(5)

# Select                                                  -- pick which columns to show
df['col']               # Series
df[['col1','col2']]     # DataFrame
df.iloc[0:5, 0:3]       # by position
df.loc[df['age']>25, ['name','salary']]  # by label + condition

# Filter
df[df['salary'] > 50000]
df[(df['dept']=='IT') & (df['salary'] > 50000)]
df[df['city'].isin(['Mumbai','Pune','Chennai'])]
df[df['name'].str.contains('Kavin', na=False)]

# Missing values
df.isnull().sum()                                         -- add up all values in this column
df['col'].fillna(df['col'].median(), inplace=True)
df['col'].fillna(df['col'].mode()[0], inplace=True)
df.dropna(subset=['important_col'], inplace=True)

# String operations
df['name'].str.lower().str.strip().str.replace('  ',' ') # ← # string accessor — apply string methods to a column -- convert text to all lowercase
df['email'].str.split('@').str[1]

# Apply
df['category'] = df['score'].apply(lambda x: 'High' if x > 80 else 'Low') # ← # apply a custom function to each row or column -- if no condition matched, use this default

# GroupBy
df.groupby('dept')['salary'].mean()
df.groupby('dept').agg({'salary':['mean','max','count'], 'age':'median'}) # ← # split data into groups by column value
df.groupby('dept').transform('mean')

# Merge / Join                                            -- connect with another table on a shared column
pd.merge(df1, df2, on='id', how='left')                   -- the condition that links the two tables
pd.merge(df1, df2, left_on='user_id', right_on='id', how='inner') # ← # SQL-style join: combine two DataFrames on a key -- connect with another table on a shared column

# Pivot
pivot = df.pivot_table(values='sales', index='region', # ← # aggregate and reshape — like a spreadsheet pivot -- pattern search (% = anything, _ = one character)
                        columns='product', aggfunc='sum', fill_value=0)

# Sort
df.sort_values('salary', ascending=False).head(10)
df.sort_values(['dept','salary'], ascending=[True, False]) # ← # sort rows by one or more column values

Pandas — Advanced & Time Series

# Time Series
df['date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['date'])
df.set_index('date', inplace=True)                        # use this column as the row label

df['year']    = df.index.year
df['month']   = df.index.month
df['weekday'] = df.index.dayofweek  # 0=Monday
df['quarter'] = df.index.quarter

# Resample: daily → monthly
df_monthly = df['revenue'].resample('M').sum()            # add all values together

# Rolling window
df['rolling_30d'] = df['revenue'].rolling(window=30, min_periods=1).mean() # ← # sliding window for moving average / rolling statistics
df['expanding']   = df['revenue'].expanding().sum()       # add all values together

# Performance optimization
df['status'] = df['status'].astype('category')  # low-cardinality strings

# Vectorized instead of apply (100x faster)
df['discount_price'] = df['price'] * (1 - df['discount']/100)  # FAST

# Read large files in chunks
chunks = pd.read_csv('large.csv', chunksize=100000)       # read a CSV file into a table
df = pd.concat([chunk for chunk in chunks], ignore_index=True) # ← # stack DataFrames vertically (or side by side)

# Explode (list in cell → rows)
df['tags'] = df['tags'].str.split(',')                    # work with text in this column
df_exploded = df.explode('tags')

# Melt (wide → long)
df_long = df.melt(id_vars=['id','name'],
                   value_vars=['q1','q2','q3','q4'],
                   var_name='quarter', value_name='revenue')

Phase 4 — Feature Engineering

Encoding Categorical Features

from sklearn.preprocessing import LabelEncoder, OrdinalEncoder, OneHotEncoder # ← # import LabelEncoder, OrdinalEncoder, OneHotEncoder from sklearn.preprocessing
from category_encoders import TargetEncoder               # grab TargetEncoder from category_encoders

# Label Encoding — for ordinal or tree-based models
le = LabelEncoder()                                       # convert text categories to numbers like 0, 1, 2
df['gender_enc'] = le.fit_transform(df['gender'])         # learn AND apply the transformation in one step

# Ordinal Encoding — when order matters
oe = OrdinalEncoder(categories=[['Low','Medium','High']])
df['education_enc'] = oe.fit_transform(df[['education']]) # ← # fit then transform in one step (use only on training data)

# One-Hot Encoding — for nominal categories (<10 unique)
df = pd.get_dummies(df, columns=['city','payment_method'], drop_first=True) # ← # one-hot encode categorical columns into binary columns

# Target Encoding — for high-cardinality (many unique values)
# ALWAYS use cross-validation to avoid leakage!
te = TargetEncoder(cols=['product_id'])
df['product_id_enc'] = te.fit_transform(df['product_id'], df['target']) # ← # fit then transform in one step (use only on training data)

# Frequency Encoding — replace category with its frequency
freq_map = df['city'].value_counts() / len(df)            # count how many times each value appears
df['city_freq'] = df['city'].map(freq_map)                # swap each value using a lookup or function
MethodUse WhenCardinality
LabelTree-based models, binaryAny
One-HotLinear models, no order<15 unique
TargetHigh-cardinality (city, product)>15 unique
FrequencyQuick, no target neededAny
OrdinalOrdered categories (Low/Med/High)Any

Scaling & Normalization

from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, MinMaxScaler, RobustScaler # ← # import StandardScaler, MinMaxScaler, RobustScaler from sklearn.preprocessing

# StandardScaler: z = (x - mean) / std → mean=0, std=1
# Use for: linear models, SVM, KNN, neural networks
scaler = StandardScaler()                                 # make all features the same scale (mean 0, spread 1)

# MinMaxScaler: x = (x - min) / (max - min) → range [0,1]
# Use for: when you need bounded range, image pixels
scaler = MinMaxScaler()                                   # squish all values to fit between 0 and 1

# RobustScaler: uses median and IQR → outlier-resistant
# Use for: data with outliers you can't remove
scaler = RobustScaler()

# CRITICAL: Fit on train only, transform both
X_train_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)  # fit+transform
X_test_scaled  = scaler.transform(X_test)        # transform only
# If you fit on test data → data leakage → inflated scores!
Never fit scaler on test data. Only transform test data using the scaler fitted on training data.

Transformations for Skewed Data

import numpy as np                                        # bring in the numpy as np toolkit
from scipy import stats                                   # grab stats from scipy
from sklearn.preprocessing import PowerTransformer        # grab PowerTransformer from sklearn.preprocessing

# Log transform (right-skewed data like income, sales)
df['log_income'] = np.log1p(df['income'])  # log1p = log(1+x), handles 0

# Box-Cox (positive values only)
df['boxcox'], lambda_ = stats.boxcox(df['income'] + 1)

# Yeo-Johnson (handles negatives too) — recommended
pt = PowerTransformer(method='yeo-johnson', standardize=True)
df['income_transformed'] = pt.fit_transform(df[['income']]) # ← # fit then transform in one step (use only on training data)

# Check skewness before and after
print(f"Before: {df['income'].skew():.3f}")               # show this value in the terminal
print(f"After:  {df['income_transformed'].skew():.3f}") # ← # print output to console
Rule: If |skewness| > 0.5 → apply transformation. If > 1.0 → log-transform. Check skewness after and confirm it's in [-0.5, 0.5].

Creating New Features

# Date features
df['day_of_week']  = df['date'].dt.dayofweek              # pull date parts like year or month from this column
df['is_weekend']   = (df['date'].dt.dayofweek >= 5).astype(int) # ← # convert column to a different data type
df['month']        = df['date'].dt.month                  # pull date parts like year or month from this column
df['quarter']      = df['date'].dt.quarter                # pull date parts like year or month from this column
df['days_since_signup'] = (pd.Timestamp.now() - df['signup_date']).dt.days # ← # datetime accessor — extract date parts (year, month etc.)

# Cyclical encoding (month 12 and 1 are close!)
df['month_sin'] = np.sin(2 * np.pi * df['month'] / 12)
df['month_cos'] = np.cos(2 * np.pi * df['month'] / 12)

# Interaction features
df['charge_per_tenure']  = df['MonthlyCharges'] / (df['tenure'] + 1)
df['total_per_contract'] = df['TotalCharges'] / df['tenure']
df['services_x_charge']  = df['num_services'] * df['MonthlyCharges']

# Binning (discretize continuous)
df['age_group'] = pd.cut(df['age'], bins=[0,25,35,50,100], # ← # bin continuous values into discrete intervals (age groups etc.)
                          labels=['Young','Mid','Senior','Elder'])
df['income_quartile'] = pd.qcut(df['income'], q=4,
                                  labels=['Q1','Q2','Q3','Q4'])

# Aggregation features (for group-level context)
dept_stats = df.groupby('dept')['salary'].agg(['mean','std','max']) # ← # split data into groups by column value
dept_stats.columns = ['dept_salary_mean','dept_salary_std','dept_salary_max']
df = df.merge(dept_stats, on='dept', how='left')          # join another table on a matching column
df['salary_vs_dept_avg'] = df['salary'] - df['dept_salary_mean']

# Text features
df['text_length']    = df['review'].str.len()             # work with text in this column
df['word_count']     = df['review'].str.split().str.len() # ← # string accessor — apply string methods to a column
df['has_exclamation']= df['review'].str.contains('!').astype(int) # ← # convert column to a different data type

Feature Selection

from sklearn.feature_selection import SelectKBest, f_classif, RFE -- specify the source table -- the table to read from
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier       -- the table to read from
import shap

# Filter method: correlation
corr_with_target = df.corr()['target'].abs().sort_values(ascending=False)
drop_cols = corr_with_target[corr_with_target < 0.05].index.tolist() -- speed up lookups on this column -- the condition that links the two tables

# Filter: multicollinearity check
corr_matrix = df.corr().abs()
upper = corr_matrix.where(np.triu(np.ones(corr_matrix.shape), k=1).astype(bool)) -- filter rows — only keep rows matching this condition -- keep only rows that meet this condition
to_drop = [col for col in upper.columns if any(upper[col] > 0.95)]

# SelectKBest (statistical test)
selector = SelectKBest(f_classif, k=15)
X_new = selector.fit_transform(X, y)
selected_features = X.columns[selector.get_support()].tolist()

# Embedded: Random Forest importance
rf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)
rf.fit(X_train, y_train)
importances = pd.Series(rf.feature_importances_, index=X_train.columns) -- speed up lookups on this column -- the condition that links the two tables
top_features = importances.sort_values(ascending=False).head(20).index -- speed up lookups on this column -- the condition that links the two tables

# SHAP values (model-agnostic)
explainer = shap.TreeExplainer(xgb_model)
shap_values = explainer.shap_values(X_test)
shap.summary_plot(shap_values, X_test, plot_type="bar")

Imbalanced Data

from imblearn.over_sampling import SMOTE, ADASYN          # grab SMOTE, ADASYN from imblearn.over_sampling
from imblearn.under_sampling import RandomUnderSampler # ← # import RandomUnderSampler from imblearn.under_sampling
from imblearn.pipeline import Pipeline as ImbPipeline     # grab Pipeline as ImbPipeline from imblearn.pipeline

print(y.value_counts(normalize=True))                     # count how many times each value appears
# If minority class < 20% → handle imbalance

# SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Oversampling)
smote = SMOTE(sampling_strategy=0.5, k_neighbors=5, random_state=42)
X_res, y_res = smote.fit_resample(X_train, y_train)

# Combined over+under sampling
pipeline = ImbPipeline([                                  # chain preprocessing + model steps into one object
    ('over',  SMOTE(sampling_strategy=0.3)),
    ('under', RandomUnderSampler(sampling_strategy=0.7))
])
X_res, y_res = pipeline.fit_resample(X_train, y_train)

# Class weights (no resampling needed)
from sklearn.utils.class_weight import compute_class_weight # ← # import compute_class_weight from sklearn.utils.class_weight
weights = compute_class_weight('balanced', classes=np.unique(y), y=y) # ← # unique values (and optionally their counts)
class_weight_dict = dict(zip(np.unique(y), weights))      # list of unique values (no repeats)
model = XGBClassifier(scale_pos_weight=weights[0]/weights[1]) # ← # XGBoost: fast gradient boosting, handles missing values
MethodProCon
SMOTECreates realistic synthetic samplesCan overfit boundary region
Class weightsNo data change, simpleLess aggressive correction
UndersamplingFaster trainingLoses majority class information
ADASYNFocuses on hard-to-classify samplesCan amplify noise

Phase 5 — Power BI & Tableau

Power BI & DAX

Data Model: Use Star Schema — 1 fact table (transactions) + dimension tables (date, customer, product). Connect via relationships on key columns.

-- Core DAX Measures
Total Sales = SUM(Sales[Revenue])
Avg Order   = AVERAGE(Sales[OrderValue])
Order Count = COUNTROWS(Sales)
Sales_2023 = CALCULATE([Total Sales], YEAR(Sales[Date]) = 2023)
Sales_Online = CALCULATE([Total Sales], Sales[Channel] = "Online")
Total_All = CALCULATE([Total Sales], ALL(Sales))
Market_Share = DIVIDE([Total Sales], [Total_All])
Category_Total = CALCULATE([Total Sales], ALLEXCEPT(Products, Products[Category]))
Pct_of_Category = DIVIDE([Total Sales], [Category_Total])
Sales_LY    = CALCULATE([Total Sales], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR(DateTable[Date]))
Sales_YTD   = TOTALYTD([Total Sales], DateTable[Date])
Sales_QTD   = TOTALQTD([Total Sales], DateTable[Date])
YoY_Growth  = DIVIDE([Total Sales] - [Sales_LY], [Sales_LY])
Running_Total = CALCULATE([Total Sales],
    FILTER(ALL(DateTable), DateTable[Date] <= MAX(DateTable[Date])))
Total_Profit = SUMX(Sales, Sales[Revenue] - Sales[Cost])
Weighted_Avg = DIVIDE(SUMX(Sales, Sales[Price] * Sales[Qty]),
                       SUM(Sales[Qty]))
Product_Rank = RANKX(ALL(Products), [Total Sales],, DESC, Dense)
Sales_Growth =
    VAR curr = [Total Sales]
    VAR prev = [Sales_LY]
    RETURN DIVIDE(curr - prev, prev)
Power Query tip: In M language, use Table.AddColumn for new columns, Table.SelectRows for filtering, Text.Trim for cleaning. Always close & apply before writing DAX measures.

Tableau

ConceptExplanation
DimensionsCategorical fields (blue pills) — slice and dice
MeasuresNumeric fields (green pills) — aggregate
DiscreteFinite set of values → headers in view
ContinuousContinuous range → axis in view
LOD FIXEDIgnore view filters — compute at specified level
LOD INCLUDEAdd granularity below current view level
LOD EXCLUDERemove dimension from current view level
// LOD Expressions

// FIXED: avg revenue per customer (regardless of view filters)
{ FIXED [Customer ID] : AVG([Revenue]) }

// INCLUDE: avg orders per customer per region
{ INCLUDE [Customer ID] : AVG([Orders]) }

// EXCLUDE: total sales excluding product dimension
{ EXCLUDE [Product] : SUM([Sales]) }

// Calculated Fields
Profit Margin: SUM([Profit]) / SUM([Sales])
Days Since Order: DATEDIFF('day', [Order Date], TODAY())
Customer Tier: IF [Revenue] > 10000 THEN "Gold"
               ELSEIF [Revenue] > 5000 THEN "Silver"
               ELSE "Bronze" END

// Table Calculations
// % of Total: Quick Table Calculation → Percent of Total
// Running Total: Quick Table Calc → Running Total
// YoY: LOOKUP(SUM([Sales]), -1) for previous period

// Parameters
// Create Parameter → Integer/String → use in calculated field:
Top N Products: RANK(SUM([Sales])) <= [Top N Parameter]

Phase 6 — End-to-End Case Studies

Case Study 1: Customer Churn Prediction

Business Problem: Telecom company losing 15% customers monthly. Build system to predict churn and recommend retention actions.
# ML Task: Binary classification (Churn=1 / Stay=0)
# Metric: Recall > 70%, AUC > 0.80
# Why Recall? Missing a churner (FN) costs more than false alarm (FP)

# Key EDA Findings: Month-to-month contract: 42% churn | Annual: 11%
# Fiber optic: 41% churn | No TechSupport: 41% churn | Tenure <12mo: 47% churn

df['avg_monthly_charge'] = df['TotalCharges'] / (df['tenure'] + 1)
df['num_services'] = (df[service_cols] == 1).sum(axis=1) # ← # sum all elements

from imblearn.over_sampling import SMOTE                  # grab SMOTE from imblearn.over_sampling
from xgboost import XGBClassifier                         # grab XGBClassifier from xgboost
sm = SMOTE(random_state=42)
X_res, y_res = sm.fit_resample(X_train, y_train)
xgb = XGBClassifier(n_estimators=300, max_depth=4, learning_rate=0.05, # ← # XGBoost: fast gradient boosting, handles missing values
                     scale_pos_weight=3, random_state=42)
xgb.fit(X_res, y_res)                                     # learn patterns from this data

import shap                                               # bring in the shap toolkit
explainer = shap.TreeExplainer(xgb)                       # SHAP explainer optimised for tree models
shap_values = explainer.shap_values(X_test)               # how much each feature pushed each prediction up or down
shap.summary_plot(shap_values, X_test, plot_type="bar") # ← # plot sorted feature importances (bar or beeswarm)
# Top drivers: Contract(+0.45), tenure(-0.38), TechSupport(+0.28)
# Estimated savings: $49,500/month at 72% recall

Case Study 2: Sales Revenue Analysis & Forecasting

Business Problem: Retail chain, 3 years data. Analyze performance, forecast next quarter, identify priorities.
# RFM Segmentation
snapshot = df['order_date'].max() + pd.Timedelta(days=1) # ← # maximum value
rfm = df.groupby('customer_id').agg(                      # split data into groups by a column
    Recency=('order_date', lambda x: (snapshot-x.max()).days), # ← # maximum value
    Frequency=('order_id','nunique'),
    Monetary=('sales','sum')
)
rfm['R'] = pd.qcut(rfm['Recency'], 4, labels=[4,3,2,1]).astype(int) # ← # convert column to a different data type
rfm['F'] = pd.qcut(rfm['Frequency'].rank(method='first'), 4, labels=[1,2,3,4]).astype(int) # ← # convert column to a different data type
rfm['M'] = pd.qcut(rfm['Monetary'].rank(method='first'), 4, labels=[1,2,3,4]).astype(int) # ← # convert column to a different data type
rfm['RFM'] = rfm['R'] + rfm['F'] + rfm['M']

# Prophet Forecasting
from prophet import Prophet                               # grab Prophet from prophet
df_prophet = df.groupby('order_date')['sales'].sum().reset_index() # ← # move current index back to regular column
df_prophet.columns = ['ds','y']
model = Prophet(seasonality_mode='multiplicative', changepoint_prior_scale=0.1)
model.fit(df_prophet)                                     # learn patterns from this data
forecast = model.predict(model.make_future_dataframe(periods=90)) # ← # apply learned model to make predictions

# Business Actions: Top 20% products = 80% revenue → protect stock
# Never exceed 25% discount (margin falls below 20%)

Case Study 3: HR Attrition Analysis

Business Problem: IT company, 8.5% attrition. Predict 90-day leavers, quantify replacement cost.
from lifelines import KaplanMeierFitter                   # grab KaplanMeierFitter from lifelines
from lifelines.statistics import logrank_test             # grab logrank_test from lifelines.statistics

kmf = KaplanMeierFitter()
for group, label in [(1,'OverTime'),(0,'No OverTime')]: # ← # loop over each item in the sequence
    mask = df['OverTime_enc'] == group
    kmf.fit(df[mask]['YearsAtCompany'],                   # learn patterns from this data
            event_observed=df[mask]['Attrition_enc'], label=label)
    kmf.plot_survival_function()

# Key EDA: OverTime=Yes attrition rate: 54% | No: 25%
# Tenure <5yr: most at-risk window
# ROI: Without action: $4.2M exposure. With 40% retention: save $1.68M.

Case Study 4: NLP Sentiment & Routing

Business Problem: Airline with 50K monthly social complaints. Classify sentiment, auto-route to departments.
import re                                                 # bring in the re toolkit
from nltk.tokenize import word_tokenize                   # grab word_tokenize from nltk.tokenize
from nltk.stem import WordNetLemmatizer                   # grab WordNetLemmatizer from nltk.stem
from nltk.corpus import stopwords                         # grab stopwords from nltk.corpus

def preprocess(text):                                     # define a reusable function
    text = text.lower()
    text = re.sub(r'http\S+|@\w+|#\w+|[^a-z\s]', '', text)
    tokens = word_tokenize(text)
    lemmatizer = WordNetLemmatizer()
    stop_words = set(stopwords.words('english'))
    tokens = [lemmatizer.lemmatize(t) for t in tokens
              if t not in stop_words and len(t) > 2]      # only run the next block if this is True
    return ' '.join(tokens)                               # send back a result from this function

from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline                     # grab Pipeline from sklearn.pipeline
from sklearn.feature_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer # ← # import TfidfVectorizer from sklearn.feature_extraction.text
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression       # grab LogisticRegression from sklearn.linear_model

pipe = Pipeline([                                         # chain preprocessing + model steps into one object
    ('tfidf', TfidfVectorizer(max_features=15000, ngram_range=(1,2), # ← # convert text to TF-IDF feature matrix
                               sublinear_tf=True, min_df=3)),
    ('clf',   LogisticRegression(C=1.0, class_weight='balanced', max_iter=1000)) # ← # logistic regression classifier (binary/multiclass)
])
pipe.fit(X_train, y_train)                                # teach the model using training examples
# Accuracy ~77%, F1 macro ~0.75

Interview Answer Framework & Top Q&A

The 7-Step Case Study Framework:
1. CLARIFY — "What's the business goal? What metric matters?"
2. DATA — "What data do I have? Quality? Missing? Imbalance?"
3. BASELINE — "Start simple — logistic regression, then improve"
4. EVALUATE — "Which metric? Precision vs Recall tradeoff?"
5. EXPLAIN — "Can I explain to a non-technical stakeholder?"
6. DEPLOY — "How does this run in production?"
7. MONITOR — "How do I know it's still working 6 months later?"
QuestionStrong Answer
Why XGBoost over Logistic Regression?LR is my baseline. XGBoost handles non-linear interactions, no heavy preprocessing. AUC: LR=0.71 vs XGB=0.84.
84% accuracy — is that good?Baseline predict-all-no-churn = 74%. I focus on Recall and AUC. My 72% recall vs 0% baseline matters more.
Model degrades over 3 months?Data drift monitoring via Evidently AI. If PSI > 0.2, trigger retraining pipeline.
Explain to non-technical manager"We can save $1.2M annually by retaining the 340 customers our model flags."
What is overfitting?Model memorizes training data including noise. Signs: high train accuracy, low test accuracy. Fix: cross-validation, regularization, more data.
Precision vs Recall — when?Precision: when FP is costly (spam filter). Recall: when FN is costly (cancer detection, fraud, churn).
How do you handle missing data?<5%: impute median/mode. 5-30%: impute + add binary flag. >30%: drop. Never impute test with test statistics.
Always quantify business impact. Numbers beat vague claims. "We saved $1.2M" beats "We improved retention".

Phase 7 — Model Evaluation Metrics

Confusion Matrix — The Foundation

Predicted: PositivePredicted: Negative
Actual: PositiveTP (True Positive)FN (False Negative) ← Miss
Actual: NegativeFP (False Positive) ← False AlarmTN (True Negative)
from sklearn.metrics import confusion_matrix, ConfusionMatrixDisplay # ← # import confusion_matrix, ConfusionMatrixDisplay from sklearn.metrics -- the table to read from
cm = confusion_matrix(y_test, y_pred)
TP = cm[1,1]; FN = cm[1,0]; FP = cm[0,1]; TN = cm[0,0]

accuracy  = (TP+TN) / (TP+TN+FP+FN)
precision = TP / (TP+FP)
recall    = TP / (TP+FN)
f1        = 2 * precision * recall / (precision + recall)
specificity = TN / (TN+FP)
Precision = "When I say Yes, how often am I right?" → TP / (TP+FP)
Recall = "Of all actual Yes cases, how many did I find?" → TP / (TP+FN)

Which Metric to Choose

ProblemPrimary MetricWhy
Cancer detectionRecall (Sensitivity)Missing a cancer patient (FN) is fatal
Spam filterPrecisionBlocking legit email (FP) is bad
Customer churnRecall + AUCMissing a churner costs revenue
Fraud detectionRecall + F1Missed fraud (FN) is costly
Imbalanced classesF1 / AUC-PRAccuracy is misleading
RegressionRMSE / MAE / R²RMSE penalizes large errors more

ROC Curve & AUC

from sklearn.metrics import roc_curve, roc_auc_score      # grab roc_curve, roc_auc_score from sklearn.metrics
y_prob = model.predict_proba(X_test)[:,1]                 # get probability scores instead of just the final label
fpr, tpr, thresholds = roc_curve(y_test, y_prob)          # shows the tradeoff between catching true vs false positives
auc = roc_auc_score(y_test, y_prob)                       # overall quality of rankings (0.5 = random, 1 = perfect)

# Find optimal threshold (Youden's J = max(TPR - FPR))
optimal_idx = (tpr - fpr).argmax()
optimal_threshold = thresholds[optimal_idx]
AUC ValueModel Quality
1.00Perfect (suspicious — likely data leakage)
0.90–0.99Excellent
0.80–0.89Good (churn model = 0.84)
0.70–0.79Fair
0.50Random classifier
AUC interpretation: "There is an 84% probability that the model ranks a random positive case higher than a random negative case." — say this in interviews.

Regression Metrics

from sklearn.metrics import mean_absolute_error, mean_squared_error, r2_score # ← # import mean_absolute_error, mean_squared_error, r2_score from sklearn.metrics
import numpy as np                                        # bring in the numpy as np toolkit

mae  = mean_absolute_error(y_test, y_pred)                # average distance between predictions and true values
rmse = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(y_test, y_pred))        # average squared error — big mistakes are penalised more
r2   = r2_score(y_test, y_pred)                           # how much of the variation the model explains (1 = perfect)
mape = np.mean(np.abs((y_test - y_pred) / y_test)) * 100 # ← # arithmetic mean (average) of values
n, p = len(y_test), X_test.shape[1]                       # shows (number of rows, number of columns)
adj_r2 = 1 - (1-r2)*(n-1)/(n-p-1)
MetricUse WhenSensitive to Outliers?
MAEYou want avg error in original unitsNo (robust)
RMSESame unit as target, penalizes outliersYes
R²How much variance is explainedNo
MAPE% error, scale-independent comparisonYes (div by small y)

Cross-Validation

from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score, StratifiedKFold, TimeSeriesSplit, KFold # ← # import cross_val_score, StratifiedKFold, TimeSeriesSplit, KFold from sklearn.model_selection

# Stratified k-fold (classification — preserves class ratio)
skf = StratifiedKFold(n_splits=5, shuffle=True, random_state=42) # ← # k-fold split that preserves class balance in each fold
scores = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=skf, scoring='roc_auc') # ← # evaluate model using k-fold cross-validation
print(f"AUC: {scores.mean():.4f} ± {scores.std():.4f}") # ← # print output to console

# Time series split (respect temporal order — no future leakage)
tscv = TimeSeriesSplit(n_splits=5)
for train_idx, test_idx in tscv.split(X):                 # loop through each item in the list
    X_tr, X_te = X[train_idx], X[test_idx]

# Learning curve to diagnose bias vs variance
from sklearn.model_selection import learning_curve        # grab learning_curve from sklearn.model_selection
import numpy as np                                        # bring in the numpy as np toolkit
train_sizes, train_scores, val_scores = learning_curve(
    model, X, y, train_sizes=np.linspace(0.1, 1.0, 10), # ← # evenly spaced numbers between start and stop
    cv=5, scoring='roc_auc', n_jobs=-1
)

Phase 8 — Hyperparameter Tuning

GridSearchCV & RandomizedSearchCV

from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV, RandomizedSearchCV # ← # import GridSearchCV, RandomizedSearchCV from sklearn.model_selection
from xgboost import XGBClassifier                         # grab XGBClassifier from xgboost
from scipy.stats import randint, uniform                  # grab randint, uniform from scipy.stats

# GridSearchCV: exhaustive search
param_grid = {
    'max_depth':     [3, 4, 5, 6],
    'learning_rate': [0.01, 0.05, 0.1],
    'n_estimators':  [100, 200, 300],
    'subsample':     [0.7, 0.8, 0.9]
}
# 4×3×3×3 = 108 combinations × 5 folds = 540 model fits

grid = GridSearchCV(                                      # try every combination of settings to find the best
    XGBClassifier(random_state=42), param_grid=param_grid, # ← # XGBoost: fast gradient boosting, handles missing values
    cv=5, scoring='roc_auc', n_jobs=-1
)
grid.fit(X_train, y_train)                                # teach the model using training examples
print(f"Best params: {grid.best_params_}")                # the settings that gave the best score
print(f"Best AUC:    {grid.best_score_:.4f}")             # the best score found during tuning

# RandomizedSearchCV: sample n random combinations
param_dist = {
    'max_depth':       randint(3, 8),
    'learning_rate':   uniform(0.01, 0.2),
    'n_estimators':    randint(100, 500),
    'subsample':       uniform(0.6, 0.4),
    'colsample_bytree':uniform(0.5, 0.5),
}
rand = RandomizedSearchCV(                                # try random settings — faster than trying everything
    XGBClassifier(random_state=42), param_distributions=param_dist, # ← # XGBoost: fast gradient boosting, handles missing values
    n_iter=50, cv=5, scoring='roc_auc', n_jobs=-1, random_state=42
)
rand.fit(X_train, y_train)                                # teach the model using training examples

Optuna (Bayesian Optimization)

import optuna                                             # bring in the optuna toolkit
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score       # grab cross_val_score from sklearn.model_selection
from xgboost import XGBClassifier                         # grab XGBClassifier from xgboost

def objective(trial):                                     # define a reusable function
    params = {
        'n_estimators':    trial.suggest_int('n_estimators', 100, 500), # ← # convert to integer (truncates decimals)
        'max_depth':       trial.suggest_int('max_depth', 3, 8), # ← # convert to integer (truncates decimals)
        'learning_rate':   trial.suggest_float('learning_rate', 0.01, 0.3, log=True), # ← # convert to floating-point number
        'subsample':       trial.suggest_float('subsample', 0.6, 1.0), # ← # convert to floating-point number
        'colsample_bytree':trial.suggest_float('colsample_bytree', 0.5, 1.0), # ← # convert to floating-point number
        'scale_pos_weight':trial.suggest_float('scale_pos_weight', 1, 5) # ← # convert to floating-point number
    }
    model = XGBClassifier(**params, random_state=42)      # supercharged gradient boosting — fast and powerful
    return cross_val_score(model, X_train, y_train,       # test model fairly by rotating which rows are for testing
                            cv=3, scoring='roc_auc').mean()

study = optuna.create_study(direction='maximize')
study.optimize(objective, n_trials=100, show_progress_bar=True)
print(f"Best AUC: {study.best_value:.4f}")                # show this value in the terminal
print(f"Best params: {study.best_params}")                # show this value in the terminal

Full Pipeline with Tuning

from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline                     # grab Pipeline from sklearn.pipeline
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler          # grab StandardScaler from sklearn.preprocessing
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer                  # grab SimpleImputer from sklearn.impute

pipe = Pipeline([                                         # chain preprocessing + model steps into one object
    ('imputer', SimpleImputer(strategy='median')),
    ('scaler',  StandardScaler()),                        # make all features the same scale (mean 0, spread 1)
    ('model',   XGBClassifier(random_state=42))           # supercharged gradient boosting — fast and powerful
])

# Prefix param names with step_name__
param_dist = {
    'model__n_estimators':  randint(100, 400),
    'model__max_depth':     randint(3, 7),
    'model__learning_rate': uniform(0.01, 0.15),
    'imputer__strategy':    ['median', 'mean']
}
search = RandomizedSearchCV(pipe, param_dist, n_iter=40, # ← # random hyperparameter search — faster than grid search
                              cv=5, scoring='roc_auc', n_jobs=-1)
search.fit(X_train, y_train)                              # teach the model using training examples
y_pred = search.best_estimator_.predict(X_test)           # let the model guess labels for test data
Always use Pipeline for tuning. Pipeline ensures scaler is fit only on training fold each time — prevents data leakage.

Phase 9 — Probability & Bayes

Core Probability Concepts

ConceptFormulaExample
P(A and B) — IndependentP(A) × P(B)P(H,H) = 0.5 × 0.5 = 0.25
P(A or B) — Mutually exclusiveP(A) + P(B)P(1 or 2 on die) = 1/6+1/6
P(A or B) — Not exclusiveP(A)+P(B)−P(A∩B)P(red or face card)
Conditional P(A|B)P(A∩B) / P(B)P(churn | month-to-month)
Expected ValueΣ x·P(x)E[dice] = 1(1/6)+2(1/6)...=3.5
# Common interview probability problems

# Birthday Problem: What's the probability 2 people in a group of 23 share a birthday?
import math                                               # bring in the math toolkit

def birthday_prob(n):                                     # define a reusable function
    p_no_match = 1.0
    for i in range(n):                                    # loop through each item in the list
        p_no_match *= (365 - i) / 365
    return 1 - p_no_match                                 # send back a result from this function

print(f"P(match in 23 people): {birthday_prob(23):.3f}")  # ≈ 0.507 → 50.7%!

# Monty Hall Problem: Switch doors → 2/3 chance win
# Intuition: initial pick has 1/3; remaining door has 2/3 after host reveals goat

# Coin flip: P(at least 1 head in 5 flips)
p_no_heads = (0.5)**5
p_at_least_one = 1 - p_no_heads
print(f"P(at least 1 head): {p_at_least_one:.4f}")  # 0.9688

Bayes' Theorem — The Most Important Formula

P(A|B) = P(B|A) × P(A) / P(B)
In plain English: Posterior = Likelihood × Prior / Evidence
# Classic interview problem: Medical Test
# Disease prevalence:    1% of population (prior)
# Test sensitivity:     95% (if sick → test positive)
# Test specificity:     90% (if healthy → test negative)
# Q: If test is positive, what's P(actually sick)?

P_disease     = 0.01   # prior
P_no_disease  = 0.99
P_pos_given_sick    = 0.95  # sensitivity = recall
P_pos_given_healthy = 0.10  # 1 - specificity

# Total P(positive test)
P_positive = (P_pos_given_sick * P_disease +
              P_pos_given_healthy * P_no_disease)

# Bayes: P(sick | positive)
P_sick_given_positive = (P_pos_given_sick * P_disease) / P_positive

print(f"P(sick | positive test): {P_sick_given_positive:.3f}") # ← # print output to console
# Answer: ~8.7% — counterintuitive! Low prevalence dominates.
# This is why screening rare diseases needs very high specificity.
Why this matters for ML: Naive Bayes uses this exact formula. And understanding base rates is crucial — a fraud model trained on 0.1% fraud data needs to account for P(fraud) being very low even when model outputs high probability.
# Spam filter (Naive Bayes in words)
# P(spam | "free money") = P("free money" | spam) × P(spam) / P("free money")
# Training: count "free money" in spam vs legit emails
# Naive assumption: each word is independent (rarely true but works well)

# Interview: "Why is it called Naive?"
# Because it assumes feature independence — P(word1,word2|spam) = P(w1|spam)×P(w2|spam)
# This is almost never true (words co-occur) but it still works surprisingly well.

Phase 10 — Business Metrics (DA Roles)

Growth & Revenue Metrics

MetricFormulaWhat It Measures
MRR (Monthly Recurring Revenue)Σ active subscriptions × monthly pricePredictable monthly revenue
ARRMRR × 12Annualized revenue
MRR Growth(MRR_now − MRR_prev) / MRR_prev × 100Month-over-month growth rate
Net Revenue Retention(MRR_start + expansion − churn − contraction) / MRR_startRevenue quality; >100% = growing from existing customers
Gross Margin(Revenue − COGS) / Revenue × 100Profitability per rupee of revenue
import pandas as pd                                       # bring in the pandas as pd toolkit

# MRR Analysis
df_mrr = df.groupby(pd.Grouper(key='subscription_date', freq='M')).agg( # ← # split data into groups by column value
    new_mrr=('monthly_price', lambda x: x[x.index.isin(new_customers)].sum()), # ← # sum all elements
    churned_mrr=('monthly_price', lambda x: x[x.index.isin(churned_customers)].sum()), # ← # sum all elements
    total_mrr=('monthly_price', 'sum')
)

# MoM Growth
df_mrr['mrr_growth'] = df_mrr['total_mrr'].pct_change() * 100

Customer Metrics

MetricFormulaBenchmark
CAC (Customer Acquisition Cost)Total Sales+Marketing Spend / New CustomersLower is better
LTV (Lifetime Value)Avg Order Value × Purchase Freq × Avg LifespanLTV:CAC > 3:1 is healthy
Payback PeriodCAC / Monthly Gross Margin per customer<12 months ideal
Churn RateLost Customers / Start Customers × 100<5% monthly for SaaS
NPS (Net Promoter Score)% Promoters (9-10) − % Detractors (0-6)>50 is excellent
CSATSatisfied responses / Total responses × 100>80% is good
# LTV Calculation
avg_order_value  = df['order_total'].mean()
purchase_freq    = df.groupby('customer_id')['order_id'].count().mean() # ← # split data into groups by column value
avg_lifespan_yrs = 3  # estimate from survival analysis or industry data

ltv = avg_order_value * purchase_freq * avg_lifespan_yrs
cac = total_marketing_spend / new_customers_acquired

ltv_cac_ratio = ltv / cac
print(f"LTV: ₹{ltv:,.0f}")                                # show this value in the terminal
print(f"CAC: ₹{cac:,.0f}")                                # show this value in the terminal
print(f"LTV:CAC = {ltv_cac_ratio:.1f}x")                  # show this value in the terminal
# Rule: <1x = unsustainable | 1-3x = OK | >3x = healthy | >5x = underinvesting in growth

# Cohort-based LTV (more accurate)
cohort_ltv = df.groupby(['cohort_month', 'period'])['revenue'].sum().unstack() # ← # split data into groups by column value
cumulative_ltv = cohort_ltv.cumsum(axis=1)                # running total — each row adds to the previous

Product & Engagement Metrics

MetricFormulaWhen to Use
DAU / MAUDaily/Monthly Active UsersApp engagement
StickinessDAU / MAU × 100>20% good; WhatsApp = ~70%
Retention RateUsers retained at Day N / Original cohortProduct-market fit signal
Conversion RateConversions / Total visitors × 100Funnel effectiveness
Bounce RateSingle-page sessions / Total sessionsLanding page quality
Session DurationAvg time per sessionEngagement depth
# Funnel Analysis
funnel_steps = ['landing_page', 'product_view', 'add_to_cart',
                'checkout', 'payment', 'order_placed']

funnel_counts = {step: df[df['event']==step]['user_id'].nunique() # ← # count distinct (unique) values in each column
                 for step in funnel_steps}                # loop through each item in the list

print("Funnel Analysis:")                                 # show this value in the terminal
prev = None
for step, count in funnel_counts.items():                 # loop through each item in the list
    if prev:                                              # only run the next block if this is True
        drop_pct = (prev - count)/prev * 100
        print(f"  {step}: {count:,} users ({drop_pct:.1f}% drop-off)") # ← # print output to console
    else:                                                 # if nothing matched above, do this instead
        print(f"  {step}: {count:,} users (entry)")       # show this value in the terminal
    prev = count

overall_cvr = funnel_counts['order_placed'] / funnel_counts['landing_page'] * 100
print(f"\nOverall CVR: {overall_cvr:.2f}%")               # show this value in the terminal

# DAU/MAU stickiness
df['date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['event_time']).dt.date     # pull date parts like year or month from this column
dau = df.groupby('date')['user_id'].nunique().mean()      # split data into groups by a column
mau = df['user_id'].nunique()                             # count how many different values exist
stickiness = dau / mau * 100
print(f"Stickiness: {stickiness:.1f}%")                   # show this value in the terminal

Phase 11 — Python Coding Round

List, Dict, String — Essentials

# ── List Comprehensions ──
squares    = [x**2 for x in range(10)]                    # generate numbers from start to end
evens      = [x for x in range(20) if x % 2 == 0]         # generate numbers from start to end
flat       = [x for row in matrix for x in row]   # flatten 2D list
pairs      = [(x,y) for x in [1,2] for y in ['a','b']]

# ── Dict Comprehensions ──
word_len   = {word: len(word) for word in ['apple','banana','cherry']} # ← # count number of items
freq       = {char: text.count(char) for char in set(text)} # ← # create a set of unique values

# ── Useful built-ins ──
nums = [3,1,4,1,5,9,2,6]
print(sorted(nums, reverse=True))      # [9,6,5,4,3,2,1,1]
print(max(nums), min(nums), sum(nums))                    # show this value in the terminal
print(list(enumerate(['a','b','c'])))  # [(0,'a'),(1,'b'),(2,'c')]
print(list(zip([1,2,3],['a','b','c'])))# [(1,'a'),(2,'b'),(3,'c')]

# Counter — most common elements
from collections import Counter                           # grab Counter from collections
text = "banana"
print(Counter(text))          # Counter({'a':3,'n':2,'b':1})
print(Counter(text).most_common(2))  # [('a',3),('n',2)]

# defaultdict — no KeyError
from collections import defaultdict                       # grab defaultdict from collections
graph = defaultdict(list)
graph['A'].append('B')   # no need to init graph['A'] = []

Common Interview Coding Problems

# Q1: Find duplicates in a list
def find_duplicates(lst):
    seen, dups = set(), set()
    for x in lst:
        if x in seen: dups.add(x)
        else: seen.add(x)                                 -- if no condition matched, use this default
    return list(dups)

# Q2: Two Sum — find pair that adds to target
def two_sum(nums, target):
    seen = {}
    for i, x in enumerate(nums):
        complement = target - x
        if complement in seen:
            return [seen[complement], i]
        seen[x] = i

# Q3: Flatten nested list
def flatten(lst):
    result = []
    for item in lst:
        if isinstance(item, list): result.extend(flatten(item))
        else: result.append(item)                         -- if no condition matched, use this default
    return result

# Q4: Most frequent element
from collections import Counter                           -- the table to read from
def most_frequent(lst):
    return Counter(lst).most_common(1)[0][0]

# Q5: Reverse words in a sentence
def reverse_words(s):
    return ' '.join(s.split()[::-1])                      -- connect with another table on a shared column

# Q6: Check if string is anagram
def is_anagram(s1, s2):
    return Counter(s1.lower()) == Counter(s2.lower())     -- convert text to all lowercase

# Q7: Fibonacci with memoization                          -- define a named temporary query (CTE)
from functools import lru_cache                           -- the table to read from
@lru_cache(maxsize=None)
def fib(n):
    if n <= 1: return n
    return fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)

# Q8: Moving average
def moving_avg(data, window):
    return [sum(data[i:i+window])/window for i in range(len(data)-window+1)] -- add up all values in this column -- add up all values in this column

# Q9: Group by key (like SQL GROUP BY)                    -- collect rows with the same value before aggregating
from itertools import groupby                             -- the table to read from
data = [('A',1),('B',2),('A',3),('B',4)]
grouped = {k: [v for _,v in g]
           for k,g in groupby(sorted(data), key=lambda x: x[0])}

# Q10: Merge two sorted lists
def merge_sorted(a, b):
    result, i, j = [], 0, 0
    while i < len(a) and j < len(b):                      -- count how many characters are in the text
        if a[i] <= b[j]: result.append(a[i]); i+=1
        else: result.append(b[j]); j+=1                   -- if no condition matched, use this default
    return result + a[i:] + b[j:]

OOP Basics (Asked in Technical Rounds)

class DataPipeline:                                       # define a new object type (blueprint)
    """Example class showing OOP concepts"""

    class_count = 0  # class variable (shared by all instances)

    def __init__(self, name, source):                     # define a reusable function
        self.name   = name    # instance variable
        self.source = source
        self.data   = None
        DataPipeline.class_count += 1

    def load(self):                            # instance method
        self.data = pd.read_csv(self.source)              # read a CSV file into a table
        return self                                       # send back a result from this function

    def clean(self):                                      # define a reusable function
        self.data.dropna(inplace=True)                    # delete rows that have missing values
        return self  # return self → enables chaining: pipe.load().clean()

    @classmethod
    def get_count(cls):                        # class method
        return cls.class_count                            # send back a result from this function

    @staticmethod
    def validate_extension(path):              # static method (no self/cls)
        return path.endswith('.csv')                      # send back a result from this function

    def __repr__(self):                        # string representation
        return f"DataPipeline(name={self.name}, rows={len(self.data) if self.data is not None else 0})" # ← # chain preprocessing + model into one reusable object

# Inheritance
class MLPipeline(DataPipeline):                           # chain preprocessing + model steps into one object
    def __init__(self, name, source, model):              # define a reusable function
        super().__init__(name, source)         # call parent __init__
        self.model = model

    def train(self, X, y):                                # define a reusable function
        self.model.fit(X, y)                              # learn patterns from this data
        return self                                       # send back a result from this function
OOP ConceptOne-line Definition
EncapsulationBundle data + methods together; hide internal details
InheritanceChild class inherits attributes/methods from parent
PolymorphismSame method name, different behavior in different classes
AbstractionShow only essential features, hide implementation

Phase 12 — How to Talk About Your Projects

Use the STAR format: Situation → Task → Action → Result. Keep each answer under 2 minutes. Always end with a number.

Project 1: Customer Churn Prediction — Script

"Tell me about your churn project."

"I built an end-to-end churn prediction system on the IBM Telco dataset — 7,043 customers, 21 features. The business problem was identifying which customers would leave before they actually do, so we could intervene with retention offers.

I started with EDA and found that month-to-month contract customers had a 42% churn rate vs 11% for annual customers — that became the most actionable business insight.

For modeling, I used XGBoost with SMOTE to handle the 26% class imbalance. I tuned with RandomizedSearchCV and achieved AUC 0.84 and 72% recall — meaning we catch 72% of actual churners before they leave.

I added SHAP explainability so the dashboard shows per-customer reasons — for example, 'this customer is at 71% churn risk because they are on month-to-month contract and have no TechSupport.' I also did KMeans clustering that revealed a 15x churn rate difference across segments — 3.5% to 54.7%.

The entire system is deployed — live Streamlit dashboard plus FastAPI REST endpoint on Render with Docker containerization. The API accepts customer data and returns churn probability plus retention recommendation in real time."

Project 2: Supplier Risk Intelligence — Script

"Walk me through your Supplier Risk project."

"This project monitors supply chain risk for 41 global semiconductor suppliers in real time — it was inspired by how companies got blind-sided during the 2021 chip shortage.

I built an ETL pipeline that pulls live data via Google News RSS and Yahoo Finance APIs with fallbacks, so monitoring never stops even if one source is unavailable.

For risk scoring I used a 70/30 ensemble — 70% ML gradient boosting model, 30% rule-based logic for known distress signals. The key result: zero false-safe classifications on validated distress events. That means no supplier under real stress was ever rated low-risk.

The system has a 5-tab Streamlit dashboard — overview, individual supplier drilldown, news sentiment, financial indicators, and alert history. Plus a FastAPI backend so it can be integrated into any procurement system.

What makes it unique is the real-time aspect — most supply risk tools are manual and quarterly; this one gives instant alerts."

Project 3: HR Attrition — Script

"Explain your HR Attrition project."

"The business problem was quantifying attrition risk and its financial cost — not just 'who might leave' but 'what does it cost us if they do.'

I used XGBoost for attrition prediction — AUC 0.79, 83% accuracy on 1,470 IBM HR records. The interesting finding was that OverTime=Yes employees had a 54% attrition rate vs 25% for others.

The unique part was the survival analysis using Kaplan-Meier curves. I proved with log-rank test (p < 0.0001) that OverTime employees leave 2 years earlier on average — median tenure 4 years vs 6 years for normal employees.

The cost API calculates replacement cost in real time using a 150% of annual salary model — industry standard. So for each at-risk employee, a manager can see: 'If this person leaves, replacement costs ₹8.4 lakhs.' That translates ML output into direct business value.

The whole thing is deployed on Render with Power BI integration for the HR dashboard."

Project 4: Retail Sales — Script

"Tell me about your Retail Sales project."

"This is a comprehensive revenue analytics project with both ML forecasting and business intelligence layers.

For forecasting I used a 4-model ensemble — XGBoost for tabular, Prophet for seasonality, ARIMA for traditional time series, and LSTM for deep learning. Combined R² was 0.9436 with RMSE of $0.10 — very tight.

The SQL layer is where I'm most proud — 19 advanced queries covering RFM segmentation, CLV modeling, Pareto 80/20 analysis, cohort retention, and window functions like LAG, LEAD, NTILE, and rolling averages.

The What-If discount API is a practical business tool — a manager types in 20% discount and instantly sees margin drops to 25.0%, vs 40% at zero discount. Break-even is at 40% discount. That kind of real-time scenario analysis is what converts an ML project into a business tool."

Project 5: Sentiment Analysis — Script

"Describe your NLP project."

"I built a 3-class sentiment classifier on 14,640 airline tweets — positive, neutral, negative — with 76.9% accuracy and 133% lift over a random baseline.

The interesting technical finding was that domain-tuned TF-IDF outperformed base BERT on this dataset. BERT is a powerful general model, but for in-domain airline vocabulary — terms like 'delayed', 'boarding pass', 'gate change' — TF-IDF with bigrams and sublinear TF scaling captured the signal better without the computational cost.

I also built an ABSA routing layer — Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis — that reads a tweet like 'rude staff and my bag was lost' and routes it simultaneously to Customer Service and Baggage teams. That's the real operational value — reducing manual triage of 50,000 monthly tweets.

Priority scoring multiplies sentiment weight by log of follower count and retweet count, so the most viral negative tweets surface at the top of the queue for immediate response."

Phase 13 — HR & Behavioral Round

"Tell Me About Yourself" — Your Script

"I'm Kavin Venkat, a final-year Mechanical Engineering student from Coimbatore Institute of Technology, graduating in 2025. Over the past two years I transitioned into data science by self-learning — and I didn't just take courses, I built and deployed real projects.

I've built five end-to-end ML systems — customer churn prediction, supplier risk intelligence, HR attrition analysis, retail sales forecasting, and NLP sentiment routing — and all of them are live in production on Streamlit and Render with FastAPI backends. Not Jupyter notebooks, actual deployed systems with REST APIs.

My internship at Flow Link Systems gave me real industry experience — I built a Power BI dashboard that eliminated manual Excel reporting and was adopted by the team within the internship window.

I'm now targeting data analyst and data science roles where I can bring both technical depth — Python, SQL, ML, deployment — and business thinking. I'm specifically drawn to [company name] because [1 specific reason about their product or data challenges]."
Tip: Last sentence must be customized per company. Research them on LinkedIn before every interview — mention one specific thing about their product, tech stack, or data problem.

Key Behavioral Questions & Answers

QuestionYour Answer Framework
Why data science with Mechanical background?"Mechanical gave me strong analytical and problem-solving foundation. I realized data drives every business decision and pivoted with intent — built 5 deployed projects to prove I'm serious. My mechanical background actually helps in supply chain and manufacturing analytics."
Where do you see yourself in 3 years?"I want to grow into a Senior Data Scientist / Analytics Lead role where I'm not just building models but owning the end-to-end data strategy for a product — from defining metrics to deployment to business impact. In 3 years I want to have driven at least one measurable business outcome — like reducing churn by X% or optimizing revenue by Y%."
Describe a challenge you overcame"My initial resume crossed 3 pages and had an ATS score of only ~55%. I researched ATS systems, understood what breaks parsing — unicode icons, CSS grid, multi-column layouts — and rebuilt it from scratch. The result: ATS score of 80-85% and a single clean page that's both machine-readable and visually appealing."
Biggest strength"I bridge technical and business thinking. I don't just build models — I translate output into rupee impact. My churn project doesn't say 'AUC 0.84' to stakeholders; it says 'we can save ₹40L/month by retaining these 340 customers.'"
Biggest weakness"I sometimes go too deep into optimization — spending extra time tuning a model from 0.83 to 0.84 AUC when the business doesn't need that last 1%. I've learned to define 'good enough' upfront based on business requirements, and timebox deep optimization."
Why do you want to join this company?"Research-specific answer: mention their product, their tech stack, or a specific data challenge they face publicly. Generic answers get rejected."
What salary are you expecting?"Based on my skill set — 5 deployed projects, full-stack DS skills, SQL, Power BI, API deployment — and the market rate for DA/DS roles at this level in [city], I'm targeting 10–12 LPA. I'm open to discussing the complete package."
Do you have any questions for us?Always ask: "What does the data team's toolstack look like?" / "What does the first 90 days look like for this role?" / "What metrics does this team own?" Never say "No, I don't have questions."

Salary Negotiation

Rule 1: Never give a number first. "What is your budget for this role?" — make them anchor first.

Rule 2: If forced, give a range: "10–12 LPA based on market research." The bottom of your range should be your actual minimum.

Rule 3: If they offer below your range, don't reject — counter once: "I was targeting 11 LPA based on the role scope and my 5 deployed production projects. Is there flexibility?"

Rule 4: Negotiate the full package: base salary, variable pay, joining bonus, ESOPs, learning budget, notice period.

Rule 5: Have competing offers if possible — even one real offer makes negotiation much stronger.
ScenarioWhat to Say
They offer 8 LPA, you want 11"Thank you for the offer. I was expecting 11 LPA given my project portfolio — 5 live deployed systems with REST APIs and Docker. Can we revisit the base?"
They say budget is fixed"I understand. Is there flexibility on the joining bonus or performance bonus target? I'd like to find a way to make this work."
You have another offer"I have another offer at X LPA. I strongly prefer your company because of [specific reason]. If you can match or come close, I'm ready to sign immediately."

Interview Day Checklist

  • Research the company the night before — product, team size, tech stack, recent news
  • Know your resume numbers by heart — AUC 0.84, 72% recall, 7,043 records, 41 suppliers, 14,640 tweets, R² 0.9436
  • Open your live projects in browser tabs before the interview — show, don't just tell
  • For technical rounds: think out loud. "My first instinct is X because Y. An alternative is Z. I'd pick X because..."
  • If you don't know something: "I haven't used that specific tool, but I've done similar work with [X] and I pick up new tools fast — I taught myself XGBoost and FastAPI from documentation in a week."
  • Send a follow-up email within 24 hours: "Thank you for the conversation today. I'm excited about [specific thing discussed]. Looking forward to next steps."

Phase 14 — Core DS Theory (Verbal Interview Questions)

Gradient Descent — Explained Simply

Interview analogy: "Imagine you're blindfolded on a hilly terrain and want to reach the lowest valley. Each step you take is in the direction that feels most downhill from where you stand. Gradient descent does the same — it looks at the slope of the loss function and moves the weights in the direction that reduces loss most."
# Manual gradient descent (linear regression)
import numpy as np                                        # bring in the numpy as np toolkit

X = np.array([1,2,3,4,5], dtype=float)                    # turn a list into a NumPy number array
y = np.array([2,4,6,8,10], dtype=float)                   # turn a list into a NumPy number array

w, b = 0.0, 0.0          # weights initialized to 0
lr    = 0.01             # learning rate
n     = len(X)                                            # count how many items are in the collection

for epoch in range(1000):                                 # loop through each item in the list
    y_pred = w*X + b
    loss   = ((y_pred - y)**2).mean()   # MSE loss

    dw = (2/n) * (X * (y_pred - y)).sum()  # gradient w.r.t. w
    db = (2/n) * (y_pred - y).sum()        # gradient w.r.t. b

    w -= lr * dw   # update: move opposite to gradient
    b -= lr * db

print(f"w={w:.3f}, b={b:.3f}, loss={loss:.6f}")           # show this value in the terminal
# converges to w≈2, b≈0 (correct: y = 2x)
VariantUpdate FrequencyProsCons
Batch GDOnce per full datasetStable, smooth convergenceSlow on large data
Stochastic GD (SGD)Once per sampleFast, can escape local minimaNoisy, unstable
Mini-batch GDOnce per batch (32–256 rows)Balance of speed + stabilityNeeds batch size tuning
Learning Rate IssueWhat HappensFix
Too highOvershoots minimum, divergesReduce LR; use LR scheduler
Too lowConverges very slowlyIncrease LR
Just rightSmooth convergenceUse Adam optimizer (adaptive LR)

Regularization — L1 vs L2

L1 (Lasso)L2 (Ridge)ElasticNet
Penalty termλ × Σ|w|λ × Σw²α×L1 + (1-α)×L2
Effect on weightsDrives some weights exactly to 0Shrinks all weights toward 0Both
Feature selectionYes (sparse model)No (keeps all features)Partial
Use whenMany irrelevant featuresAll features likely relevant / correlated featuresBoth conditions exist
Sklearn parampenalty='l1'penalty='l2'penalty='elasticnet'
Interview answer for "What is regularization?"
"Regularization prevents overfitting by adding a penalty for large weights to the loss function. L1 (Lasso) can zero out weights completely — it does built-in feature selection. L2 (Ridge) shrinks all weights proportionally — useful when all features matter. I always start with Ridge, then try Lasso if I suspect many features are irrelevant."

Ensemble Methods — Bagging vs Boosting

BaggingBoosting
How it worksTrain multiple models in parallel on random subsets; average predictionsTrain models sequentially; each corrects errors of previous
GoalReduce variance (fix overfitting)Reduce bias (fix underfitting)
Models independent?Yes — parallelNo — sequential
ExampleRandom ForestXGBoost, LightGBM, AdaBoost
Overfit riskLow (averaging reduces variance)Higher (can overfit if too many rounds)
Interview answer: "Bagging trains diverse models independently and averages them — Random Forest is the best example. It reduces variance. Boosting trains models sequentially where each one focuses on the mistakes of the previous one — XGBoost does this with gradient descent on the residuals. It reduces bias. For tabular data, boosting almost always wins on accuracy but takes more careful tuning."
# Stacking (advanced ensemble) — use predictions as features
from sklearn.ensemble import StackingClassifier           # grab StackingClassifier from sklearn.ensemble
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression       # grab LogisticRegression from sklearn.linear_model
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier       # grab RandomForestClassifier from sklearn.ensemble
from xgboost import XGBClassifier                         # grab XGBClassifier from xgboost

estimators = [
    ('rf',  RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, random_state=42)), # ← # ensemble of trees voting together — reduces overfitting
    ('xgb', XGBClassifier(n_estimators=200, random_state=42)) # ← # XGBoost: fast gradient boosting, handles missing values
]
stack = StackingClassifier(
    estimators=estimators,
    final_estimator=LogisticRegression(),  # meta-learner
    cv=5, passthrough=False
)
stack.fit(X_train, y_train)                               # teach the model using training examples

Backpropagation — What It Is

Interview answer (keep it simple):
"Backpropagation is how neural networks learn. In the forward pass, input flows through layers to produce a prediction. We calculate the loss — how wrong we were. In the backward pass, we use the chain rule of calculus to compute how much each weight contributed to that error. We then update weights using gradient descent to reduce the loss. This happens for every batch of training data until the loss converges."
StepWhat Happens
1. Forward passInput → Hidden layers → Output → Prediction
2. Loss calculationCompare prediction to true label (e.g. cross-entropy)
3. Backward passChain rule: ∂Loss/∂w = ∂Loss/∂output × ∂output/∂w
4. Weight updatew = w − learning_rate × gradient
5. RepeatUntil validation loss stops improving

Kernel Trick in SVM

Interview answer:
"SVM finds a hyperplane that separates classes with maximum margin. But in real data, classes aren't linearly separable in the original space. The kernel trick maps data into a higher-dimensional space where it becomes linearly separable — without actually computing the transformation. We just replace the dot product with a kernel function like RBF: K(x,z) = exp(−γ||x−z||²). This is computationally efficient because we never explicitly compute the high-dimensional mapping — just the kernel value between pairs of points."
KernelBest ForParameter
LinearLinearly separable data, text classificationC only
RBF (Gaussian)Non-linear boundaries, most tabular dataC, gamma
PolynomialImage recognition, structured dataC, degree, gamma
SigmoidNeural network-like boundariesC, gamma, coef0

Other Core Theory Questions

QuestionAnswer
What is a decision boundary?The surface that separates classes in feature space. Linear for LR/SVM-linear; curved for tree-based, RBF-SVM, neural nets.
What is the curse of dimensionality?As features increase, data becomes sparse — distance metrics lose meaning (all points are "far apart"). KNN and clustering degrade. Fix: PCA, feature selection.
Parametric vs Non-parametric?Parametric (Linear Reg, Logistic Reg, Naive Bayes): assumes data distribution, fixed number of parameters. Non-parametric (KNN, Decision Tree, Random Forest): no distribution assumption, parameters grow with data.
What is data leakage?When information from test/future data leaks into training — causes inflated scores that don't hold in production. Examples: scaling before split, target encoding without CV, using future features.
Explain the Bias-Variance tradeoffBias = error from wrong assumptions (underfitting). Variance = sensitivity to training data fluctuations (overfitting). Total error = Bias² + Variance + Irreducible noise. Increasing complexity reduces bias but raises variance.
What is multicollinearity?When two or more features are highly correlated. Harms linear models (unstable coefficients) but not tree-based. Detect with correlation matrix or VIF (>10 = high). Fix: drop one of the correlated features, PCA, or use Ridge.
What is heteroscedasticity?Variance of residuals is not constant across predictions. Violates linear regression assumption. Detect with residual plot (fan shape). Fix: log-transform target, weighted regression.
Explain p-value simplyProbability of observing data this extreme if the null hypothesis is true. p=0.03 means: "If there's truly no effect, there's only a 3% chance we'd see this result by chance." Below 0.05 → reject null → result is significant.
Why use log loss for classification?It penalizes confident wrong predictions heavily. Predicting 0.99 for the wrong class gets a much higher loss than predicting 0.51. This forces the model to be calibrated, not just right-or-wrong.
What is vanishing gradient?In deep networks, gradients shrink exponentially as they propagate backward through many layers — early layers learn almost nothing. Fix: ReLU activation (doesn't saturate), batch normalization, residual connections (ResNet).

Phase 15 — Time Series Deep Dive

Stationarity — The Foundation

A time series is stationary if its statistical properties (mean, variance, autocorrelation) do not change over time. Most classical models (ARIMA) require stationarity.
import pandas as pd                                       # bring in the pandas as pd toolkit
import numpy as np                                        # bring in the numpy as np toolkit
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from statsmodels.tsa.stattools import adfuller            # grab adfuller from statsmodels.tsa.stattools
from statsmodels.graphics.tsaplots import plot_acf, plot_pacf # ← # import plot_acf, plot_pacf from statsmodels.graphics.tsaplots

# ── Visual check ──
df['sales'].plot(figsize=(12,4), title='Sales over time')
df['sales'].rolling(12).mean().plot(label='12-month rolling mean') # ← # sliding window for moving average / rolling statistics
df['sales'].rolling(12).std().plot(label='12-month rolling std') # ← # sliding window for moving average / rolling statistics
plt.legend(); plt.show()                                  # display the chart
# If rolling mean/std change over time → non-stationary

# ── ADF Test (Augmented Dickey-Fuller) ──
# H0: series has unit root (non-stationary)
# H1: series is stationary
result = adfuller(df['sales'].dropna())                   # delete rows that have missing values
print(f"ADF Statistic: {result[0]:.4f}")                  # show this value in the terminal
print(f"p-value:       {result[1]:.4f}")                  # show this value in the terminal

if result[1] < 0.05:                                      # only run the next block if this is True
    print("STATIONARY — proceed with ARIMA")              # show this value in the terminal
else:                                                     # if nothing matched above, do this instead
    print("NON-STATIONARY — apply differencing")          # show this value in the terminal
ADF p-valueConclusionAction
p < 0.05Stationary (reject H0)Proceed with ARIMA(p,0,q)
p > 0.05Non-stationaryDifference the series (d=1 or d=2)

Making a Series Stationary & Decomposition

# ── 1st order differencing (removes trend) ──
df['sales_diff1'] = df['sales'].diff(1)                   # difference from the previous row
result = adfuller(df['sales_diff1'].dropna())             # delete rows that have missing values
print(f"After 1st diff — p={result[1]:.4f}")  # usually <0.05 after 1 diff

# ── Seasonal differencing (removes seasonality) ──
df['sales_sdiff'] = df['sales'].diff(12)  # 12 = seasonal period (monthly data)

# ── Log transform (stabilizes variance) ──
df['log_sales'] = np.log1p(df['sales'])                   # log(1+x) — same as log but safe when x=0
df['log_diff']  = df['log_sales'].diff(1)                 # difference from the previous row

# Decompose series into trend + seasonal + residual
from statsmodels.tsa.seasonal import seasonal_decompose # ← # import seasonal_decompose from statsmodels.tsa.seasonal
decomp = seasonal_decompose(df['sales'], model='multiplicative', period=12) # ← # decompose time series into trend + seasonal + residual
decomp.plot()
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()                            # display the chart

ACF & PACF — Choosing ARIMA Parameters

ACF (AutoCorrelation Function): correlation of series with its own lags.
PACF (Partial ACF): correlation at lag k after removing effects of shorter lags.
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14,4))            # create a figure with one or more chart panels
plot_acf(df['sales_diff1'].dropna(),  lags=30, ax=axes[0]) # ← # remove rows (or columns) containing NaN values
plot_pacf(df['sales_diff1'].dropna(), lags=30, ax=axes[1]) # ← # remove rows (or columns) containing NaN values
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()                            # display the chart
Pattern in ACFPattern in PACFModelParameter
Exponential decay / sine waveCuts off after lag pAR(p)p = where PACF cuts off
Cuts off after lag qExponential decayMA(q)q = where ACF cuts off
Both decay slowlyBoth decay slowlyARMA(p,q)Try small values of both
No decay (or slow)—Need differencing firstd = 1
# ── ARIMA Model ──  ARIMA(p, d, q)
# p = AR order (lags of y), d = differencing order, q = MA order (lags of errors)
from statsmodels.tsa.arima.model import ARIMA             # grab ARIMA from statsmodels.tsa.arima.model

model = ARIMA(df['sales'], order=(2, 1, 1))  # p=2, d=1, q=1
result = model.fit()                                      # learn patterns from this data
print(result.summary())                                   # show this value in the terminal

forecast = result.forecast(steps=12)                      # predict future values using the fitted model
conf_int = result.get_forecast(steps=12).conf_int()

# Auto-ARIMA (finds best p,d,q automatically)
import pmdarima as pm                                     # bring in the pmdarima as pm toolkit
auto_model = pm.auto_arima(df['sales'], seasonal=True, m=12,
                             stepwise=True, information_criterion='aic',
                             error_action='ignore', suppress_warnings=True)
print(auto_model.summary())                               # show this value in the terminal

Time Series Models Comparison

ModelBest ForHandles Seasonality?Explainable?
ARIMAStationary series, short-termNo (use SARIMA)Yes
SARIMASeasonal patternsYesYes
ProphetBusiness data with holidays/trends, missing values OKYes (auto)Yes (components)
LSTMComplex non-linear patterns, long sequencesYes (learned)No (black box)
XGBoost + lagsTabular features + time context, multiple seriesWith feature engineeringPartial (SHAP)
# ── Prophet (recommended for business data) ──
from prophet import Prophet                               # grab Prophet from prophet

df_prophet = df[['date','sales']].rename(columns={'date':'ds','sales':'y'}) # ← # rename column or index labels

model = Prophet(
    seasonality_mode='multiplicative',  # use when amplitude grows with level
    yearly_seasonality=True,
    weekly_seasonality=True,
    changepoint_prior_scale=0.1,        # flexibility of trend; higher=more flexible
    seasonality_prior_scale=10          # strength of seasonality
)
model.add_country_holidays(country_name='IN')  # Indian holidays
model.fit(df_prophet)                                     # learn patterns from this data

future   = model.make_future_dataframe(periods=90)
forecast = model.predict(future)                          # use the trained model to make guesses

# Components plot: trend + weekly + yearly seasonality
model.plot_components(forecast)
plt.show()                                                # display the chart

# ── Evaluation ──
from prophet.diagnostics import cross_validation, performance_metrics # ← # import cross_validation, performance_metrics from prophet.diagnostics
df_cv = cross_validation(model, initial='365 days', period='90 days', horizon='90 days')
df_perf = performance_metrics(df_cv)
print(df_perf[['horizon','mape','rmse']].tail())          # show the last 5 rows

Phase 16 — Excel for DA Interviews

Must-Know Formulas

FormulaSyntaxUse Case
VLOOKUP=VLOOKUP(lookup, table, col_index, FALSE)Find value in another table by key
INDEX-MATCH=INDEX(return_range, MATCH(lookup, search_range, 0))Better than VLOOKUP — works left, faster
SUMIF=SUMIF(range, criteria, sum_range)Sum where condition is true
SUMIFS=SUMIFS(sum_range, range1, crit1, range2, crit2)Sum with multiple conditions
COUNTIF=COUNTIF(range, criteria)Count matching cells
COUNTIFS=COUNTIFS(range1, crit1, range2, crit2)Count with multiple conditions
AVERAGEIF=AVERAGEIF(range, criteria, avg_range)Conditional average
IF nested=IF(A1>100,"High",IF(A1>50,"Mid","Low"))Multiple condition bucketing
IFERROR=IFERROR(formula, "N/A")Handle errors gracefully
TEXT=TEXT(date,"mmm-yyyy")Format dates as text
LEFT/MID/RIGHT=LEFT(A1,3) =MID(A1,2,4)Extract substrings
TRIM + UPPER=TRIM(UPPER(A1))Clean text data
CONCATENATE / &=A1&" "&B1Combine text fields
DATEDIF=DATEDIF(start,end,"D")Days/months/years between dates
RANK=RANK(A1, $A$1:$A$100, 0)Rank within a list
PERCENTILE=PERCENTILE(range, 0.75)Find Q3 / any percentile
-- Real interview scenario: Sales data — find top 10% customers by spend
-- Step 1: Calculate total spend per customer with SUMIF
= SUMIF($B:$B, E2, $C:$C)
= PERCENTILE($F:$F, 0.9)
= IF(F2 >= $H$1, "Top 10%", "Other")
= INDEX(C:C, MATCH(F2, A:A, 0))                           -- speed up lookups on this column
-- Returns value from C where A matches F2
-- Unlike VLOOKUP: works with lookup column on right, not slower with large data

Pivot Tables & Data Analysis

-- Pivot Table interview questions:
-- A: Insert → PivotTable → Rows: Region, Columns: Product, Values: Sum of Sales
-- A: Right-click value → Show Values As → % of Row Total
-- A: PivotTable Analyze → Fields, Items → Calculated Field → enter formula
-- Ctrl+Shift+L  → Toggle AutoFilter
-- Ctrl+T        → Create Table (essential for dynamic ranges)
-- Alt+D+P       → PivotTable wizard
-- F5 → Go To Special → Blanks (select all blank cells at once)
-- Ctrl+` (backtick) → Show all formulas
Tip: Always convert raw data to a Table (Ctrl+T) before creating PivotTables. This makes ranges dynamic — adding new rows auto-updates the pivot when refreshed.

Phase 17 — Git & Version Control

Essential Git Commands

# ── Setup ──
git config --global user.name "Kavin Venkat"
git config --global user.email "kv.sherly0217@gmail.com"

# ── Start ──
git init                      # new repo in current folder
git clone                # clone remote repo

# ── Daily workflow ──
git status                    # see what changed
git add filename.py           # stage specific file
git add .                     # stage all changes
git commit -m "feat: add churn prediction endpoint"
git push origin main          # push to remote
git pull origin main          # pull latest from remote -- specify the source table -- the table to read from

# ── Branching ──
git branch feature/new-model  # create branch
git checkout feature/new-model# switch to branch
git checkout -b feature/hotfix # create + switch in one command
git merge feature/new-model   # merge into current branch
git branch -d feature/new-model # delete branch after merge

# ── History ──
git log --oneline             # compact commit history
git log --graph --oneline     # visualize branches
git diff HEAD~1               # diff vs previous commit

# ── Undo ──
git restore filename.py       # discard unstaged changes
git reset HEAD filename.py    # unstage a file
git revert       # undo commit safely (creates new commit)
git stash                     # save work-in-progress temporarily
git stash pop                 # restore stashed changes

# ── .gitignore (always create this!) ──
# __pycache__/
# *.pkl
# .env
# data/*.csv
# venv/
# .ipynb_checkpoints/
ConceptSimple Explanation
BranchIndependent line of development — make changes without affecting main
MergeCombine changes from one branch into another
Merge conflictTwo branches changed the same line — must manually choose which version to keep
Pull Request (PR)Request to merge your branch into main — reviewed by team before merging
ForkYour own copy of someone else's repo — used for open-source contributions
RebaseReplay your commits on top of another branch — cleaner history than merge
Interview tip: "I use Git daily — all 5 projects are version-controlled on GitHub. I work on feature branches and merge via PRs. I write descriptive commit messages like 'feat: add SHAP explainability to churn dashboard' rather than 'updated stuff'."

Phase 18 — 50 Most-Asked DS/DA Interview Questions

Use this for last-minute revision before any interview. Read every answer out loud once.

Statistics & Probability (10 Questions)

#QuestionAnswer
1What is the Central Limit Theorem?As sample size increases (n≥30), the distribution of sample means approaches normal — regardless of the population distribution. This is why we can use z-tests and t-tests even on non-normal data with large samples.
2Difference between Type I and Type II error?Type I (α): False positive — reject null when it's true. Type II (β): False negative — fail to reject null when it's false. Medical analogy: Type I = healthy patient diagnosed sick; Type II = sick patient diagnosed healthy.
3What is confidence interval?A range where the true population parameter lies with X% probability. 95% CI means: if we repeat the experiment 100 times, 95 of those intervals will contain the true parameter.
4Pearson vs Spearman correlation?Pearson: linear relationship between continuous variables, sensitive to outliers. Spearman: rank-based, works for non-linear monotonic relationships and ordinal data, robust to outliers.
