• Use when: Warehouse is powerful enough to handle transformations (Redshift, BigQuery)

Why ELT is winning: Cloud warehouses are extremely powerful. Loading raw first preserves source data, and SQL transformations are version-controlled with dbt.
💬 Say in interview: "Modern stacks use ELT — raw data lands in the warehouse, then dbt models transform it into clean analytical tables. This keeps the raw data intact for reprocessing."
Serverless vs Managed vs Self-managed — Know the spectrum
Self-managed: You provision servers, install software, manage scaling, patching. Full control, full responsibility.
  • Example: EC2 running your own Postgres. You handle everything.

Managed: Provider handles infrastructure (patching, backups, scaling) but you still choose instance types.
  • Example: Amazon RDS, SageMaker training jobs. You pick compute, provider manages it.

Serverless: No servers to manage at all. Auto-scales to zero when not in use. Pay per execution.
  • Example: Lambda, BigQuery, Athena, Glue. No compute to provision — just submit code/query.

DS practical takeaway: Trend is toward serverless for analytics (BigQuery) and managed for training (SageMaker). Less infrastructure work, more data science work.
Parquet vs CSV — Why Parquet matters in cloud
CSV: Row-based, text format. Easy to read, but slow for analytical queries (must scan entire file).

Parquet: Columnar, binary, compressed format. Designed for analytical workloads.
Why Parquet wins for cloud analytics:
• Columnar storage: Query only the columns you need — 10x less data scanned
• Compression: 3-10x smaller than CSV (Snappy, GZIP encoding)
• Schema embedded: Data types stored in the file
• Cost: Athena charges per TB scanned — Parquet saves 90% of query cost vs CSV
# Save as Parquet in pandas
df.to_parquet("s3://bucket/data/sales.parquet",
              engine="pyarrow", compression="snappy")

# Read Parquet
df = pd.read_parquet("s3://bucket/data/sales.parquet",
                     columns=["city","revenue"])  # read only 2 cols
💬 Say in interview: "I'd store processed datasets in Parquet on S3 rather than CSV — it's columnar and compressed, so Athena/Redshift queries scan less data and cost less."
Docker + Cloud — How your existing skill connects
You already know Docker from your projects! Here's how it maps to cloud:

Your workflow:          Cloud equivalent:
---                     ---
docker build            -> Build container image
docker push to DockerHub -> Push to ECR (Elastic Container Registry)
docker run (local)      -> Run on ECS (Elastic Container Service)
Render deployment       -> AWS App Runner / ECS Fargate
FastAPI endpoint        -> SageMaker real-time endpoint
AWS ECS (Elastic Container Service): Run Docker containers in the cloud. You describe what image to run, how many copies, and ECS manages it.

AWS ECR (Elastic Container Registry): Private Docker Hub for AWS. Store your Docker images here.

💬 Say in interview: "I containerized my FastAPI model endpoint with Docker and deployed to Render. The same Docker image could be pushed to AWS ECR and run on ECS or SageMaker — I'm already comfortable with the containerization workflow."
MLOps on Cloud — The full deployment pipeline
This is what the cloud ML workflow looks like end-to-end:

1. DATA LAYER
   Raw data -> S3 (data lake)
   Glue/dbt transforms -> Redshift (data warehouse)

2. EXPERIMENTATION
   SageMaker Studio notebooks
   MLflow tracks experiments (params, metrics, artifacts)

3. TRAINING PIPELINE
   SageMaker Training Job / Vertex AI Custom Training
   Reads data from S3, trains model, saves .pkl to S3

4. MODEL REGISTRY
   SageMaker Model Registry / MLflow Model Registry
   Version control for models (model v1.2, v1.3...)

5. DEPLOYMENT
   SageMaker Endpoint (REST API)
   Lambda (serverless, lightweight)
   ECS/Fargate (Docker container)

6. MONITORING
   SageMaker Model Monitor (data/model drift)
   CloudWatch (latency, errors, invocations)
💬 Say in interview: "I understand the cloud MLOps pipeline conceptually — data in S3, training on SageMaker, models versioned in Model Registry, deployed as endpoints, monitored with CloudWatch. My Render+Docker experience covers the deployment step of this pipeline."
Cloud Interview Questions
10 questions
Q1What is the difference between S3, RDS, and Redshift?Must Know▶
All three are storage services but for completely different purposes:

S3 (Simple Storage Service): Object storage for ANY file type (CSV, Parquet, images, model.pkl). Unstructured. No SQL. Think of it as a filesystem.

RDS (Relational Database Service): Managed PostgreSQL/MySQL. Structured, row-based, for transactional workloads (OLTP). Handles live writes from your application — orders being placed, users registering.

Redshift: Columnar data warehouse for analytical queries (OLAP). Optimized for SELECT aggregations on billions of rows, not individual inserts. Where the BI team runs their dashboards.

Typical data flow: 
App -> RDS (live transactions)
RDS -> Glue ETL -> S3 (raw data lake)
S3 -> Redshift (cleaned analytics warehouse)
Redshift -> Power BI (dashboards)
Why asked: Very common opening question at data engineering and analytics interviews.
Q2What is the difference between a data lake and a data warehouse?Must Know▶
Data Lake (S3/GCS/Blob Storage):
• Stores raw, unprocessed data in any format (CSV, JSON, Parquet, images, logs)
• Schema-on-read: structure is defined when you read the data
• Very cheap storage, flexible
• Risk: becomes a "data swamp" without proper cataloging

Data Warehouse (Redshift/BigQuery/Snowflake):
• Stores cleaned, structured, query-optimized data
• Schema-on-write: structure enforced before data enters
• Expensive but fast SQL queries across billions of rows
• Used by BI team and data scientists for analysis

Lakehouse (Delta Lake/Iceberg): Emerging architecture — store files in S3 but add ACID transactions and SQL support on top. Best of both worlds.
Why asked: Every MNC and analytics company interview at this level asks this. Shows you understand data infrastructure.
Q3How would you deploy a trained scikit-learn model to production using cloud?Must Know▶
Option 1: Docker + SageMaker (enterprise approach) 
1. Train model locally, save to pkl
   import joblib; joblib.dump(model, 'model.pkl')

2. Write inference script (predict.py)
   def predict(data): return model.predict(data)

3. Containerize with Docker
   FROM python:3.10; COPY . .; RUN pip install -r req.txt

4. Push to AWS ECR
   docker push 123.ecr.aws/my-model:v1

5. Deploy as SageMaker endpoint
   predictor = model.deploy(instance_type='ml.t2.medium')
Option 2: FastAPI + Docker + Render (your current approach) — already production-ready for small scale

Option 3: Lambda (serverless) — for low-traffic, lightweight models. Cold start issue for heavy models. 
def lambda_handler(event, context):
    data = event['body']
    prediction = model.predict(data)
    return {'prediction': int(prediction)}
Why asked: Deployment questions appear at every company with a live ML product. You already do this with Render — this extends that knowledge.
Q4What is BigQuery and why do analytics companies use it over a normal database?Must Know▶
BigQuery is Google's serverless, fully managed data warehouse that can query terabytes in seconds using standard SQL.

Why it beats a normal database for analytics:
• Scale: PostgreSQL struggles above 100M rows. BigQuery handles petabytes.
• Speed: Massively parallel processing — 1TB query completes in seconds.
• Serverless: No cluster to provision. Start querying immediately.
• Cost model: Pay per TB scanned (not per hour). Idle costs nothing.
• BigQuery ML: Train models directly in SQL — no Python needed for basic models.
-- Train a logistic regression IN BigQuery SQL
CREATE OR REPLACE MODEL `dataset.churn_model`
OPTIONS(model_type='logistic_reg', input_label_cols=['churned'])
AS SELECT * FROM `dataset.features`;
Indian companies using BigQuery: Zomato, Zepto, Meesho, CRED, Razorpay
Why asked: If the company uses GCP (many Indian startups do), this is asked in round 1.
Q5What is Parquet and why would you use it instead of CSV in a cloud pipeline?Must Know▶
CSV problems at scale: Row-based, text format, no compression, no schema. Querying 1 column means reading ALL columns.

Parquet advantages:
• Columnar: Data stored column-by-column. Reading 2 of 50 columns reads only 4% of the file.
• Compressed: Snappy/GZIP encoding — 5-10x smaller than equivalent CSV.
• Schema embedded: Data types stored in the file — no type inference errors.
• Cost impact: Athena charges per TB scanned. Parquet + column pruning = 90% cost reduction vs CSV.
# CSV: 1 GB | Parquet: ~120 MB (with Snappy)
df.to_parquet('s3://bucket/data.parquet', compression='snappy')

# Read only needed columns (major speedup)
df = pd.read_parquet('s3://bucket/data.parquet',
                     columns=['customer_id', 'revenue'])
Rule of thumb: CSV for sharing/exploration. Parquet for production pipelines and cloud storage.
Why asked: Asked by any company with a cloud data pipeline. Shows you think about performance and cost.
Q6What is ETL? What is ELT? Which is better?Must Know▶
ETL (Extract-Transform-Load): Old approach. Data is cleaned/transformed OUTSIDE the warehouse before loading.
• Tools: AWS Glue, Informatica, Talend
• Pros: Warehouse only gets clean data. Works with limited warehouse compute.
• Cons: Complex pipelines, raw data not preserved in warehouse.

ELT (Extract-Load-Transform): Modern approach. Raw data loaded first, transformed INSIDE the warehouse using SQL.
• Tools: dbt (data build tool) + BigQuery/Snowflake/Redshift
• Pros: Raw data always available for reprocessing. SQL transforms = version controlled, testable.
• Cons: Need a powerful warehouse (cloud handles this easily now).

Which is better? ELT is the modern standard for cloud data stacks. The reason ETL existed was because old warehouses were expensive to run transformations on. Cloud warehouses removed that constraint.
Why asked: Fundamental data engineering concept asked at analytics companies and any role touching data pipelines.
Q7What is serverless computing? When would you use it for ML?Basic▶
Serverless: Run code without provisioning or managing servers. Cloud provider automatically allocates compute, scales to zero when idle, charges per execution.

Key examples: AWS Lambda, GCP Cloud Functions, Azure Functions

When to use for ML:
• Good fit: Lightweight inference (small sklearn models), event-driven scoring (new customer signs up -> get churn score), low traffic, unpredictable load
• Bad fit: Heavy deep learning models (Lambda has 10GB memory limit, 15-min timeout), high-traffic prediction APIs (cold start latency), GPU inference

Cold start problem: First invocation after idle period takes 1-3 seconds to initialize. Subsequent calls are fast. This is why Lambda is not ideal for real-time inference where latency matters.

DS use case example: "When a new order is placed in RDS, trigger a Lambda that reads customer features from Redshift, calls our churn model, and writes the score back."
Why asked: Shows you understand cloud architecture tradeoffs, not just that cloud services exist.
Q8How would you read data from AWS S3 into a pandas DataFrame?Basic▶
import pandas as pd
import boto3

# Method 1: Directly with pandas (if credentials configured)
df = pd.read_csv('s3://my-bucket/data/sales.csv')
df = pd.read_parquet('s3://my-bucket/data/sales.parquet')

# Method 2: boto3 (explicit control)
s3 = boto3.client('s3',
    aws_access_key_id='KEY',
    aws_secret_access_key='SECRET',
    region_name='ap-south-1'
)
import io
obj = s3.get_object(Bucket='my-bucket', Key='data/sales.csv')
df = pd.read_csv(io.BytesIO(obj['Body'].read()))

# Method 3: s3fs (filesystem interface)
import s3fs
fs = s3fs.S3FileSystem()
with fs.open('my-bucket/data/sales.parquet') as f:
    df = pd.read_parquet(f)
Credential management: In production, use IAM roles (not hardcoded keys). AWS SDK auto-picks credentials from environment variables, ~/.aws/credentials, or instance metadata.
Why asked: Practical hands-on question. Shows you can actually work with cloud data, not just talk about it.
Q9What is the difference between SageMaker and just running Python on EC2?Must Know▶
EC2 (raw virtual machine):
• Full control: install anything, run any workflow
• You manage: Python environment, dependency conflicts, storage mounting, scaling, shutdown when done
• No MLOps features: no experiment tracking, no model registry, no monitoring built in
• Risk: forgetting to shut down = wasted $$$

SageMaker (managed ML platform):
• Auto-provisions compute, shuts down after training completes
• Built-in: experiment tracking, model registry, endpoint deployment, drift monitoring
• Managed containers: pre-built Docker images for sklearn, TF, PyTorch, XGBoost
• Cost: slightly more expensive per hour but less wasted compute

When to use EC2: Custom environment, long-running research, need root access
When to use SageMaker: Production ML pipelines, team collaboration, need MLOps features, don't want to manage infrastructure
Why asked: Asked at companies building ML infrastructure. Shows you understand managed vs unmanaged tradeoffs.
Q10How would you explain cloud cost optimization to a non-technical manager?Often Missed▶
Three main levers for cloud cost:

1. Right-size your compute: Don't use a GPU instance to run pandas. Match compute to workload.
• Train: use spot instances (up to 90% cheaper — interruptible, fine for training)
• Inference: use the smallest instance that meets latency requirements

2. Storage tiers: Hot data (accessed daily) in S3 Standard. Cold data in S3 Glacier (90% cheaper). Archive rarely accessed raw data.

3. Format and query optimization: Parquet over CSV = 90% less Athena cost. Partition data by date/region = queries scan only relevant partitions.
-- Partitioned table: Athena only scans 2024 data
SELECT * FROM sales
WHERE year=2024 AND month=1  -- scans only 1/12th of the data
Interview framing: "As a data scientist, my biggest cost impact is data format (Parquet), reading only needed columns, and shutting down training jobs promptly. Infrastructure cost optimization beyond that is the data engineering team's scope."
Why asked: Sr. DS interviews increasingly include cost awareness. Shows business maturity beyond just writing models.

🐘 Pandas & NumPy — Reference + Interview Prep

Code reference cards for every operation + 15 interview questions. Use the search to filter cards.

🐘 Pandas Operations
⚡ NumPy Essentials
❓ Interview Q&A
💡 Performance order: vectorized ops > .str/.dt accessors > .apply() > iterrows(). Always prefer operations on the left.
Reading & Inspection
Read CSV / Excel / SQL
df = pd.read_csv("file.csv")
df = pd.read_excel("file.xlsx", sheet_name="Sheet1")
df = pd.read_sql("SELECT * FROM t", conn)
Use parse_dates=['col'] in read_csv for auto date parsing
Quick Inspect
df.shape        # (rows, cols)
df.dtypes        # column types
df.info()        # nulls + types
df.describe()    # stats summary
df.head(3)       # first 3 rows
Column Info
df.columns.tolist()      # list of names
df.nunique()             # unique count per col
df["col"].value_counts() # frequency table
df.isnull().sum()        # nulls per column
Selection & Filtering
Select Columns
df["col"]             # Series
df[["col1","col2"]]   # DataFrame
df.filter(like="rev") # cols containing "rev"
.loc[ ] — label based
df.loc[0, "name"]          # single cell
df.loc[0:3, "a":"c"]       # slice (inclusive)
df.loc[df["age"]>25, "name"]
Row and column labels. Slice end is INCLUSIVE
.iloc[ ] — position based
df.iloc[0, 1]        # row 0, col 1
df.iloc[0:3, 0:2]    # first 3 rows, 2 cols
df.iloc[-1]          # last row
Integer positions. Slice end is EXCLUSIVE
Boolean Filtering
df[df["age"] > 25]
df[(df["age"]>25) & (df["city"]=="MUM")]
df[df["city"].isin(["MUM","BLR"])]
df[~df["col"].isnull()]   # not null
.query() — readable filter
df.query("age > 25 and city == 'MUM'")
val = 25
df.query("age > @val")  # use local var with @
Cleaner than boolean masks for complex conditions
Sample Rows
df.sample(n=100, random_state=42)
df.sample(frac=0.1)   # 10% of rows
GroupBy & Aggregation
Basic GroupBy
df.groupby("city")["revenue"].sum()
df.groupby("city")["revenue"].mean()
df.groupby(["city","cat"])["rev"].sum()
Returns a Series; use reset_index() to get DataFrame back
.agg() — multiple stats
df.groupby("city").agg(
  total=("revenue","sum"),
  avg=("revenue","mean"),
  cnt=("id","count")
)
Named aggregation syntax (pandas 0.25+)
.transform() — broadcast back
# Add group-level stat back to each row
df["city_avg"] = df.groupby("city")["rev"]\
                   .transform("mean")
Returns same-shape result as original DataFrame
.apply() — custom function
# Custom aggregation per group
def top2(g): return g.nlargest(2)
df.groupby("city").apply(top2)
More flexible but slower than built-in agg functions
Pivot Table
pd.pivot_table(
  df,
  values="revenue",
  index="city",
  columns="category",
  aggfunc="sum",
  fill_value=0
)
groupby reshapes data; pivot_table creates cross-tab matrix
Rolling & Expanding
# 7-day rolling average
df["rev_7d"] = df["revenue"].rolling(7).mean()
# Cumulative sum
df["rev_cum"] = df["revenue"].expanding().sum()
# Shift (lag)
df["prev"] = df["revenue"].shift(1)
Merging & Joining
pd.merge() — SQL-style JOIN
pd.merge(left, right, on="id", how="inner")
pd.merge(left, right, on="id", how="left")
pd.merge(left, right,
         left_on="cust_id", right_on="id")
how: inner | left | right | outer
pd.concat() — stack rows/cols
# Stack rows (axis=0 default)
pd.concat([df1, df2], ignore_index=True)
# Stack columns (axis=1)
pd.concat([df1, df2], axis=1)
concat stacks; merge matches on keys
Merge suffixes & validate
pd.merge(left, right, on="id",
         how="left",
         suffixes=("_l","_r"),
         validate="m:1")
validate catches unexpected duplicates: 1:1, 1:m, m:1, m:m
Missing Data
Detect & Count Nulls
df.isnull().sum()           # nulls per col
df.isnull().sum() / len(df) # null rate
df[df["col"].isnull()]      # rows with null
Fill Nulls
df["col"].fillna(0)             # constant
df["col"].fillna(df["col"].mean())
df["col"].fillna(method="ffill") # forward fill
df["col"].fillna(method="bfill")
For time series: ffill (carry forward) works well
Drop Nulls
df.dropna()             # rows with ANY null
df.dropna(subset=["col1","col2"])
df.dropna(thresh=5)     # keep rows with >= 5 non-null
Duplicates
df.duplicated().sum()           # count dupes
df[df.duplicated()]             # see dupes
df.drop_duplicates()            # drop all
df.drop_duplicates(subset=["email"], keep="first")
Transformations
.apply() on column
df["col"].apply(lambda x: x*2)
df["col"].apply(lambda x: "high" if x>100 else "low")
df.apply(lambda row: row["a"]+row["b"], axis=1)
Slower than vectorized ops; use for complex logic only
map / replace
# Map via dict
df["grade"] = df["score"].map({1:"A",2:"B",3:"C"})
# Replace values
df["city"].replace("Bengaluru","Bangalore",inplace=True)
Binning (cut / qcut)
# Equal-width bins
df["age_band"] = pd.cut(df["age"],
                        bins=[0,18,35,60,100],
                        labels=["<18","18-35","35-60","60+"])
# Equal-frequency bins
df["quartile"] = pd.qcut(df["score"], q=4)
Type Casting
df["col"].astype(int)
df["col"].astype("category")  # memory efficient
pd.to_numeric(df["col"], errors="coerce")
pd.to_datetime(df["date"], format="%Y-%m-%d")
Rename & Drop
df.rename(columns={"old":"new"})
df.drop(columns=["col1","col2"])
df.drop(index=0)
df.reset_index(drop=True)
Sort & Rank
df.sort_values("col", ascending=False)
df.sort_values(["city","rev"], ascending=[True,False])
df.nlargest(5, "revenue")
df["rank"] = df["score"].rank(method="dense",
                               ascending=False)
String Operations (.str)
Common String Ops
df["name"].str.lower()
df["name"].str.strip()
df["email"].str.contains("@gmail")
df["name"].str.startswith("A")
df["phone"].str.replace("-","")
Split & Extract
# Split into multiple columns
df[["first","last"]] = df["name"].str.split(" ", expand=True)
# Extract via regex
df["pin"] = df["address"].str.extract(r"(\d{6})")
Datetime Operations (.dt)
Date Components
df["date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["date"])
df["year"]    = df["date"].dt.year
df["month"]   = df["date"].dt.month
df["dayofweek"] = df["date"].dt.dayofweek # 0=Mon
df["quarter"] = df["date"].dt.quarter
Date Arithmetic
# Days between two dates
(df["end"] - df["start"]).dt.days
# Add 30 days
df["due"] = df["date"] + pd.Timedelta(days=30)
# Group by month
df.groupby(df["date"].dt.to_period("M")).sum()
Reshaping
melt() — wide to long
pd.melt(df,
        id_vars=["city"],
        value_vars=["q1","q2","q3","q4"],
        var_name="quarter",
        value_name="revenue")
Converts multiple columns into rows — useful for seaborn/tableau
stack / unstack
# stack: columns -> rows (MultiIndex)
df.stack()
# unstack: rows -> columns
df.unstack(level=0)
💡 Key insight: NumPy arrays are fixed-type, C-contiguous blocks of memory. This is why they're 10-100x faster than Python lists for numerical ops.
Array Creation
Create Arrays
np.array([1,2,3])           # from list
np.zeros((3,4))             # 3x4 zeros
np.ones((2,3))              # 2x3 ones
np.eye(3)                   # 3x3 identity
np.full((2,2), 7)           # filled with 7
Range Arrays
np.arange(0, 10, 2)        # [0,2,4,6,8]
np.linspace(0, 1, 5)       # 5 evenly spaced
np.logspace(0, 3, 4)       # [1,10,100,1000]
Random Arrays
np.random.seed(42)         # reproducibility
np.random.rand(3,4)        # uniform [0,1)
np.random.randn(3,4)       # standard normal
np.random.randint(0,10,5)  # int in [0,10)
np.random.choice(arr, 5, replace=False)
Shape & Indexing
Shape Operations
a.shape       # (rows, cols)
a.ndim        # number of dimensions
a.dtype       # data type
a.size        # total elements
a.reshape(2,6)  # new shape (must be compatible)
a.flatten()     # always returns copy
a.ravel()       # returns view if possible
Transpose & Axes
a.T              # transpose
np.transpose(a)  # same
a.swapaxes(0,1)  # swap two axes
Indexing & Slicing
a[0]            # first row (2D: returns row)
a[0, 1]         # row 0, col 1
a[:, 1]         # all rows, col 1
a[0:2, 1:3]     # slice
a[[0,2], :]     # fancy indexing (copy!)
Basic slicing returns a VIEW; fancy indexing returns a COPY
Boolean Indexing
a[a > 5]               # values > 5
a[(a > 2) & (a < 8)]  # combined condition
np.where(a > 5, a, 0) # replace falsy with 0
np.where(a > 5, "H", "L")
Math & Stats
Element-wise Math
a + b   # add (broadcasting applies)
a * b   # multiply element-wise
a ** 2  # square each element
np.sqrt(a)  np.abs(a)  np.log(a)
np.exp(a)   np.sin(a)  np.cos(a)
Aggregation
np.sum(a)           # all elements
np.sum(a, axis=0)   # sum each column
np.sum(a, axis=1)   # sum each row
np.mean(a)  np.std(a)  np.var(a)
np.min(a)   np.max(a)
np.argmin(a)  np.argmax(a)  # index of min/max
Clip & Round
np.clip(a, 0, 100)    # bound values
np.round(a, 2)        # round to 2 decimal
np.floor(a)  np.ceil(a)
Sorting
np.sort(a)             # returns sorted copy
np.argsort(a)          # indices that sort a
np.sort(a, axis=0)     # sort each column
a[np.argsort(a)]       # same as np.sort
Combining Arrays
Stack & Concat
# Same shape, along new axis
np.stack([a, b], axis=0)
# Horizontal stack (same rows)
np.hstack([a, b])   # or np.c_[a, b]
# Vertical stack (same cols)
np.vstack([a, b])   # or np.r_[a, b]
# General concat
np.concatenate([a, b], axis=1)
Splitting
np.split(a, 3)         # 3 equal parts
np.hsplit(a, 2)        # split horizontally
np.vsplit(a, 2)        # split vertically
Broadcasting & Linear Algebra
Broadcasting Rules
# Shapes are compatible if dimensions are
# equal or one of them is 1
a = np.ones((3, 4))    # (3,4)
b = np.ones((1, 4))    # (1,4) -> broadcasts to (3,4)
a + b  # works: b stretched across 3 rows
c = np.ones((3,))      # (3,) -> (1,3) -> mismatches (3,4)
Rule: compare shapes right-to-left; must be equal or 1
Dot Product & Matrix Ops
np.dot(a, b)         # matrix multiply
a @ b                # same (Python 3.5+)
np.linalg.inv(a)     # matrix inverse
np.linalg.det(a)     # determinant
np.linalg.norm(a)    # L2 norm
np.linalg.eig(a)     # eigenvalues/vectors
Useful Utilities
np.unique(a)              # unique values
np.unique(a, return_counts=True)
np.percentile(a, [25,50,75])
np.corrcoef(x, y)         # correlation matrix
np.cov(x, y)              # covariance matrix
np.isnan(a).sum()         # count NaNs
Pandas & NumPy Interview Questions
15 questions
Q1What is the difference between .loc[] and .iloc[]?Must Know▶
.loc[] is label-based — you use actual index labels and column names. Slice end is inclusive.
.iloc[] is position-based — you use integer positions (0-indexed). Slice end is exclusive (like Python lists).
df = pd.DataFrame({'score':[90,80,70]}, index=['a','b','c'])
df.loc['a':'b', 'score']   # rows a AND b (inclusive)
df.iloc[0:2, 0]            # rows 0 and 1 only (exclusive end)
Common trap: When the index is integers (0,1,2...), df.loc[0:2] includes row 2 but df.iloc[0:2] excludes it.
Why asked: Asked in nearly every interview. Shows you understand pandas indexing fundamentals.
Q2merge vs join vs concat — when to use which?Must Know▶
pd.merge(): SQL-style join on columns or index. Most flexible. Use when matching on key columns.
df.join(): Joins on index by default. Shortcut for merging on index.
pd.concat(): Stacks DataFrames along rows (axis=0) or columns (axis=1). No key matching — just appends.
# merge: customer + orders on customer_id
pd.merge(customers, orders, on='customer_id', how='left')
# concat: stack two monthly reports
pd.concat([jan_df, feb_df], ignore_index=True)
# join: both DataFrames share the same index
df1.join(df2, how='inner')
Why asked: Classic interview question. Tests whether you know the difference between combining on keys vs just stacking.
Q3How does groupby work internally?Must Know▶
GroupBy uses a Split-Apply-Combine pattern:
1. Split: divide DataFrame into groups based on key(s)
2. Apply: apply a function to each group independently
3. Combine: assemble results back into a DataFrame
df.groupby('city')['revenue'].sum()
# Split: {MUM: rows, BLR: rows, ...}
# Apply: sum() on each group's revenue column
# Combine: city -> total Series
transform vs agg: agg() returns one row per group (reduces); transform() returns same number of rows as original (broadcasts group result back).
Why asked: Appears in almost every Pandas interview. Understanding transform vs agg is a common follow-up.
Q4When would you use apply() vs vectorized operations?Must Know▶
Use vectorized operations whenever possible — they use NumPy's C internals and are 10-100x faster.
Use .apply() only when: the logic is too complex for vectorized ops, involves external APIs, or requires row-level conditions across multiple columns.
# SLOW: apply for simple math
df['doubled'] = df['x'].apply(lambda x: x * 2)
# FAST: vectorized
df['doubled'] = df['x'] * 2

# apply is justified here (multi-col logic)
df['flag'] = df.apply(
    lambda r: 'high' if r['rev'] > r['cost']*2 else 'low',
    axis=1
)
Why asked: Shows performance awareness — a key trait for a data scientist working with large datasets.
Q5What is the difference between a copy and a view in pandas?Often Missed▶
A view shares memory with the original DataFrame — modifying it modifies the original.
A copy is an independent object — changes don't affect the original.
sub = df[df['age'] > 25]     # might be view or copy (ambiguous!)
sub['name'] = 'test'          # SettingWithCopyWarning

# Safe approach: always explicit
sub = df[df['age'] > 25].copy()   # explicit copy
sub['name'] = 'test'              # safe, doesn't affect df
Rule of thumb: whenever you filter a DataFrame and plan to modify it, call .copy() to avoid the SettingWithCopyWarning.
Why asked: SettingWithCopyWarning is one of the most confusing pandas errors. Interviewers love this.
Q6How do you handle missing values strategically (not just fillna)?Must Know▶
Strategy depends on WHY data is missing:
• MCAR (Missing Completely At Random): safe to drop or impute with mean/median
• MAR (Missing At Random, conditional): impute using related columns (e.g., KNNImputer)
• MNAR (Missing Not At Random): dropping biases results; consider adding a missing indicator column
from sklearn.impute import KNNImputer, SimpleImputer

# Numerical: median imputation
df['salary'].fillna(df['salary'].median(), inplace=True)

# Add missingness indicator before imputing
df['salary_missing'] = df['salary'].isnull().astype(int)

# KNN imputation (uses relationships between columns)
imp = KNNImputer(n_neighbors=5)
df[['salary','age']] = imp.fit_transform(df[['salary','age']])
Why asked: Interviewers at product companies (Flipkart, Amazon) ask about handling real-world messy data.
Q7What is broadcasting in NumPy? Give an example.Must Know▶
Broadcasting is NumPy's rule for performing operations on arrays of different shapes without explicit replication.
Rules (compare shapes right-to-left):
1. Dimensions must be equal, or
2. One of them must be 1 (gets stretched)
a = np.array([[1,2,3],[4,5,6]])  # shape (2,3)
b = np.array([10,20,30])          # shape (3,)  -> (1,3) -> (2,3)
a + b   # [[11,22,33],[14,25,36]]

# Column-wise operation
col = np.array([[100],[200]])      # shape (2,1) -> (2,3)
a + col  # [[101,102,103],[204,205,206]]
Why it matters: saves memory (no copying) and speed (C-level ops). Most scikit-learn transformations use broadcasting internally.
Why asked: Fundamental NumPy concept tested at MNCs and data engineering roles.
Q8What is the difference between pivot_table and groupby?Must Know▶
Both aggregate data but output differently:
groupby: returns a long/tall result (one row per group). You get a Series or DataFrame.
pivot_table: returns a wide cross-tabulation matrix (rows x columns = two grouping dimensions).
# groupby: city -> total revenue (long format)
df.groupby('city')['revenue'].sum()

# pivot_table: city x category matrix
pd.pivot_table(df,
    values='revenue', index='city',
    columns='category', aggfunc='sum', fill_value=0)
# Result: cities as rows, categories as columns, revenue as values
Use pivot_table when you need a cross-tab view (like an Excel pivot). Use groupby for everything else.
Why asked: Comes up in analytics and reporting interviews. Shows you can present data in business-ready format.
Q9How do you optimize Pandas for large datasets?Must Know▶
1. Use efficient dtypes — biggest win
df['city'] = df['city'].astype('category')  # string -> category
df['flag'] = df['flag'].astype('int8')       # int64 -> int8 (if values fit)
df['price'] = df['price'].astype('float32')  # halves memory
2. Read only needed columns: pd.read_csv(f, usecols=['a','b'])
3. Chunked reading for huge files:
for chunk in pd.read_csv('big.csv', chunksize=10000):
    process(chunk)
4. Use vectorized ops and avoid loops
5. Use query() instead of boolean masks for complex filters
6. Consider Polars or Dask for truly large data
Why asked: Senior DS interviews at scale-ups and MNCs test whether you know performance, not just correctness.
Q10What is method chaining in pandas and why use it?Basic▶
Method chaining strings operations together without creating intermediate variables.
# Without chaining (creates temp variables)
df1 = df.dropna(subset=['revenue'])
df2 = df1[df1['revenue'] > 0]
df3 = df2.groupby('city')['revenue'].sum()
result = df3.reset_index()

# With chaining (clean, readable)
result = (
    df
    .dropna(subset=['revenue'])
    .query('revenue > 0')
    .groupby('city')['revenue']
    .sum()
    .reset_index()
)
Benefits: readable top-to-bottom, fewer intermediate variables, easier to debug step by step.
Why asked: Shows you write clean, production-quality pandas code — valued at any company.
Q11Explain np.where() with a real use case.Must Know▶
np.where(condition, value_if_true, value_if_false) — vectorized if/else.
# Simple flag column
df['tier'] = np.where(df['revenue'] > 10000, 'High', 'Low')

# Nested conditions (chained np.where)
df['tier'] = np.where(df['revenue'] > 10000, 'Platinum',
             np.where(df['revenue'] > 5000, 'Gold', 'Silver'))

# Replace outliers with cap
df['price'] = np.where(df['price'] > 500, 500, df['price'])
# Same as: np.clip(df['price'], 0, 500)
Key advantage over apply(): vectorized — operates on the entire array at C speed, 10-50x faster than apply(lambda).
Why asked: np.where is used in almost every real-world EDA and feature engineering pipeline.
Q12How do you detect and handle outliers in pandas?Must Know▶
Detection methods:
# IQR method
Q1 = df['price'].quantile(0.25)
Q3 = df['price'].quantile(0.75)
IQR = Q3 - Q1
lower = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR
upper = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR
outliers = df[(df['price'] < lower) | (df['price'] > upper)]

# Z-score method
from scipy import stats
z = np.abs(stats.zscore(df['price']))
outliers = df[z > 3]
Handling options:
• Cap/Winsorize: df['price'].clip(lower, upper)
• Remove: df[z < 3]
• Transform: np.log1p(df['price']) (log reduces skew)
• Keep + flag: df['is_outlier'] = z > 3
Why asked: Every EDA round asks this. Shows you handle real data, not just clean textbook data.
Q13What are common pandas performance anti-patterns?Often Missed▶
1. Iterating over rows with for loop (always replace with vectorized ops):
# BAD: O(n) Python loop
for i, row in df.iterrows():
    df.at[i, 'result'] = row['a'] + row['b']
# GOOD: vectorized
df['result'] = df['a'] + df['b']
2. Growing a DataFrame with append in a loop (creates new copy each time):
# BAD
for item in data:
    df = df.append(item, ignore_index=True)
# GOOD: collect list then concat once
rows = []
for item in data:
    rows.append(item)
df = pd.DataFrame(rows)
3. Not using category dtype for repetitive string columns
4. Using .apply() where vectorized ops exist
Why asked: Performance questions appear at companies processing millions of rows — Flipkart, Swiggy, PhonePe.
Q14How do you merge datasets with different granularity (many-to-one)?Often Missed▶
Many-to-one merges are common in real data: transactions table (many) + customers table (one).
# Orders (many) + Customers (one) -> each order gets customer info
merged = pd.merge(orders,           # many-side (left)
                  customers,        # one-side (right)
                  on='customer_id',
                  how='left',       # keep all orders
                  validate='m:1')   # validate the relationship!

# After merge: check for nulls (unmatched orders)
merged['customer_id'].isnull().sum()
validate parameter is critical: 'm:1' raises an error if the right table has duplicates on the key, helping catch data quality issues early.
Why asked: Common in product analytics. Shows you think about data relationships, not just syntax.
Q15How do you use pandas for time series analysis?Must Know▶
# Step 1: parse dates and set as index
df['date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['date'])
df = df.set_index('date').sort_index()

# Step 2: resample by period
monthly = df['revenue'].resample('M').sum()
weekly = df['revenue'].resample('W').mean()

# Step 3: rolling stats
df['rolling_avg'] = df['revenue'].rolling(7).mean()
df['rolling_std'] = df['revenue'].rolling(7).std()

# Step 4: lag features for ML
df['prev_week'] = df['revenue'].shift(7)
df['yoy_growth'] = df['revenue'] / df['revenue'].shift(365) - 1
Period aliases: D=day, W=week, M=month, Q=quarter, Y=year
Why asked: Critical for the Retail Sales Forecasting project. Interviewers will probe this if you mention Prophet or ARIMA.

📋 Basics Mastery — Senior DS Interview Bank

Every foundational concept a senior interviewer will test + deep-dive on your projects. Click any question to expand.

🐍 Python OOP
🗃️ SQL Fundamentals
📈 Statistics & ML Theory
📊 DAX & Power BI
📑 Excel & VBA
🚀 Your Projects
35 questions
01 What are the 4 pillars of OOP? Define each.
Basic
▶
Encapsulation — bundling data + methods together and hiding internal state (private variables with __). Inheritance — a child class inherits attributes/methods from a parent class. Polymorphism — same method name behaves differently across classes. Abstraction — hiding implementation details, exposing only what's necessary (abstract base classes).
💡 Why asked: This is the first OOP question in 90% of interviews. If you can't answer this, the rest of the interview is uphill.
02 What is a class vs an instance?
Basic
▶
A class is a blueprint/template. An instance is a concrete object created from that blueprint.
class Dog:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name

my_dog = Dog("Bruno")  # instance
Dog is the class. my_dog is an instance.
💡 Why asked: Tests if you understand the difference between type and object.
03 What does __init__ do? What is self?
Basic
▶
__init__ is the constructor — it runs automatically when an instance is created and initialises the object's attributes. self refers to the current instance of the class. It must be the first parameter of every instance method.
class Student:
    def __init__(self, name, grade):
        self.name = name    # instance attribute
        self.grade = grade
💡 Why asked: Every Python class interview starts here. Must be instant recall.
04 What is __str__ vs __repr__?
Must Know
▶
Both define string representations. __str__ is for human-readable output (used by print()). __repr__ is for developer/debugging output (used by the REPL, repr()). Rule: __repr__ should return a string that could recreate the object.
class Point:
    def __str__(self):
        return f"({self.x}, {self.y})"      # readable
    def __repr__(self):
        return f"Point({self.x}, {self.y})" # unambiguous
💡 Why asked: Often asked to test depth of Python knowledge beyond basics.
05 What is encapsulation? How do you make a variable private?
Basic
▶
Encapsulation = data + methods in one unit, with controlled access. In Python: _var = convention for protected (don't touch from outside), __var = name-mangled to _ClassName__var (harder to access accidentally).
class BankAccount:
    def __init__(self):
        self.__balance = 0   # private

    def deposit(self, amt):
        if amt > 0:
            self.__balance += amt

    def get_balance(self):
        return self.__balance
💡 Why asked: Direct test of OOP encapsulation principle.
06 What is inheritance? Single vs multiple?
Basic
▶
Inheritance: child class gets all attributes and methods of parent.
class Animal:
    def speak(self): return "..."

class Dog(Animal):       # single inheritance
    def speak(self): return "Woof"

class GuideDog(Dog, ServiceAnimal):  # multiple inheritance
    pass
Multiple inheritance can cause the Diamond Problem — Python resolves it with MRO (C3 linearization).
💡 Why asked: Tests core OOP. MRO follow-up is common.
07 What is super() and when do you use it?
Must Know
▶
super() gives you a proxy object that lets you call a parent class method from a child. Used in __init__ to avoid repeating parent initialisation code.
class Animal:
    def __init__(self, name):
        self.name = name

class Dog(Animal):
    def __init__(self, name, breed):
        super().__init__(name)   # calls Animal.__init__
        self.breed = breed
💡 Why asked: Without super(), you'd have to repeat parent __init__ code. Interviewers test if you know this.
08 Method overriding vs method overloading?
Must Know
▶
Overriding: child class redefines a method from parent (Python supports this natively).
Overloading: same method name with different signatures (Python does NOT support true overloading — you simulate it with *args/**kwargs or @singledispatch).
class Shape:
    def area(self): return 0

class Circle(Shape):
    def area(self): return 3.14 * self.r ** 2  # overriding
💡 Why asked: Python doesn't have overloading — this distinction trips many candidates.
09 What is polymorphism? Give a Python example.
Basic
▶
Same interface, different behaviour depending on object type.
class Cat:
    def speak(self): return "Meow"
class Dog:
    def speak(self): return "Woof"

def make_noise(animal):
    print(animal.speak())  # works for ANY class with speak()

make_noise(Cat())   # Meow
make_noise(Dog())   # Woof
This is duck typing — if it has a speak() method, it works.
💡 Why asked: Tests understanding of runtime polymorphism and duck typing.
10 What is abstraction? How do you create an abstract class?
Must Know
▶
Abstraction = hide implementation, expose only interface. In Python use ABC:
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod

class Shape(ABC):
    @abstractmethod
    def area(self): pass   # must be implemented by child

class Circle(Shape):
    def area(self): return 3.14 * self.r ** 2
You cannot instantiate Shape directly.
💡 Why asked: Abstract classes are used in production code (e.g., base model classes in ML pipelines).
11 @staticmethod vs @classmethod?
Must Know
▶
@staticmethod: no access to class or instance. Just a utility function scoped inside the class.
@classmethod: receives the class (cls) as first argument. Used as alternative constructors.
class Temperature:
    @staticmethod
    def celsius_to_fahrenheit(c):
        return c * 9/5 + 32

    @classmethod
    def from_fahrenheit(cls, f):
        return cls((f - 32) * 5/9)
💡 Why asked: Very common interview question. confusing staticmethod with classmethod is a red flag.
12 What is @property decorator?
Must Know
▶
Makes a method behave like an attribute (getter). Combined with @x.setter for validation.
class Circle:
    def __init__(self, radius):
        self._radius = radius

    @property
    def radius(self):
        return self._radius

    @radius.setter
    def radius(self, value):
        if value < 0:
            raise ValueError("Radius must be positive")
        self._radius = value

c = Circle(5)
c.radius = 10    # calls setter
print(c.radius)  # calls getter
💡 Why asked: Used everywhere in production Python. Shows you know Pythonic patterns.
13 What is __slots__?
Often Missed
▶
__slots__ restricts which instance attributes a class can have. Saves memory by avoiding per-instance __dict__.
class Point:
    __slots__ = ['x', 'y']   # only x and y allowed
    def __init__(self, x, y):
        self.x = x
        self.y = y
Use it when you're creating thousands of instances (e.g., rows in a dataset).
💡 Why asked: Memory optimisation technique. Shows you think about performance.
14 What is MRO (Method Resolution Order)?
Often Missed
▶
MRO defines which parent class method is called in multiple inheritance. Python uses C3 linearisation. Check it with ClassName.__mro__.
class A: pass
class B(A): pass
class C(A): pass
class D(B, C): pass
print(D.__mro__)
# [D, B, C, A, object]
Order: D → B → C → A. This resolves the Diamond Problem.
💡 Why asked: Advanced OOP — asked at senior/FAANG-style rounds to test depth.
15 What is a decorator? Write a simple timer decorator.
Must Know
▶
A decorator wraps a function to add behaviour without modifying it.
import time
import functools

def timer(func):
    @functools.wraps(func)
    def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
        start = time.time()
        result = func(*args, **kwargs)
        print(f"{func.__name__} took {time.time()-start:.4f}s")
        return result
    return wrapper

@timer
def train_model():
    time.sleep(1)

train_model()  # prints: train_model took 1.0001s
💡 Why asked: Decorators are used for logging, timing, caching. You WILL be asked to write one.
16 What is a generator? yield vs return?
Must Know
▶
A generator produces values one at a time (lazy evaluation) instead of building the whole list in memory.
def fibonacci():
    a, b = 0, 1
    while True:
        yield a        # pauses here, resumes next call
        a, b = b, a+b

gen = fibonacci()
print(next(gen))  # 0
print(next(gen))  # 1
return ends the function. yield suspends it — state is preserved between calls. Critical for large datasets.
💡 Why asked: Used in data pipelines. Essential for handling large files without memory explosion.
17 What is a lambda function?
Basic
▶
Anonymous single-expression function. Useful for short throwaway functions.
double = lambda x: x * 2
print(double(5))  # 10

# Common use: sort by key
students = [('Alice', 85), ('Bob', 92), ('Carol', 78)]
students.sort(key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
# [('Bob', 92), ('Alice', 85), ('Carol', 78)]
💡 Why asked: Tested constantly in pandas (apply, map) and general Python.
18 What is *args and **kwargs?
Basic
▶
*args collects extra positional arguments as a tuple. **kwargs collects extra keyword arguments as a dict.
def model_params(*args, **kwargs):
    print(args)    # ('XGBoost', 100)
    print(kwargs)  # {'learning_rate': 0.1, 'max_depth': 6}

model_params('XGBoost', 100, learning_rate=0.1, max_depth=6)
Used in wrapper functions, decorators, and flexible APIs.
💡 Why asked: Almost every Python interview includes this. It's about function flexibility.
19 Difference between 'is' and '=='?
Often Missed
▶
== checks value equality. is checks identity (same object in memory).
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = [1, 2, 3]
print(a == b)   # True  (same values)
print(a is b)   # False (different objects)

c = a
print(a is c)   # True  (same object)
Use is only for None checks: if x is None.
💡 Why asked: Classic Python gotcha. Interviewers love this because many candidates get it wrong.
20 Mutable vs immutable types in Python?
Basic
▶
Immutable (cannot be changed after creation): int, float, str, tuple, frozenset.
Mutable (can be changed): list, dict, set.
s = "hello"
s[0] = "H"   # TypeError! strings are immutable

lst = [1, 2, 3]
lst[0] = 99   # works fine
Matters for default arguments: never use mutable defaults (def f(x=[]): is a bug).
💡 Why asked: Understanding mutability is essential for avoiding subtle bugs in ML code.
21 Shallow copy vs deep copy?
Must Know
▶
Shallow copy: creates new outer object but nested objects are still shared references.
Deep copy: recursively copies everything — fully independent.
import copy
original = [[1, 2], [3, 4]]
shallow = copy.copy(original)
deep    = copy.deepcopy(original)

original[0][0] = 99
print(shallow[0][0])  # 99  ← shared reference!
print(deep[0][0])     # 1   ← fully independent
💡 Why asked: Common source of bugs in ML pipelines when sharing data structures.
22 What is a context manager (with statement)?
Must Know
▶
Context managers handle setup and teardown automatically. Implement with __enter__ / __exit__ or @contextmanager.
class Timer:
    def __enter__(self):
        self.start = time.time()
        return self
    def __exit__(self, *args):
        print(f"Elapsed: {time.time()-self.start:.2f}s")

with Timer():
    train_model()   # auto-prints elapsed on exit
Files, database connections, and ML experiment trackers use this pattern.
💡 Why asked: Used constantly with file I/O, database connections, and MLflow.
23 List comprehension vs generator expression?
Must Know
▶
List comprehension: creates the full list immediately — uses memory.
Generator expression: lazy — produces one value at a time.
squares_list = [x**2 for x in range(1_000_000)]   # 8 MB in memory
squares_gen  = (x**2 for x in range(1_000_000))    # almost 0 memory

# Use generator when you only iterate once
total = sum(x**2 for x in range(1_000_000))
💡 Why asked: Critical for large-scale data processing. Interviewers test memory awareness.
24 What is a dict comprehension?
Basic
▶
Creates a dictionary from an iterable in one line.
prices = {'apple': 1.2, 'banana': 0.5, 'cherry': 3.0}

# Filter: only items over 1.0
expensive = {k: v for k, v in prices.items() if v > 1.0}
# {'apple': 1.2, 'cherry': 3.0}

# Transform keys
upper_prices = {k.upper(): v for k, v in prices.items()}
💡 Why asked: Pandas users need this for quickly transforming columns.
25 Exception handling: try/except/finally/raise?
Basic
▶ 
def divide(a, b):
    try:
        result = a / b
    except ZeroDivisionError:
        print("Cannot divide by zero")
        return None
    except TypeError as e:
        raise ValueError(f"Bad input: {e}")  # re-raise as different type
    else:
        return result         # runs only if no exception
    finally:
        print("Cleanup")      # runs ALWAYS (even on exception)
💡 Why asked: Production ML code must handle exceptions (missing data, API failures). Must know.
26 List vs tuple vs set vs dict?
Basic
▶
list: ordered, mutable, allows duplicates. Use for sequences.
tuple: ordered, immutable. Use for fixed data (coordinates, DB rows).
set: unordered, unique items only, O(1) lookup. Use for deduplication.
dict: key-value pairs, ordered (Python 3.7+), O(1) lookup by key.
# Deduplication
unique_ids = set([1, 2, 2, 3, 3])  # {1, 2, 3}

# Fast lookup
age_map = {'Alice': 25, 'Bob': 30}  # O(1)
💡 Why asked: The most fundamental Python data structures question.
27 What is zip() and enumerate()?
Basic
▶
zip() pairs elements from multiple iterables. enumerate() adds an index counter.
names = ['Alice', 'Bob', 'Carol']
scores = [85, 92, 78]

# zip: iterate together
for name, score in zip(names, scores):
    print(f"{name}: {score}")

# enumerate: index + value
for i, name in enumerate(names, start=1):
    print(f"{i}. {name}")
💡 Why asked: Used constantly in data processing loops. Should be instant recall.
28 map(), filter(), reduce()?
Must Know
▶
map(func, iterable): apply function to each element.
filter(func, iterable): keep elements where func returns True.
reduce(func, iterable): cumulatively apply function.
from functools import reduce

nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
doubled   = list(map(lambda x: x*2, nums))       # [2,4,6,8,10]
evens     = list(filter(lambda x: x%2==0, nums)) # [2,4]
total     = reduce(lambda x,y: x+y, nums)        # 15
Often replaced by list comprehensions in modern Python, but still tested.
💡 Why asked: Functional programming fundamentals. Shows you know Python's functional tools.
29 What is the GIL (Global Interpreter Lock)?
Often Missed
▶
The GIL is a mutex in CPython that prevents multiple threads from executing Python bytecode simultaneously. Impact: threading doesn't give true parallelism for CPU-bound tasks in Python.
Solutions: Use multiprocessing (bypasses GIL) for CPU-bound, or asyncio for I/O-bound tasks.
from multiprocessing import Pool
with Pool(4) as p:
    results = p.map(train_model, dataset_chunks)  # true parallelism
💡 Why asked: Asked at senior/ML engineer roles. Shows you understand Python's concurrency limits.
30 What is if __name__ == '__main__'?
Basic
▶
__name__ is '__main__' when a file is run directly, but equals the module name when it's imported. This block ensures code only runs when the file is the entry point, not when imported as a module.
def train():
    print("Training model...")

if __name__ == '__main__':
    train()   # only runs when you execute: python model.py
              # NOT when you do: import model
💡 Why asked: Every Python script should have this. Shows you write modular, importable code.
31 What is a closure?
Must Know
▶
A closure is a function that remembers variables from its enclosing scope even after that scope has finished executing.
def make_multiplier(factor):
    def multiply(x):
        return x * factor   # 'factor' is closed over
    return multiply

double = make_multiplier(2)
triple = make_multiplier(3)
print(double(5))  # 10
print(triple(5))  # 15
Decorators are built using closures.
💡 Why asked: Shows understanding of Python's scoping rules and how decorators work internally.
32 What is duck typing?
Must Know
▶
'If it walks like a duck and quacks like a duck, it's a duck.' Python doesn't check types at compile time — it only cares if an object has the method/attribute you're calling.
def process(data):
    for item in data:   # works with list, tuple, generator, set...
        print(item)

process([1, 2, 3])    # list
process((1, 2, 3))    # tuple
process({1, 2, 3})    # set
💡 Why asked: Core Python philosophy. Relates to polymorphism without inheritance.
33 What is @dataclass?
Must Know
▶
@dataclass auto-generates __init__, __repr__, __eq__ etc. for classes that mainly hold data.
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class ModelConfig:
    learning_rate: float = 0.01
    max_depth: int = 6
    n_estimators: int = 100

cfg = ModelConfig(learning_rate=0.05)
print(cfg)  # ModelConfig(learning_rate=0.05, max_depth=6, n_estimators=100)
💡 Why asked: Modern Python. Used for config classes in ML projects. Shows you're up to date.
34 What are dunder/magic methods? Name 5.
Must Know
▶
Dunder = double underscore. Special methods Python calls automatically:
__init__ — constructor
__str__ — print(obj)
__len__ — len(obj)
__add__ — obj1 + obj2 (operator overloading)
__getitem__ — obj[key]
class Dataset:
    def __len__(self): return len(self.data)
    def __getitem__(self, idx): return self.data[idx]
    def __add__(self, other): return Dataset(self.data + other.data)
💡 Why asked: Understanding dunders = understanding how Python objects work at a deep level.
35 How does Python's garbage collection work?
Often Missed
▶
Python uses reference counting as primary GC: when an object's reference count drops to 0, it's deallocated. Cyclic garbage collector handles circular references.
import gc
a = []
b = [a]
a.append(b)   # circular reference
del a, b
gc.collect()  # force-collect circular refs
Use del to explicitly remove references when working with large ML models/data.
💡 Why asked: Shows you understand Python memory management — relevant for ML with large models.
30 questions
01 Difference between WHERE and HAVING?
Basic
▶
WHERE filters rows before grouping (works on raw columns).
HAVING filters groups after GROUP BY (works on aggregates).
-- WRONG: can't use aggregate in WHERE
SELECT dept, AVG(salary) FROM employees
WHERE AVG(salary) > 80000  -- Error!
GROUP BY dept;

-- CORRECT: use HAVING for aggregates
SELECT dept, AVG(salary) FROM employees
GROUP BY dept
HAVING AVG(salary) > 80000;
💡 Why asked: Most common SQL basics question. Getting this wrong immediately fails the interview.
02 INNER JOIN vs LEFT JOIN vs RIGHT JOIN vs FULL OUTER JOIN?
Basic
▶
INNER JOIN: only matching rows in both tables.
LEFT JOIN: all rows from left table + matching from right (NULLs for no match).
RIGHT JOIN: all rows from right + matching from left.
FULL OUTER JOIN: all rows from both, NULLs for non-matches.
-- Find customers with no orders (LEFT JOIN + IS NULL)
SELECT c.name FROM customers c
LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.id = o.customer_id
WHERE o.id IS NULL;
💡 Why asked: 90% of SQL interview questions involve JOINs. Must know all types cold.
03 What is a subquery? Correlated vs non-correlated?
Must Know
▶
Non-correlated: inner query runs once, independently.
Correlated: inner query references outer query and runs once PER ROW.
-- Non-correlated (runs once)
SELECT * FROM employees
WHERE salary > (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees);

-- Correlated (runs per row, slower)
SELECT name, salary FROM employees e
WHERE salary > (
  SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees
  WHERE dept_id = e.dept_id  -- references outer query
);
💡 Why asked: Correlated subqueries are slow but show up in interviews constantly.
04 What is a CTE? Why use it over a subquery?
Must Know
▶
A CTE (Common Table Expression) is a named temporary result set defined with WITH. Benefits: readable, reusable within the same query, recursive support.
WITH dept_avg AS (
  SELECT dept_id, AVG(salary) AS avg_sal
  FROM employees GROUP BY dept_id
)
SELECT e.name, e.salary, d.avg_sal
FROM employees e
JOIN dept_avg d ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
WHERE e.salary > d.avg_sal;
💡 Why asked: CTEs = cleaner code + recursive queries. Shows SQL maturity.
05 What is a window function? Name 5 with use cases.
Must Know
▶
Window functions compute across a set of rows related to the current row without collapsing them (unlike GROUP BY).
ROW_NUMBER(): unique sequential rank.
RANK(): rank with gaps for ties.
DENSE_RANK(): rank without gaps.
LAG()/LEAD(): access previous/next row.
SUM() OVER(): running total.
SELECT name, salary,
  RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary DESC) AS dept_rank,
  LAG(salary) OVER (ORDER BY hire_date) AS prev_salary
FROM employees;
💡 Why asked: Window functions are tested at EVERY senior DA/DS interview. Non-negotiable.
06 RANK() vs DENSE_RANK() vs ROW_NUMBER() — exact difference?
Must Know
▶
Given salaries: 100, 100, 80:
ROW_NUMBER(): 1, 2, 3 (always unique)
RANK(): 1, 1, 3 (gaps after ties)
DENSE_RANK(): 1, 1, 2 (no gaps)
SELECT name, salary,
  ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rn,
  RANK()       OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rnk,
  DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS drnk
FROM employees;
Use DENSE_RANK for "Nth highest salary" problems.
💡 Why asked: This exact comparison is asked in 70% of DA SQL interviews.
07 What is COALESCE()? When do you use it?
Basic
▶
COALESCE(val1, val2, ...) returns the first non-NULL value. Used to handle NULLs.
SELECT
  name,
  COALESCE(phone, email, 'No contact') AS contact,
  COALESCE(salary, 0) AS salary  -- replace NULL with 0
FROM employees;
Also used in LEFT JOIN to handle unmatched rows: COALESCE(orders.amount, 0).
💡 Why asked: NULL handling is critical in real data. COALESCE appears in almost every real-world query.
08 DELETE vs TRUNCATE vs DROP?
Basic
▶
DELETE: removes specific rows (can use WHERE), logged, can be rolled back.
TRUNCATE: removes ALL rows instantly, minimal logging, cannot use WHERE, resets identity.
DROP: removes the entire table structure + data permanently.
DELETE FROM orders WHERE status = 'cancelled';  -- specific rows
TRUNCATE TABLE temp_staging;   -- all rows, fast, reset auto-increment
DROP TABLE old_backup;         -- table gone permanently
💡 Why asked: Common in interviews and real operations. Getting DROP and DELETE confused is risky.
09 What is normalization? 1NF, 2NF, 3NF?
Must Know
▶
Normalization = organizing data to reduce redundancy.
1NF: Atomic values (no arrays/multi-values in a cell).
2NF: 1NF + no partial dependency (non-key column depends on WHOLE primary key).
3NF: 2NF + no transitive dependency (non-key column depends only on primary key, not on other non-key columns).
Example of 3NF violation: storing city name in Orders table when city already tied to customer_id in Customers.
💡 Why asked: Database design fundamentals. Asked in every SQL interview at 12+ LPA.
10 What is an index? When to create one?
Must Know
▶
An index is a data structure (usually B-tree) that speeds up SELECT queries. Cost: slower INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE.
Create index when: columns in WHERE/JOIN/ORDER BY, high-cardinality columns, large tables.
Avoid index when: small tables, columns rarely queried, heavy write operations.
CREATE INDEX idx_cust_city ON customers(city);
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_date ON orders(order_date);
💡 Why asked: Performance is asked at senior levels. You need to know trade-offs.
11 Primary key vs foreign key vs unique key?
Basic
▶
Primary key: uniquely identifies each row, NOT NULL, only one per table.
Foreign key: references primary key in another table, enforces referential integrity.
Unique key: all values must be unique (unlike PK, can have ONE NULL, can have multiple unique keys).
CREATE TABLE orders (
  id INT PRIMARY KEY,
  customer_id INT REFERENCES customers(id),  -- foreign key
  email VARCHAR(100) UNIQUE                   -- unique key
);
💡 Why asked: Fundamental database design. Cannot miss this.
12 UNION vs UNION ALL?
Basic
▶
UNION: combines results + removes duplicates (slower, requires deduplication).
UNION ALL: combines results keeping ALL rows including duplicates (faster).
-- Report: all customers from Mumbai OR Delhi
SELECT name, city FROM customers WHERE city = 'Mumbai'
UNION ALL
SELECT name, city FROM customers WHERE city = 'Delhi';

-- Use UNION when you need deduplicated results
-- Use UNION ALL when you know there are no duplicates (faster)
💡 Why asked: Appears in reporting/dashboard queries constantly.
13 What is SQL execution order?
Must Know
▶
SQL queries execute in this order (NOT the order you write them):
1. FROM + JOIN
2. WHERE
3. GROUP BY
4. HAVING
5. SELECT
6. DISTINCT
7. ORDER BY
8. LIMIT
This is why you can't use SELECT aliases in WHERE (alias is defined in step 5, WHERE runs in step 2).
💡 Why asked: Understanding execution order explains every 'why doesn't this work?' SQL question.
14 How do you find and remove duplicates?
Must Know
▶ 
-- Find duplicates
SELECT email, COUNT(*)
FROM customers GROUP BY email HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;

-- Delete duplicates, keep only one (using ROW_NUMBER)
DELETE FROM customers
WHERE id NOT IN (
  SELECT MIN(id) FROM customers GROUP BY email
);

-- Or with CTE
WITH dupes AS (
  SELECT id, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY email ORDER BY id) AS rn
  FROM customers
)
DELETE FROM dupes WHERE rn > 1;
💡 Why asked: Comes up in every data cleaning interview round.
15 What is a self JOIN?
Must Know
▶
A self JOIN joins a table to itself. Classic use case: find employees who earn more than their manager.
SELECT e.name AS employee, e.salary,
  m.name AS manager, m.salary AS manager_salary
FROM employees e
JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id
WHERE e.salary > m.salary;
Also used for hierarchy queries (reporting chains, org charts).
💡 Why asked: Self JOIN is a top-5 most asked SQL interview question.
16 NULL handling — IS NULL vs = NULL?
Basic
▶
NULL = NULL is always UNKNOWN (not TRUE). You must use IS NULL.
-- Wrong!
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE manager_id = NULL;  -- returns 0 rows

-- Correct
SELECT * FROM employees WHERE manager_id IS NULL;

-- COALESCE: replace NULL
SELECT COALESCE(salary, 0) FROM employees;

-- NULLIF: returns NULL if values are equal
SELECT NULLIF(discount, 0) FROM orders;  -- NULL instead of 0
💡 Why asked: NULL handling trips up 40% of candidates. Interviewers know this.
17 What is ACID? What does each letter mean?
Must Know
▶
ACID = properties that guarantee database transaction reliability:
Atomicity — all operations in a transaction succeed or all fail (no partial commits).
Consistency — database moves from one valid state to another.
Isolation — concurrent transactions don't interfere with each other.
Durability — committed transactions persist even after crashes (written to disk).
💡 Why asked: Asked at every company that uses production databases. Shows you understand reliability.
18 What is EXISTS vs IN?
Must Know
▶
Both check for existence, but differ in performance.
IN: evaluates subquery first, creates a list, checks membership. Efficient for small result sets.
EXISTS: short-circuits on first match. Efficient when subquery returns many rows.
-- EXISTS: stops as soon as one match is found
SELECT name FROM customers c
WHERE EXISTS (
  SELECT 1 FROM orders o WHERE o.customer_id = c.id
);

-- IN: loads entire subquery result
SELECT name FROM customers WHERE id IN (SELECT customer_id FROM orders);
💡 Why asked: Performance question. Shows you think beyond correctness to efficiency.
19 What is LAG() and LEAD()?
Must Know
▶
Window functions that access the previous or next row's value without a self JOIN.
SELECT
  order_date,
  amount,
  LAG(amount, 1) OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS prev_day_amount,
  amount - LAG(amount, 1) OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS day_over_day_change
FROM orders;

-- LEAD: look ahead
LEAD(order_date, 1) OVER (ORDER BY order_date) AS next_order_date
Critical for time-series analysis and cohort calculations.
💡 Why asked: LAG/LEAD appears in almost every advanced SQL test for DA roles.
20 How do you calculate a running total?
Must Know
▶ 
SELECT
  order_date,
  amount,
  SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY order_date
    ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
  ) AS running_total
FROM orders;

-- Shorthand (same result):
SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY order_date)
Running totals power cumulative revenue charts, burn-rate dashboards, etc.
💡 Why asked: Running total is asked in most DA/DS SQL tests — usually part of a dashboard scenario.
21 How do you find the Nth highest salary?
Must Know
▶ 
-- Using DENSE_RANK (handles ties correctly)
SELECT salary FROM (
  SELECT salary,
    DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY salary DESC) AS rnk
  FROM employees
) ranked
WHERE rnk = 2;  -- change for Nth

-- Alternative: OFFSET/LIMIT (no ties handling)
SELECT DISTINCT salary FROM employees
ORDER BY salary DESC
LIMIT 1 OFFSET 1;  -- 2nd highest
💡 Why asked: Classic interview question. Asked at 80% of SQL screening rounds.
22 What is a view? Limitations?
Must Know
▶
A view is a saved SELECT query treated like a virtual table. Benefits: abstraction, security (hide columns), simplify complex queries. Limitations: most views are read-only; you can't always INSERT/UPDATE. Doesn't store data (queries underlying tables every time).
CREATE VIEW premium_customers AS
SELECT * FROM customers WHERE segment = 'Premium';

SELECT * FROM premium_customers;  -- easy access
💡 Why asked: Shows you understand database abstraction layers.
23 What is a transaction?
Basic
▶
A transaction is a unit of work that must either fully complete or fully rollback.
BEGIN TRANSACTION;
  UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 1000 WHERE id = 1;  -- debit
  UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance + 1000 WHERE id = 2;  -- credit
COMMIT;   -- make permanent

-- If something fails:
ROLLBACK;  -- undo both operations
Essential for financial and e-commerce applications.
💡 Why asked: Fundamental database concept. Asked when discussing data integrity.
24 What is a GROUP_CONCAT / STRING_AGG?
Often Missed
▶
Aggregates multiple row values into a single delimited string.
-- SQLite / MySQL
SELECT customer_id,
  GROUP_CONCAT(product_name, ', ') AS products_bought
FROM orders
GROUP BY customer_id;
-- Result: 1 | 'Laptop, Headphones, Keyboard'

-- SQL Server / PostgreSQL
STRING_AGG(product_name, ', ') AS products_bought
💡 Why asked: Used in reporting to display related items per customer/order.
25 How do you handle slow query performance?
Must Know
▶
1. Check the execution plan: EXPLAIN ANALYZE — find full table scans.
2. Add missing indexes on WHERE/JOIN/ORDER BY columns.
3. Avoid SELECT * — select only needed columns.
4. Avoid functions on indexed columns in WHERE (prevents index use).
5. Use CTEs/subqueries wisely — sometimes rewriting eliminates redundant scans.
6. Partition large tables by date.
7. Avoid correlated subqueries — replace with JOINs or window functions.
💡 Why asked: Senior DA/DS roles expect you to think about query performance, not just correctness.
26 What is partitioning?
Often Missed
▶
Partitioning splits a large table into smaller, more manageable pieces based on a column value (usually date or region). Queries that filter on the partition key only scan relevant partitions.
CREATE TABLE orders_2024_q1
PARTITION OF orders
FOR VALUES FROM ('2024-01-01') TO ('2024-04-01');
Used in data warehouses (BigQuery, Snowflake) to massively speed up time-range queries.
💡 Why asked: Data engineering knowledge tested at mid-senior DA roles.
27 CHAR vs VARCHAR vs TEXT?
Basic
▶
CHAR(n): fixed-length, always uses n bytes. Faster for fixed-size data (country codes).
VARCHAR(n): variable-length, uses only what's needed. Best for most strings.
TEXT: unlimited length. Slower, no indexing in some DBs. Use for descriptions/comments.
country_code CHAR(2),     -- always 2 chars: 'IN', 'US'
email VARCHAR(254),       -- variable, max 254
product_desc TEXT         -- long text, no length limit
💡 Why asked: Database design basics. Choosing the wrong type affects storage and performance.
28 What is a materialized view vs a regular view?
Often Missed
▶
Regular view: no data stored, runs underlying query every time.
Materialized view: physically stores the query result. Faster reads, but needs refreshing when data changes.
-- Materialized view (Postgres/Snowflake)
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW monthly_revenue AS
SELECT MONTH(order_date), SUM(amount)
FROM orders GROUP BY MONTH(order_date);

REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW monthly_revenue;  -- update it
💡 Why asked: Snowflake and BigQuery interview question. Shows data warehousing knowledge.
29 What is a cross join?
Basic
▶
A cross join returns the Cartesian product — every row from table A paired with every row from table B. If A has 100 rows and B has 50, result has 5,000 rows.
-- All possible date-product combinations (for filling gaps)
SELECT d.date, p.product_id
FROM dates d
CROSS JOIN products p;
Used to generate all combinations (e.g., complete date ranges for a time series).
💡 Why asked: Understanding cross joins shows you know all JOIN types.
30 How do you pivot data in SQL?
Often Missed
▶ 
-- CASE WHEN pivot (works in all SQL dialects)
SELECT
  customer_id,
  SUM(CASE WHEN category='Electronics' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS electronics,
  SUM(CASE WHEN category='Furniture' THEN amount ELSE 0 END)   AS furniture,
  SUM(CASE WHEN category='Education' THEN amount ELSE 0 END)   AS education
FROM orders o JOIN products p ON o.product_id = p.id
GROUP BY customer_id;
💡 Why asked: Pivoting is extremely common in reporting. Most DA candidates forget CASE WHEN trick.
20 questions
01 What is a p-value? What does p < 0.05 mean?
Must Know
▶
The p-value is the probability of observing your results (or more extreme) assuming the null hypothesis is true. p < 0.05 means: if there were truly no effect, you'd see results this extreme less than 5% of the time — so you reject the null. Misconception: p-value is NOT the probability that the null is true. It is NOT the probability your result is a fluke.
💡 Why asked: P-value is asked in EVERY statistics interview. Getting the definition wrong is an instant red flag.
02 Type I error vs Type II error?
Must Know
▶
Type I (False Positive): Reject H₀ when it's actually true. α = significance level = probability of Type I error. (Crying wolf — alarm when no fire).
Type II (False Negative): Fail to reject H₀ when it's actually false. β = probability of Type II error. (Missing the fire).
Power = 1 - β = probability of detecting a real effect.
Tradeoff: reducing α increases β. In churn models, false negatives (missing churners) are usually more costly.
💡 Why asked: Directly relevant to model evaluation. Misclassification cost analysis uses this framework.
03 What is a confidence interval?
Must Know
▶
A 95% CI means: if you repeated the experiment 100 times, 95 of those intervals would contain the true population parameter. It is NOT a 95% probability the true value is in THIS interval (it either is or it isn't).
Mean = 50, 95% CI = [45, 55]
Interpretation: We're 95% confident the true population mean
falls between 45 and 55.
Wider CI = more uncertainty = need more data.
💡 Why asked: CI interpretation is a classic interview trap. Most candidates state it incorrectly.
04 What is the Central Limit Theorem?
Must Know
▶
The CLT states: regardless of the population's distribution, the sampling distribution of the sample mean approaches a normal distribution as sample size increases (n ≥ 30 is the rule of thumb). This is why so many statistical tests assume normality even when underlying data isn't normal. Foundation of A/B testing.
💡 Why asked: Foundation of all frequentist statistics and A/B testing.
05 Mean vs median vs mode — when to use which?
Basic
▶
Mean: use for symmetric distributions (no extreme outliers).
Median: use for skewed distributions (income, house prices, order values). Robust to outliers.
Mode: categorical data, most frequent value.
Salaries: [30k, 35k, 40k, 45k, 500k]
Mean = 130k  ← distorted by outlier
Median = 40k ← better represents typical salary
💡 Why asked: This comes up in every EDA discussion and data interpretation question.
06 Standard deviation vs variance?
Basic
▶
Variance: average of squared deviations from mean (units²). Standard deviation = √variance (same units as data — more interpretable).
data = [2, 4, 4, 4, 5, 5, 7, 9]
mean = 5
variance  = 4.0  (units²)
std_dev   = 2.0  (same units)

# In practice:
import numpy as np
np.std(data)     # 2.0
np.var(data)     # 4.0
💡 Why asked: Foundational stats. If you're working in ML, you must know these cold.
07 What is skewness? Positive vs negative?
Must Know
▶
Skewness measures asymmetry of distribution.
Positive skew (right-skewed): long tail on right, mean > median. Example: income, order values.
Negative skew (left-skewed): long tail on left, mean < median. Example: exam scores (many high scores, few low).
Rule of thumb: if |skewness| > 1, consider log transformation or use median instead of mean.
💡 Why asked: EDA question. Tells interviewers you know how to handle real-world messy data.
08 What is the bias-variance tradeoff?
Must Know
▶
Bias: error from wrong assumptions (underfitting). High bias → model too simple → misses patterns.
Variance: error from sensitivity to training data (overfitting). High variance → model memorises noise.
Total Error = Bias² + Variance + Irreducible Noise
You can't minimize both simultaneously. A complex model (deep tree) has low bias but high variance. A simple model (linear) has high bias but low variance. Regularization, ensembles, and cross-validation manage this tradeoff.
💡 Why asked: THE most important ML theory question. Asked at every 12+ LPA interview.
09 What is overfitting? How do you detect and prevent it?
Must Know
▶
Overfitting: model learns training data too well (memorises noise) — performs great on train, poorly on test.
Detect: large gap between train score and validation score.
Prevent: more training data, cross-validation, regularization (L1/L2), dropout (neural nets), pruning (trees), early stopping, feature selection, reduce model complexity.
💡 Why asked: Every ML interview includes this. You need practical prevention strategies, not just theory.
10 What is cross-validation? K-fold?
Must Know
▶
Cross-validation estimates how well a model generalises by training and testing on different subsets. K-fold: split data into K equal folds; train on K-1, test on 1, rotate K times, average results.
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score
scores = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=5, scoring='roc_auc')
print(f"AUC: {scores.mean():.3f} ± {scores.std():.3f}")
5-fold is standard. For imbalanced data, use Stratified K-fold.
💡 Why asked: Cross-validation is how you properly evaluate ML models. Not using it is a red flag.
11 What is A/B testing? How do you determine sample size?
Must Know
▶
A/B testing = controlled experiment comparing two versions to measure which performs better.
Sample size depends on: baseline conversion rate, minimum detectable effect (MDE), significance level (α), power (1-β).
from statsmodels.stats.power import NormalIndPower
analysis = NormalIndPower()
n = analysis.solve_power(
    effect_size=0.2,   # Cohen's d
    alpha=0.05,
    power=0.8
)
Always pre-register your hypothesis. Don't peek at results early (p-hacking).
💡 Why asked: A/B testing is in EVERY product analytics and growth role interview.
12 Precision vs recall vs F1 score?
Must Know
▶
Precision = TP/(TP+FP): of all predicted positives, how many are correct? (Minimise false positives)
Recall = TP/(TP+FN): of all actual positives, how many did we catch? (Minimise false negatives)
F1 = harmonic mean of precision and recall.
Use recall when false negatives are costly (cancer detection, fraud, churn). Use precision when false positives are costly (spam filter). Use F1 when both matter equally.
💡 Why asked: Directly related to your churn project (you optimised for 72% recall). Must explain why.
13 What is AUC-ROC?
Must Know
▶
ROC curve: plots True Positive Rate vs False Positive Rate at every threshold.
AUC (Area Under Curve): probability that the model ranks a random positive example higher than a random negative. Interpretation: AUC = 0.5 → random guess. AUC = 1.0 → perfect. AUC = 0.84 → good for imbalanced datasets.
AUC is threshold-independent — evaluates overall model quality, not one threshold.
💡 Why asked: You have AUC 0.84 on your resume. You MUST explain this clearly when asked.
14 What is RMSE vs MAE?
Must Know
▶
MAE (Mean Absolute Error): average of |predicted - actual|. Intuitive, robust to outliers.
RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error): √(average of squared errors). Penalises large errors more heavily.
# Your retail project: RMSE = $0.10
# This means: on average, your prediction is off by $0.10

import numpy as np
mae  = np.mean(np.abs(y_pred - y_true))
rmse = np.sqrt(np.mean((y_pred - y_true)**2))
Use RMSE when large errors are especially bad. Use MAE for more interpretable error.
💡 Why asked: You have RMSE $0.10 and R²=0.9436 on your resume. Interviewers WILL ask what these mean.
15 What is R²?
Must Know
▶
R² (coefficient of determination) = proportion of variance in the dependent variable explained by the model.
R² = 1 − (SS_res / SS_tot)
Range: 0 to 1 (can be negative for very bad models).
R² = 0.9436: your model explains 94.36% of the variance in sales revenue. Very strong for retail data.
💡 Why asked: Your retail project has R²=0.9436. Explain this to a non-technical interviewer.
16 Pearson vs Spearman correlation?
Must Know
▶
Pearson: measures linear relationship. Assumes normality, sensitive to outliers.
Spearman: measures monotonic relationship (ranks). Works with non-normal data, robust to outliers.
import scipy.stats as stats
r_pearson,  p = stats.pearsonr(x, y)
r_spearman, p = stats.spearmanr(x, y)
Use Spearman when data is skewed or contains outliers. For income vs spending data, Spearman is usually better.
💡 Why asked: Correlation questions appear in every EDA round.
17 What is multicollinearity?
Must Know
▶
Multicollinearity = two or more features are highly correlated with each other. Problem: inflates standard errors of coefficients, makes them unstable and uninterpretable (coefficient signs can flip).
Detection: VIF (Variance Inflation Factor) > 10 indicates severe multicollinearity.
Solutions: remove one of the correlated features, use PCA/dimensionality reduction, use Ridge regression (handles it via regularization).
💡 Why asked: Critical for linear models and feature engineering. Shows you understand model assumptions.
18 What is regularization (L1/L2)?
Must Know
▶
Regularization adds a penalty term to the loss function to prevent overfitting.
L1 (Lasso): penalty = λΣ|w|. Drives some weights to EXACTLY zero → feature selection.
L2 (Ridge): penalty = λΣw². Shrinks weights toward zero but rarely exactly zero → keeps all features but small.
Elastic Net: combination of L1 + L2.
from sklearn.linear_model import Lasso, Ridge
lasso = Lasso(alpha=0.01)    # L1: sparse model
ridge = Ridge(alpha=1.0)     # L2: small weights
💡 Why asked: Regularization is asked constantly in ML theory rounds at 12+ LPA.
19 What is Bayes' theorem?
Must Know
▶
P(A|B) = P(B|A) × P(A) / P(B)
In plain English: probability of A given B = (how likely B is if A is true) × (prior probability of A) / (overall probability of B).
Example: If 1% of customers churn (prior), and your model flags a customer, what's the actual probability they churn? Depends on precision of the model.
Bayes is the foundation of Bayesian ML, spam filters, and probabilistic models.
💡 Why asked: Shows statistical depth. Often asked as a follow-up to classification model discussions.
20 What is log transformation and when to use it?
Must Know
▶
Log transformation converts skewed distributions to approximately normal. Use when:
- Data spans many orders of magnitude (income: 20k to 10M)
- Distribution is right-skewed
- You need multiplicative relationships to become additive
import numpy as np
df['log_income'] = np.log1p(df['income'])  # log(1+x) handles zeros
Used in your retail project — sales data is almost always right-skewed. Log-transforming target variable often improves regression performance.
💡 Why asked: Feature engineering fundamental. Appear in EDA and preprocessing discussions.
15 questions
01 What is DAX? How is it different from SQL?
Basic
▶
DAX (Data Analysis Expressions) is the formula language in Power BI, Excel Power Pivot, and SSAS. Key differences from SQL:
SQL operates on tables row by row. DAX operates in a context-aware filter model.
DAX has two contexts: row context (current row in a table) and filter context (active filters from slicers/visuals).
