SQL retrieves data. DAX calculates measures that respond to user interactions dynamically.
💡 Why asked: First question in every Power BI interview. You listed Power BI + DAX on your resume — must nail this.
02 Measure vs calculated column?
Must Know
▶
Calculated column: computed at data refresh time, stored in the model, uses row context. Good for: fixed attributes per row.
Measure: computed dynamically at query time based on filter context. NOT stored. Good for: aggregations that change based on slicers.
// Calculated Column (stored, row context)
Profit = Sales[Revenue] - Sales[Cost]

// Measure (dynamic, filter context)
Total Revenue = SUM(Sales[Revenue])
Avg Order = AVERAGE(Sales[Amount])
💡 Why asked: Most important conceptual distinction in Power BI. Wrong answer = failed interview.
03 What is CALCULATE()? Give an example.
Must Know
▶
CALCULATE(expression, filter1, filter2, ...) evaluates an expression in a modified filter context. It's the most powerful DAX function — lets you override slicer/report filters.
// Revenue for Premium customers only
Premium Revenue =
CALCULATE(
    SUM(Orders[Amount]),
    Customers[Segment] = "Premium"
)

// Revenue for same period last year
LY Revenue =
CALCULATE(
    [Total Revenue],
    SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR(Dates[Date])
)
💡 Why asked: CALCULATE is the backbone of DAX. If you only know one DAX function, it's this one.
04 Filter context vs row context?
Must Know
▶
Row context: the current row being processed. Created by calculated columns, iterators (SUMX, AVERAGEX), and EARLIER().
Filter context: the set of active filters. Created by visuals, slicers, report filters, and CALCULATE(). Measures always run in filter context.
// Row context: evaluates per row
Profit Margin = Sales[Revenue] - Sales[Cost]  // row context

// Filter context: changes when user clicks a slicer
Total Sales = SUM(Sales[Revenue])  // filter context
💡 Why asked: The hardest concept in DAX. If you can explain this, you stand out.
05 SUM vs SUMX?
Must Know
▶
SUM(column): adds all values in a column. Simple, fast.
SUMX(table, expression): iterates row by row, evaluates expression, then sums. Row-by-row calculation.
// SUM: just adds the column
Total Revenue = SUM(Sales[Amount])

// SUMX: calculate per row first, then sum
Total Profit = SUMX(
    Sales,
    Sales[Quantity] * Sales[UnitPrice] - Sales[Cost]
)
Use SUMX when you need per-row calculation before aggregating.
💡 Why asked: SUM vs SUMX is a very common DAX interview question.
06 ALL() vs ALLEXCEPT()?
Must Know
▶
ALL(table/column): removes ALL filters from a table or column. Used to calculate percentage of total.
ALLEXCEPT(table, col1, col2): removes all filters EXCEPT the specified columns.
// % of total sales
Revenue % = DIVIDE(
    [Total Revenue],
    CALCULATE([Total Revenue], ALL(Sales))  // removes all filters
)

// % within category only
Category % = DIVIDE(
    [Total Revenue],
    CALCULATE([Total Revenue], ALLEXCEPT(Products, Products[Category]))
)
💡 Why asked: Used in almost every percentage-of-total DAX calculation.
07 What is RANKX()?
Must Know
▶
RANKX(table, expression, [value], [order], [ties]) ranks each row in a table based on an expression.
// Rank products by revenue (highest = 1)
Product Rank =
RANKX(
    ALL(Products[ProductName]),  // rank within all products
    [Total Revenue],
    ,                            // current row's value
    DESC,                        // descending
    Dense                        // dense ranking (no gaps)
)
Different from SQL RANK() — RANKX works in the filter context model.
💡 Why asked: Asked when interviewers test your Power BI ranking visualisation skills.
08 Time intelligence: SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR, DATEADD, DATESYTD
Must Know
▶ 
// Year-over-year comparison
LY Revenue = CALCULATE([Total Revenue], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR(Dates[Date]))

// Month-over-month (go back 1 month)
PM Revenue = CALCULATE([Total Revenue], DATEADD(Dates[Date], -1, MONTH))

// YTD (Year-to-Date)
YTD Revenue = CALCULATE([Total Revenue], DATESYTD(Dates[Date]))

// YoY Growth %
YoY Growth = DIVIDE([Total Revenue] - [LY Revenue], [LY Revenue])
Requires a proper date table connected to your fact table.
💡 Why asked: Time intelligence is the #1 use case for DAX in business reporting.
09 What is DIVIDE() vs / operator?
Basic
▶
DIVIDE(numerator, denominator, [alternate_result]) safely handles division by zero — returns 0 (or alternate) instead of an error.
// Risky: returns error if denominator is 0
Margin = [Profit] / [Revenue]

// Safe:
Margin = DIVIDE([Profit], [Revenue], 0)  // returns 0 if Revenue = 0

// Show blank instead of 0
Margin = DIVIDE([Profit], [Revenue])     // default alternate = BLANK()
💡 Why asked: Always use DIVIDE in DAX. Using / is a code quality red flag.
10 What is a star schema?
Must Know
▶
A star schema has one central fact table (transactions, orders, events) surrounded by dimension tables (customers, products, dates, geography). Shape looks like a star.
Benefits: fast query performance, simple relationships, Power BI is optimised for it.
Your retail project: Orders = fact table. Customers/Products/Dates = dimension tables.
💡 Why asked: Power BI and data warehousing foundation. Your project naturally follows this structure.
11 What is DirectQuery vs Import mode?
Must Know
▶
Import mode: data copied into Power BI's in-memory engine (VertiPaq). Fastest performance. Data refreshed on schedule. Limited by memory.
DirectQuery: queries sent live to the source database. Always current data. Slower (limited DAX). No memory limit.
Import: best for datasets under ~1GB, fast dashboard response needed.
DirectQuery: best for real-time data, very large datasets, restricted data access policies.
💡 Why asked: Asked in every Power BI architecture discussion.
12 What is row-level security (RLS)?
Must Know
▶
RLS restricts which rows a user can see in Power BI based on their identity. You define DAX filter rules on dimension tables.
// Sales by region — restrict to user's region only
[Region] = USERNAME()  // or USERPRINCIPALNAME()

// Or use a separate access table:
Sales[RegionID] IN
    CALCULATETABLE(
        VALUES(UserAccess[RegionID]),
        UserAccess[Email] = USERPRINCIPALNAME()
    )
💡 Why asked: Every enterprise Power BI implementation uses RLS. Shows production-readiness.
13 What is Power Query? When to use it vs DAX?
Must Know
▶
Power Query (M language): ETL tool — cleans, reshapes, and transforms data BEFORE it loads into the model. No memory overhead for transformations.
DAX: calculations on already-loaded data, responds to filter context.
Rule: do transformations in Power Query (rename columns, remove nulls, merge tables, unpivot). Do aggregations and business logic in DAX measures.
💡 Why asked: Distinguishing where to put logic (PQ vs DAX) shows you understand the Power BI architecture.
14 How do you create a YTD measure?
Must Know
▶ 
// Using DATESYTD
Revenue YTD = CALCULATE(
    [Total Revenue],
    DATESYTD(Dates[Date])
)

// For fiscal year ending March 31
Revenue FY YTD = CALCULATE(
    [Total Revenue],
    DATESYTD(Dates[Date], "3/31")
)

// YTD with year filter for comparison
2024 YTD = CALCULATE(
    [Revenue YTD],
    Dates[Year] = 2024
)
💡 Why asked: YTD is in almost every business dashboard. You built one at Flow Link Systems.
15 VAR keyword in DAX — why use it?
Must Know
▶
VAR stores intermediate results to avoid recalculating the same expression multiple times. Makes DAX readable and often faster.
// Without VAR: [Total Revenue] evaluated twice
YoY % = DIVIDE(
    [Total Revenue] - CALCULATE([Total Revenue], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR(Dates[Date])),
    CALCULATE([Total Revenue], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR(Dates[Date]))
)

// With VAR: cleaner and more efficient
YoY % =
VAR CurrentRevenue = [Total Revenue]
VAR LastYearRevenue = CALCULATE([Total Revenue], SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR(Dates[Date]))
RETURN DIVIDE(CurrentRevenue - LastYearRevenue, LastYearRevenue)
💡 Why asked: VAR improves DAX quality. Using it shows professional-level Power BI skills.
16 questions
01 VLOOKUP vs XLOOKUP vs INDEX-MATCH?
Must Know
▶
VLOOKUP: searches leftmost column only, returns column by number, breaks when columns are inserted.
INDEX-MATCH: flexible, can look left/right, uses column name not number, faster for large data.
XLOOKUP (Office 365+): modern replacement — looks any direction, returns array, cleaner syntax.
=VLOOKUP(A2, Table, 3, 0)               -- lookup in col 1, return col 3
=INDEX(B:B, MATCH(A2, A:A, 0))          -- flexible
=XLOOKUP(A2, LookupCol, ReturnCol)
In interviews: explain why XLOOKUP is best, then show you know INDEX-MATCH as backup.
💡 Why asked: Excel lookup functions are in every DA job interview. Must know all three.
02 What is INDEX-MATCH? Why is it better than VLOOKUP?
Must Know
▶
INDEX(return_range, MATCH(lookup_value, lookup_range, 0))
=INDEX(C:C, MATCH("Alice", A:A, 0))  -- returns value from C where A = "Alice"
Why better than VLOOKUP:
- Can look left (VLOOKUP only goes right)
- Not affected by inserting/deleting columns
- Faster on large datasets
- Can do two-way lookup: INDEX with two MATCHes
💡 Why asked: INDEX-MATCH mastery shows Excel depth. Interviewers at DA roles always test this.
03 SUMIF vs SUMIFS?
Basic
▶
SUMIF(range, criteria, sum_range): sum with ONE condition.
SUMIFS(sum_range, range1, criteria1, range2, criteria2, ...): sum with MULTIPLE conditions.
=SUMIF(B:B, "Mumbai", C:C)               -- sum sales for Mumbai
=SUMIFS(C:C, B:B, "Mumbai", D:D, ">1000") -- Mumbai + amount > 1000
Note: SUMIFS argument order is different (sum_range FIRST).
💡 Why asked: Used in every financial report in Excel. Must be instant recall.
04 Nested IF vs IFS?
Basic
▶
Nested IF: complicated and hard to read beyond 3 levels.
IFS (Excel 2016+): cleaner for multiple conditions.
-- Nested IF (hard to read)
=IF(A2>90,"A",IF(A2>80,"B",IF(A2>70,"C","D")))

-- IFS (much cleaner)
=IFS(A2>90,"A", A2>80,"B", A2>70,"C", TRUE,"D")
Use IFS when available. Shows you write clean, maintainable formulas.
💡 Why asked: Appears in every Excel test for DA roles.
05 What is IFERROR()?
Basic
▶
IFERROR(value, value_if_error): returns alternative if the formula produces an error. Used to handle #N/A, #DIV/0!, #VALUE! errors.
=IFERROR(VLOOKUP(A2, Table, 2, 0), "Not Found")
=IFERROR(B2/C2, 0)   -- return 0 instead of division error
💡 Why asked: Defensive formula writing shows maturity. Interviewers expect clean error handling.
06 What is a Pivot Table? Key features?
Basic
▶
Pivot table summarises large datasets by dragging and dropping fields. Key features:
- Rows/Columns: dimensions to group by
- Values: aggregation (SUM, COUNT, AVERAGE, etc.)
- Filters/Slicers: interactive filtering
- Drill-down: click to see underlying data
- Calculated fields: custom formulas within the pivot
You built Power BI dashboards at Flow Link Systems — pivot tables are the Excel equivalent.
💡 Why asked: Every DA role uses pivot tables. This is a must-demonstrate skill.
07 What is Power Query in Excel?
Must Know
▶
Power Query is Excel's ETL tool (Get & Transform Data). Lets you connect to data sources, clean, and reshape data without writing formulas — all steps recorded and replayable.
Common uses: unpivot columns, merge tables, clean messy headers, split columns, filter rows, handle data type conversions.
The query is saved and auto-refreshes when you click Refresh All. Like a mini ETL pipeline inside Excel.
💡 Why asked: Power Query is increasingly expected in DA roles alongside SQL and Python for data prep.
08 LEFT(), RIGHT(), MID(), LEN(), TRIM()?
Basic
▶ 
=LEFT("KV Analytics", 2)    -- "KV" (first 2 chars)
=RIGHT("KV Analytics", 9)   -- "Analytics" (last 9 chars)
=MID("KV Analytics", 4, 3)  -- "Ana" (start at 4, take 3)
=LEN("KV Analytics")         -- 12 (length)
=TRIM("  hello  ")            -- "hello" (removes extra spaces)
Used together for parsing messy text data (phone numbers, addresses, product codes).
💡 Why asked: Text functions are in every Excel DA test. Useful for cleaning messy import data.
09 What are dynamic array functions?
Must Know
▶
Office 365 functions that spill results into multiple cells automatically:
=FILTER(A:B, C:C="Mumbai")    -- filter rows matching condition
=UNIQUE(A:A)                   -- unique values
=SORT(A:A, 1, -1)              -- sort descending
=SEQUENCE(5, 3, 1, 1)          -- 5x3 grid starting from 1
Replace many complex VLOOKUP/INDEX combinations. If you're on Office 365, use these.
💡 Why asked: Modern Excel skills. Shows you're up to date beyond basic formulas.
10 What is data validation?
Basic
▶
Data validation restricts what users can enter in a cell (prevents bad data entry):
- Dropdown list (select from options)
- Number range (1 to 100 only)
- Date range
- Text length
- Custom formula validation
Used to create user-friendly data entry forms and prevent errors in shared spreadsheets.
💡 Why asked: Shows you can build robust, error-proof Excel tools for non-technical users.
11 What is a macro in Excel VBA?
Basic
▶
A macro is a recorded sequence of actions that can be replayed with one click. VBA (Visual Basic for Applications) is the programming language behind macros.
Types: recorded macros (Excel records your clicks), written macros (you code the logic directly).
Sub FormatReport()
    Range("A1:D1").Font.Bold = True
    Range("A1:D1").Interior.Color = RGB(0, 112, 192)
    Columns("A:D").AutoFit
End Sub
💡 Why asked: DA roles that use Excel often need macro knowledge for automating repetitive reports.
12 Sub vs Function in VBA?
Basic
▶
Sub (Subroutine): performs actions, doesn't return a value. Called by buttons/macros.
Function: performs calculation, returns a value. Can be used as a custom worksheet function.
Sub FormatData()
    ' Does something, returns nothing
    Range("A1").Value = "Hello"
End Sub

Function CalcBonus(salary As Double) As Double
    ' Returns a value
    CalcBonus = salary * 0.1
End Function

' Use in cell: =CalcBonus(A2)
💡 Why asked: Basic VBA structure. Shows you know the difference between procedures and functions.
13 How do you loop in VBA?
Basic
▶ 
' For loop: known number of iterations
For i = 1 To 10
    Cells(i, 1).Value = i * 2
Next i

' For Each: iterate over collection
For Each ws In ThisWorkbook.Worksheets
    ws.Range("A1").Value = "Header"
Next ws

' Do While: condition-based
Dim i As Integer: i = 1
Do While Cells(i, 1).Value <> ""
    Cells(i, 2).Value = Cells(i, 1).Value * 2
    i = i + 1
Loop
💡 Why asked: Looping is how you automate reports across multiple sheets, rows, or files.
14 How do you reference a cell in VBA?
Basic
▶ 
' Range: by address
Range("A1").Value = 100
Range("A1:D10").ClearContents

' Cells: by row and column number (better for loops)
Cells(1, 1).Value = 100  ' Row 1, Column 1 = A1
Cells(i, j).Value = ...  ' Dynamic reference in loops

' ActiveCell, Selection
ActiveCell.Offset(1, 0).Value = "Next row"
💡 Why asked: Cell referencing is fundamental to any VBA automation.
15 What is error handling in VBA?
Must Know
▶ 
Sub SafeProcess()
    On Error GoTo ErrorHandler   ' jump to handler on error

    ' Your code here
    Dim x As Double
    x = 1 / 0    ' triggers error

    Exit Sub     ' skip error handler if no error

ErrorHandler:
    MsgBox "Error " & Err.Number & ": " & Err.Description
    Resume Next  ' continue after error line
End Sub

' Quick silent error skip (use carefully!)
On Error Resume Next
💡 Why asked: Without error handling, macros crash with unhelpful messages. Shows professional VBA.
16 How do you work with worksheets in VBA?
Basic
▶ 
' Reference by name
Sheets("Sales").Range("A1").Value = 100

' Reference by index
Sheets(1).Activate

' Loop through all sheets
For Each ws In ThisWorkbook.Worksheets
    If ws.Name <> "Summary" Then
        ws.Range("A1").Value = Date
    End If
Next ws

' Add/delete sheet
Sheets.Add(After:=Sheets(Sheets.Count)).Name = "New Sheet"
💡 Why asked: Multi-sheet operations are the most common VBA automation use case.
25 questions
01 Walk me through your Customer Churn project end to end.
Project
▶
Churn Prediction
Business problem: Telecom company losing customers. Goal: predict who will churn so retention team can intervene.
Data: 7,043 IBM Telco records, 21 features, 26% churn rate (imbalanced).
EDA: Found that month-to-month contracts (42% churn), no tech support, and high monthly charges were top churn drivers.
Preprocessing: Label encoding, SMOTE (oversampled minority class to fix 74/26 imbalance).
Modelling: Compared Logistic Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost. XGBoost won (AUC 0.84, recall 72%).
KMeans: Clustered customers into 4 segments — found 15x churn rate difference (3.5% to 54.7%).
Deployment: FastAPI REST endpoint + Docker + Render. Streamlit dashboard with SHAP explanations and per-customer retention recommendations.
💡 Why asked: Interviewers expect you to narrate this coherently in under 3 minutes. Practice out loud.
02 Why XGBoost over Logistic Regression or Random Forest?
Project
▶
Churn Prediction
vs Logistic Regression: Logistic Regression assumes linear relationships. Churn has complex non-linear interactions (e.g., senior citizen + month-to-month contract + no partner = very high risk, not a linear combination). XGBoost captures these.
vs Random Forest: Both are ensemble tree methods, but XGBoost is gradient-boosted (each tree corrects previous errors). XGBoost gave AUC 0.84 vs Random Forest's 0.81. Also faster training and built-in regularization.
Key advantage for this project: Native SHAP compatibility for explainability, which was a business requirement.
💡 Why asked: You WILL be asked this. Have a specific, metrics-backed answer ready.
03 What is SMOTE? Why did you use it? What are its risks?
Project
▶
Churn Prediction
SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique): generates synthetic samples for the minority class by interpolating between existing minority class examples (not just duplicating).
Why used: 74% non-churn vs 26% churn — without handling, model predicts non-churn most of the time (74% accuracy = useless). SMOTE balanced the training set.
Risks: Overfitting if applied to test set (ALWAYS apply SMOTE only to training set). Can introduce noisy synthetic samples near class boundaries. May not work well with very high-dimensional data.
from imblearn.over_sampling import SMOTE
X_train_res, y_train_res = SMOTE(random_state=42).fit_resample(X_train, y_train)
💡 Why asked: SMOTE misuse (applying to test data) is a common mistake interviewers probe for.
04 What is SHAP? Explain a SHAP value of -0.3 for a feature.
Project
▶
Churn Prediction + HR Attrition
SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations): measures each feature's contribution to a specific prediction, grounded in game theory (Shapley values from cooperative game theory).
SHAP value of -0.3 for 'monthly_charges': this feature pushed the churn probability DOWN by 0.3 (log-odds) for this specific customer. It made the model less likely to predict churn.
Positive SHAP: feature pushed prediction toward churn.
Negative SHAP: feature pushed prediction away from churn.
import shap
explainer = shap.TreeExplainer(model)
shap_values = explainer.shap_values(X_test)
💡 Why asked: SHAP is on your resume twice. You must be able to explain it to a non-technical manager.
05 AUC is 0.84 — is that good? How do you interpret it?
Project
▶
Churn Prediction
AUC interpretation: There is an 84% probability that the model ranks a randomly chosen churner higher than a randomly chosen non-churner. In other words, it's 84% better than random guessing at ranking customers by churn risk.
Is 0.84 good? Yes — for telecom churn on a 26% imbalanced dataset, 0.84 is strong. Industry benchmark is typically 0.75-0.85. Above 0.9 often indicates data leakage.
Why AUC over accuracy?: Accuracy would be 74% even if you predicted "no churn" for everyone. AUC evaluates ranking quality regardless of class imbalance.
💡 Why asked: Interviewers WILL test if you truly understand AUC vs accuracy. Be ready.
06 Why 72% recall instead of optimising for accuracy?
Project
▶
Churn Prediction
Business reason: False negatives (missing a churner) cost far more than false positives (contacting a non-churner unnecessarily). If we miss a churner, we lose that customer's lifetime value. If we incorrectly flag a non-churner, we just offer them a discount they accept happily.
Cost asymmetry: telecom CLV ≈ ₹12,000/year. Cost of a retention offer ≈ ₹500. So we'd rather have 72% recall with some false positives than miss churners.
Threshold tuning: I lowered the classification threshold from 0.5 to ~0.35 to increase recall at the cost of some precision.
💡 Why asked: This shows you think in business terms, not just model metrics. This is what separates a DA from a data entry person.
07 How does your KMeans clustering work? How did you choose K?
Project
▶
Churn Prediction
KMeans clustering: groups customers by RFM-like features (tenure, monthly charges, contract type) into K clusters. Each cluster gets a mean centroid and customers are assigned to nearest centroid.
Choosing K: Used the Elbow Method (plot inertia vs K, pick the 'elbow') and Silhouette Score. K=4 gave the best separation. The 4 segments ranged from 3.5% to 54.7% churn rate — a 15x difference that validated the segmentation quality.
Why cluster?: Instead of one retention message for all customers, each segment gets a targeted strategy.
💡 Why asked: Shows you used unsupervised learning for a business purpose, not just to add to the resume.
08 What does 'zero false-safe classifications on validated distress events' mean in your supplier risk project?
Project
▶
Supplier Risk
Plain English: For every supplier that genuinely went into financial distress (validated by news events), the system flagged them as 'at risk' — it never missed a real distress event by classifying it as 'safe'.
Why it matters: In supply chain, a false-safe classification = you're unaware of a supplier about to fail = you get blindsided by a stockout/supply disruption with no time to find alternatives. That's the worst-case outcome.
How achieved: The 70/30 ML-rule ensemble — the rule component catches obvious red flags (sudden news volume spike, stock price drop > 15%) that the ML might miss.
💡 Why asked: Interviewers test if you can translate technical metrics into business impact.
09 What is your 70/30 ML-rule ensemble in the supplier risk project?
Project
▶
Supplier Risk
Design: Final risk score = 0.7 × ML score + 0.3 × rule-based score.
ML component (70%): Gradient Boosting model trained on historical supplier data — scores 0 to 1 based on financial patterns.
Rule component (30%): Hard-coded thresholds — if sentiment score < -0.5 OR stock price dropped > 20% in 30 days OR recent news mentions "bankruptcy/recall/shutdown" → score = 1.0 (immediate flag).
Why hybrid?: ML is good at subtle patterns. Rules catch obvious red flags the ML might underweight if they're rare in training data. Rules also provide interpretable reasoning for stakeholders.
💡 Why asked: Hybrid approaches are common in production ML. This shows production thinking.
10 What is Kaplan-Meier survival analysis? Why use it for HR attrition?
Project
▶
HR Attrition
Kaplan-Meier: non-parametric estimator of the survival function — probability of 'surviving' (not leaving) past time t, accounting for censoring (employees still at company = censored data).
Why for attrition?: Unlike binary classification (will they leave? yes/no), survival analysis answers: when are they likely to leave? The 90-day at-risk window means the model predicts elevated attrition probability in the next 90 days specifically.
Log-rank test p < 0.0001: statistically significant difference between high-risk vs low-risk survival curves. Not a random fluctuation.
💡 Why asked: Survival analysis is rare knowledge for a fresher — this is a huge differentiator. Explain it simply.
11 How did you derive the 90-day at-risk window?
Project
▶
HR Attrition
Process: I plotted the Kaplan-Meier survival curves stratified by risk score quintiles. The curves for high-risk employees (top 20%) diverged most sharply from low-risk employees in the 60-120 day window.
90 days was chosen because: the separation was statistically significant (log-rank p < 0.0001) and 90 days gives HR enough lead time to intervene (schedule 1-on-1s, salary reviews, project reassignment) before the employee resigns.
Practical implication: "This employee has a 67% probability of leaving within the next 90 days" is more actionable than "this employee might leave someday.".
💡 Why asked: Shows you connected the statistical finding to a specific, actionable business output.
12 Why is 150% salary the model for replacement cost?
Project
▶
HR Attrition
Industry benchmark: SHRM (Society for Human Resource Management) estimates total replacement cost at 50-200% of annual salary depending on role complexity. 150% is conservative for knowledge workers.
Components included: Recruitment costs (job posting, agency fees, interviews) + onboarding cost + productivity ramp-up (new hire takes 3-6 months to reach full productivity, which means 3-6 months of partial output at full salary) + training + institutional knowledge loss.
Business value: If a ₹8L/year data scientist leaves, the company incurs ₹12L in replacement cost. Spending ₹50K on a retention programme is clearly ROI-positive if it reduces attrition by even 10%.
💡 Why asked: Interviewers love seeing you quantify business impact. 150% needs a justification — have it.
13 You used a 4-model ensemble (XGBoost + Prophet + ARIMA + LSTM) for retail sales. Why all 4?
Project
▶
Retail Sales
XGBoost: captures non-linear feature interactions (promotions, holidays, competitor prices). Best for structured tabular data.
Prophet: designed for business time series. Handles seasonality, holidays, and trend changepoints cleanly. Easy to tune.
ARIMA: classic statistical baseline. Good at capturing autocorrelation in the time series itself. Acts as a check on the complex models.
LSTM: captures long-term temporal dependencies that ARIMA misses. Good for patterns that span multiple seasons.
Ensemble benefit: different models excel in different market conditions. Averaging reduces individual model risk. The ensemble R²=0.9436 outperformed any single model.
💡 Why asked: Expect 'why not just use XGBoost?' — have a specific answer for each model's unique contribution.
14 What is RFM analysis?
Project
▶
Retail Sales
RFM = Recency, Frequency, Monetary — framework for customer segmentation:
Recency: how recently did the customer buy? (days since last purchase)
Frequency: how often do they buy? (number of transactions in period)
Monetary: how much do they spend? (total or average order value)
Each customer gets an RFM score. Champions (high R, high F, high M) get VIP treatment. At-Risk (low R, previously high F/M) get win-back campaigns. You implemented this in your 19 advanced SQL queries using NTILE() windowing.
💡 Why asked: RFM is the most widely used customer analytics framework. Must explain it cold.
15 R² = 0.9436 — what does this mean for the business?
Project
▶
Retail Sales
Technical meaning: the model explains 94.36% of the variance in sales revenue. Only 5.64% of variance is unexplained.
Business meaning: if historical sales fluctuated between ₹1L and ₹10L per month, the model's predictions are within the range of 94.36% of that variation — it accurately mirrors the business's revenue patterns.
Caveat to add: R² alone doesn't tell you about prediction accuracy on future unseen data. RMSE=$0.10 on held-out data validates real-world performance. Also check if train R² vs test R² are similar to confirm no overfitting.
💡 Why asked: R²=0.9436 sounds impressive. Make sure you can explain it AND caveat it appropriately.
16 How does your What-If API work mechanically?
Project
▶
Retail Sales
Endpoint: POST /predict accepts: product_id, quantity, discount_pct, promotion_type, day_of_week.
Mechanics: the input features are passed to the trained ensemble model. The model returns predicted revenue. The margin is calculated as: margin = (predicted_revenue - cost) / predicted_revenue.
20% discount example: at 20% discount, the predicted revenue drops. When plugged into the margin formula with fixed COGS, margin drops from 24.8% to 8.4%. This is real-time quantification of discount impact.
FastAPI docs at /docs provides an interactive Swagger UI for testing.
💡 Why asked: Shows you built a functional product, not just a Jupyter notebook.
17 What is CLV modeling?
Project
▶
Retail Sales
CLV (Customer Lifetime Value): total revenue expected from a customer over their entire relationship with the business.
Simple formula: CLV = Average Order Value × Purchase Frequency × Customer Lifespan
Advanced: Pareto/NBD model (for non-contractual settings) or survival analysis.
In your project: used historical RFM data to estimate CLV, then segmented customers by CLV quartile. High-CLV customers get priority in retention budgets.
💡 Why asked: CLV drives marketing budget allocation. Interviewers in e-commerce/retail roles always ask this.
18 What is ABSA (Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis)?
Project
▶
Airline Sentiment
Regular sentiment: "This airline is terrible." → Negative
ABSA: "The seat was comfortable but the food was awful and the staff was rude." → Seat: Positive, Food: Negative, Staff: Negative
Why it matters: overall negative doesn't tell the airline WHERE to fix. ABSA identifies WHICH aspect to fix (food, delays, baggage, customer service).
Your routing layer: If food complaint → routes to Catering team. If delay complaint → routes to Operations. Automated triage replaces manual sorting.
💡 Why asked: ABSA is an advanced NLP concept. If you can explain it simply, you stand out from other freshers.
19 Why did TF-IDF outperform BERT on in-domain airline data?
Project
▶
Airline Sentiment
Why BERT underperformed: The base BERT model (bert-base-uncased) is pretrained on Wikipedia + BookCorpus — general text. Airline Twitter language is highly domain-specific: "DL123 delayed 3hrs again, thanks for nothing" is very different from Wikipedia text.
Why TF-IDF worked better: TF-IDF trained directly on 14,640 in-domain airline tweets. It captured domain vocabulary (flight numbers, airline codes, delay-specific language) that BERT's pretrained representations don't weight appropriately.
Fix: Fine-tuning BERT on domain data would likely beat TF-IDF. But fine-tuning requires compute resources.
💡 Why asked: This is the most interesting finding in your project. Explain WHY it happened, not just THAT it happened.
20 What is 133% lift? How do you calculate it?
Project
▶
Airline Sentiment
Lift = (model precision / baseline precision) − 1, expressed as %.
Baseline precision: if you randomly assigned sentiment labels, you'd get 33.3% precision (3 classes, equal distribution).
Your model precision: ~76.9% accuracy → lift = (0.769 / 0.333) − 1 = 1.31 = 131% lift (approximately 133%).
Business meaning: Your model is 133% more precise than randomly routing complaints. You correctly route 2.33x more complaints to the right department than random.
💡 Why asked: Lift is used in marketing analytics constantly. Being able to calculate it on your own project is impressive.
21 How would you improve the airline sentiment model from 76.9% to 85%+?
Project
▶
Airline Sentiment
1. Fine-tune BERT on domain data (airline tweets with labels). Even 5K labelled examples can push accuracy to 85%+.
2. Ensemble TF-IDF + fine-tuned BERT: combine both models' probabilities.
3. Better data: 14,640 tweets is small. Collect more labelled data, especially for ambiguous cases (neutral is hardest to classify).
4. Preprocessing: better handling of @mentions, hashtags, flight numbers (domain-specific tokenisation).
5. Address class imbalance: if neutral is under-represented, use class weights or SMOTE for text.
💡 Why asked: Shows you think like a senior — not just 'here's what I did' but 'here's how to make it better.'
22 How would you scale your sentiment system to 1M tweets/day?
Project
▶
Airline Sentiment
Architecture changes:
1. Message queue: Kafka or RabbitMQ to buffer incoming tweets
2. Async processing: FastAPI + Celery workers (multiple workers process queue in parallel)
3. Model serving: TorchServe or TF Serving — optimised batch inference
4. Database: Move from SQLite to PostgreSQL + Redis for fast writes
5. Auto-scaling: Kubernetes for scaling workers based on queue depth
6. Monitoring: Track model drift (accuracy drop over time) and alert when data distribution shifts
💡 Why asked: System design for ML at scale. This separates freshers from candidates who think production.
23 How does your FastAPI endpoint handle a prediction request?
Project
▶
Churn / HR / Supplier APIs
from fastapi import FastAPI
from pydantic import BaseModel
import pickle

app = FastAPI()
model = pickle.load(open("model.pkl", "rb"))

class CustomerInput(BaseModel):
    tenure: int
    monthly_charges: float
    contract: str

@app.post("/predict")
def predict(data: CustomerInput):
    features = preprocess(data.dict())  # encode, scale
    prob = model.predict_proba(features)[0][1]
    return {"churn_probability": prob, "risk": "High" if prob > 0.5 else "Low"}
Pydantic validates input types. Docker containerises the app. Render hosts it publicly.
💡 Why asked: Interviewers will probe your API knowledge since you built 5 of them.
24 What is Docker and why did you containerise your projects?
Project
▶
All Projects
Docker: packages your application + all its dependencies into a container that runs identically everywhere (laptop, server, cloud).
Why containerise: "It works on my machine" is not good enough for production. Docker ensures the same Python version, library versions, and system dependencies everywhere.
# Dockerfile example
FROM python:3.10-slim
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install -r requirements.txt
COPY . .
CMD ["uvicorn", "main:app", "--host", "0.0.0.0", "--port", "8000"]
Deployed on Render using Docker image.
💡 Why asked: Docker on a fresher's resume is impressive. Be ready to explain what it is and why you used it.
25 What is Prophet? How does it handle seasonality?
Project
▶
Retail Sales
Prophet (by Meta/Facebook) decomposes time series into: Trend (using changepoint detection) + Seasonality (Fourier series for yearly/weekly/daily patterns) + Holiday effects + Noise.
Seasonality handling: additive (total sales = trend + seasonal pattern) or multiplicative (total sales = trend × seasonal factor — better when seasonal fluctuations grow with trend).
from prophet import Prophet
model = Prophet(seasonality_mode='multiplicative',
               holidays=indian_holidays)
model.fit(df)  # df must have 'ds' (date) and 'y' (value) columns
💡 Why asked: Prophet is increasingly used in business forecasting. Shows real-world time series knowledge.
TS
Time Series Analysis
ARIMA . SARIMA . Prophet . LSTM . Stationarity . Forecasting -- zero to expert
What is Time Series?
A time series is simply a list of values collected over time -- one measurement per time step. Examples: daily stock price, monthly sales, hourly temperature, yearly GDP.

Why is it special? The ORDER of the data matters. Tuesday depends on Monday. Normal ML ignores this. Time series models EXPLOIT this dependency.

4 Components of Every Time Series

  • Trend -- long-term rise or fall (sales growing every year)
  • Seasonality -- repeating pattern at fixed intervals (sales spike every December)
  • Cyclical -- long waves without fixed period (economic boom & bust)
  • Irregular/Noise -- random unexplained fluctuations

Stationarity -- Why it matters

Most models need the series to be stationary -- meaning:

  • Constant mean (no trend)
  • Constant variance (no widening swings)
  • No seasonality

ADF Test: p < 0.05 -> stationary. Fix with differencing (subtract previous value).

The Family Tree of Time Series Models
Simple -> Complex: Moving Average -> Exponential Smoothing -> ARIMA -> SARIMA -> Prophet -> LSTM

MA -- Moving Average

Idea: Smooth out noise by averaging the last N values.
Analogy: You estimate tomorrow's temperature as the average of the past 7 days.
Problem: Always lags behind reality. Can't catch a sudden spike.
Use when: Data is very noisy and you just want the trend.

ES -- Exponential Smoothing

Idea: Weighted average where recent values count MORE than old ones.
Analogy: Yesterday matters more than last week. Last week matters more than last month.
Alpha (alpha): Controls how fast old values fade. High alpha = forget quickly.
Holt-Winters: ES + handles trend + seasonality.

AR -- AutoRegressive Model

Idea: Predict today using the last P values of the same series.
Formula: Y(t) = c + phi1.Y(t-1) + phi2.Y(t-2) + ... + phip.Y(t-p) + error
Analogy: Stock price today depends on the last P days of stock price.
p = how many past values to use (found via PACF plot).

MA -- Moving Average (in ARIMA context)

Idea: Predict today using the last Q forecast ERRORS (not values).
Formula: Y(t) = c + error(t) + theta1.error(t-1) + ... + thetaq.error(t-q)
Analogy: If I over-predicted yesterday by 10, I'll correct today by 10.
q = how many past errors to use (found via ACF plot).

ARIMA(p, d, q) -- The Classic

AR + I + MA combined:
p = lags in AR part (look at PACF)
d = number of times you difference to make stationary (usually 0 or 1)
q = lags in MA part (look at ACF)
Analogy: ARIMA(2,1,1) means: look at last 2 values + 1 error, after differencing once.
Best for: Non-seasonal data with trends.

SARIMA -- Seasonal ARIMA

ARIMA extended for seasonality:
SARIMA(p,d,q)(P,D,Q,m)
m = seasonal period (12 for monthly, 7 for weekly)
P, D, Q = AR, diff, MA for the seasonal part
Example: Monthly airline passengers -> m=12
Best for: Data with clear repeating seasonal patterns.

Prophet -- Facebook's Model

Idea: Decomposes series into trend + yearly season + weekly season + holidays.
Why it's great for beginners: Works out of the box. Handles missing data and outliers.
Trend: Linear or logistic growth
Use when: Business time series with holidays, multiple seasonalities.
One line: model = Prophet(); model.fit(df); future = model.make_future_dataframe(periods=30)

LSTM for Time Series

Deep learning approach: LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) remembers long-range patterns.
When ARIMA fails: non-linear patterns, very long dependencies.
Input: sliding window of past N steps -> predict next step
Best for: Complex multivariate time series (many input features).
Downside: Needs lots of data. Hard to interpret. Slower to train. 

import pandas as pd
from statsmodels.tsa.arima.model import ARIMA       # bring in ARIMA model
from statsmodels.tsa.statespace.sarimax import SARIMAX  # bring in SARIMA
from prophet import Prophet                          # Facebook's forecasting tool
from statsmodels.tsa.stattools import adfuller      # ADF stationarity test

# ── Step 1: Check stationarity ────────────────────────────────
result = adfuller(df['value'])                      # run ADF test
print('p-value:', result[1])                        # p < 0.05 means stationary

# ── Step 2: Difference if not stationary ──────────────────────
df['diff'] = df['value'].diff()                     # subtract previous row from current
df.dropna(inplace=True)                             # first row becomes NaN after diff

# ── Step 3: Fit ARIMA ─────────────────────────────────────────
model = ARIMA(df['value'], order=(2, 1, 1))         # p=2 lags, d=1 difference, q=1 error lag
result = model.fit()                                # learn parameters from data
forecast = result.forecast(steps=30)               # predict next 30 time steps

# ── Step 4: SARIMA for seasonal data ──────────────────────────
sarima = SARIMAX(df['value'],
                 order=(1,1,1),                     # non-seasonal part
                 seasonal_order=(1,1,1,12))         # seasonal part with m=12 (monthly)
sarima_fit = sarima.fit(disp=False)                 # train without printing optimizer output
sarima_forecast = sarima_fit.forecast(steps=12)     # predict next 12 months

# ── Step 5: Prophet ───────────────────────────────────────────
df_prophet = df.rename(columns={'date':'ds','value':'y'})  # Prophet needs columns 'ds' and 'y'
m = Prophet(yearly_seasonality=True,                # capture yearly patterns
            weekly_seasonality=True)                # capture weekly patterns
m.fit(df_prophet)                                   # learn patterns from data
future = m.make_future_dataframe(periods=90)        # create date frame for 90 future days
forecast = m.predict(future)                        # generate forecast with confidence intervals
m.plot(forecast)                                    # show forecast chart
m.plot_components(forecast)                         # show trend + seasonality breakdown
Which model should I pick?
Simple, stationary, no seasons -> ARIMA
Seasonal data (monthly/weekly) -> SARIMA
Business data with holidays -> Prophet
Lots of data, complex non-linear patterns -> LSTM
Just want the trend, very noisy -> Exponential Smoothing

ACF Plot -- Find q

AutoCorrelation Function
Shows correlation of series with its OWN past lags.
Where it cuts off = value of q (MA part).
Spike at lag 1 only -> q=1

PACF Plot -- Find p

Partial AutoCorrelation
Shows direct correlation at each lag (removes shorter lag effects).
Where it cuts off = value of p (AR part).
Spike at lags 1-2 -> p=2

Error Metrics

MAE: mean absolute error (same unit as data)
RMSE: root mean squared error (punishes big errors)
MAPE: mean absolute % error (percentage, easy to explain)
Rule: MAPE < 10% is excellent for most business cases.

DL
Deep Learning -- From Zero
Neurons . Backprop . CNNs . RNNs . LSTMs . Training tips -- complete beginner guide
What is Deep Learning?
Deep Learning = Machine Learning using Neural Networks with MANY layers.
A neural network loosely mimics how your brain works: thousands of tiny connected units that pass signals to each other and learn from examples.

Why "Deep"? Because the network has MANY layers (deep = many hidden layers). Each layer learns progressively more abstract features. Layer 1 -> edges. Layer 2 -> shapes. Layer 3 -> objects. Layer 10 -> faces.

Neuron -- The Basic Unit

A neuron receives inputs, multiplies each by a weight, adds them up, adds a bias, then passes through an activation function.

Formula: output = activation(w1.x1 + w2.x2 + ... + bias)

Analogy: A neuron is like a voting machine. Each input votes (with weight). If total vote exceeds a threshold, the neuron fires.

Layers

Input Layer: Your raw data (features). One node per feature.
Hidden Layers: Where learning happens. Can have 1 to 100+ layers.
Output Layer: Final prediction. 1 node for regression. N nodes for N classes.

Deep network = more than 2 hidden layers.
Width = how many neurons per layer.

Activation Functions -- Why?

Without activation, the network is just a linear equation no matter how many layers.
Activations add non-linearity -- letting the network learn curves, boundaries, complex patterns.

ReLU: max(0, x) -- most common. Simple and fast.
Sigmoid: squashes to 0-1 -- good for binary output.
Softmax: squashes to probabilities summing to 1 -- multi-class output.
Tanh: squashes to -1 to 1 -- used in RNNs.

Forward Pass

Data flows LEFT to RIGHT through the network:
Input -> Layer 1 -> Layer 2 -> ... -> Output

Each layer: multiply inputs by weights, add bias, apply activation.
At the end, compare prediction to true answer using a Loss Function.
Loss measures how wrong we are -- we want to MINIMISE it.

Backpropagation

After forward pass, error flows RIGHT to LEFT.
Using calculus (chain rule), we compute how much each weight contributed to the error.
Then we NUDGE each weight slightly in the direction that reduces error.

Analogy: You got a wrong answer. Blame each step backwards -- fix the biggest culprit first.

Gradient Descent

The algorithm that updates weights to reduce loss.
Learning Rate (lr): how big each step is.
Too big -> overshoots. Too small -> takes forever.

SGD: basic gradient descent
Adam: adaptive lr -- best default choice
RMSprop: good for RNNs 

import torch                                        # bring in PyTorch deep learning library
import torch.nn as nn                              # bring in neural network building blocks

# ── Define a simple neural network ─────────────
class SimpleNet(nn.Module):                        # define a new network class
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(10, 64)              # layer 1: 10 inputs -> 64 neurons
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(64, 32)              # layer 2: 64 -> 32 neurons
        self.fc3 = nn.Linear(32, 1)               # output layer: 32 -> 1 output
        self.relu = nn.ReLU()                      # ReLU activation function
        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(0.3)             # randomly turn off 30% neurons to prevent overfitting

    def forward(self, x):                          # defines the forward pass (how data flows)
        x = self.relu(self.fc1(x))                # pass through layer 1 then activate
        x = self.dropout(x)                        # apply dropout for regularisation
        x = self.relu(self.fc2(x))                # pass through layer 2 then activate
        x = self.fc3(x)                            # final output (no activation for regression)
        return x

model = SimpleNet()                                # create the network
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(),  # Adam optimiser adjusts weights
                             lr=0.001)             # learning rate -- step size for updates
criterion = nn.MSELoss()                           # mean squared error loss for regression

# ── Training loop ───────────────────────────────
for epoch in range(100):                           # loop over the dataset 100 times
    optimizer.zero_grad()                          # clear old gradients before each step
    output = model(X_train)                        # forward pass: make predictions
    loss = criterion(output, y_train)              # compute how wrong we are
    loss.backward()                                # backprop: compute how to fix each weight
    optimizer.step()                               # update all weights by one step
    if epoch % 10 == 0:
        print(f'Epoch {epoch}, Loss: {loss.item():.4f}')  # show progress every 10 epochs
CNN -- Convolutional Neural Network
CNNs are the go-to for images (also works on text and time series). Instead of connecting every pixel to every neuron (too many weights), CNNs slide a small filter over the image, learning local patterns like edges, textures, shapes.

How a CNN works

1. Convolution layer: A small filter (e.g. 3×3) slides across the image, computing dot product at each position. Learns features like edges.
2. Activation (ReLU): Remove negative values.
3. Pooling layer: Shrink the feature map (max pooling = keep the biggest value in each 2×2 block).
4. Repeat: More Conv -> ReLU -> Pool layers = higher-level features.
5. Flatten + Dense: Turn into 1D and pass to regular layers for final prediction.

Famous CNN Architectures

LeNet (1998): First practical CNN (handwritten digits)
AlexNet (2012): Won ImageNet -- started the DL revolution
VGG (2014): Very deep, simple 3×3 filters throughout
ResNet (2015): Skip connections -- allows 100+ layers without vanishing gradient
EfficientNet: Best accuracy/size tradeoff -- great for production 

import torch.nn as nn

class SimpleCNN(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.conv1 = nn.Conv2d(1, 32, kernel_size=3) # 32 filters of size 3x3 on grayscale image
        self.pool  = nn.MaxPool2d(2, 2)               # take max in each 2x2 block (halves size)
        self.conv2 = nn.Conv2d(32, 64, kernel_size=3) # 64 filters to detect higher-level features
        self.fc1   = nn.Linear(64 * 6 * 6, 128)      # flatten and connect to 128 neurons
        self.fc2   = nn.Linear(128, 10)               # 10 outputs (one per digit class 0-9)
        self.relu  = nn.ReLU()

    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.pool(self.relu(self.conv1(x)))       # convolve, activate, pool
        x = self.pool(self.relu(self.conv2(x)))       # convolve again, activate, pool
        x = x.view(-1, 64 * 6 * 6)                   # flatten to 1D
        x = self.relu(self.fc1(x))                    # dense layer with activation
        x = self.fc2(x)                               # final classification output
        return x
RNN / LSTM / GRU -- Models for Sequences
Regular neural networks process each input independently. But for text, speech, and time series, ORDER matters. RNNs have a "memory" -- they carry information from previous steps to the next.

RNN -- Recurrent Neural Network

At each time step, an RNN takes:
• The current input (x_t)
• The hidden state from the previous step (h_{t-1})
Produces: a new hidden state h_t

Problem: RNNs forget things from many steps ago (vanishing gradient).

LSTM -- Long Short-Term Memory

LSTM solves the forgetting problem using 3 GATES:
Forget gate: decide what to throw away from memory
Input gate: decide what new info to store
Output gate: decide what to output

Analogy: LSTM is like a sticky note. You decide what to write, what to erase, and what to use.

GRU -- Gated Recurrent Unit

Simplified version of LSTM with only 2 gates:
Update gate: how much past memory to keep
Reset gate: how much past to forget

Faster than LSTM, often similar performance.
Use: when you want speed + memory without full LSTM complexity.

Overfitting Prevention in DL

Dropout: randomly turn off neurons during training (they can't co-depend)
Batch Normalisation: normalise activations per mini-batch (faster training)
L1/L2 Regularisation: penalty on large weights
Early Stopping: stop training when validation loss stops improving
Data Augmentation: create more training data (flip/rotate images)

TR
Transformers & Attention
Self-Attention . Multi-Head . Positional Encoding . Encoder-Decoder . Architecture
The Problem Transformers Solved
RNNs process text word-by-word in order. This is SLOW (can't parallelize) and forgets long-range context ("The cat, which sat on the mat for hours, was happy" -- the RNN may forget "cat" by the time it reaches "happy").

Transformers (2017, "Attention is All You Need") process ALL words at once and directly compute relationships between ANY two words -- no matter how far apart they are.

Attention Mechanism -- The Core Idea

When processing a word, attention says: "which other words should I pay attention to?"

Every word creates 3 vectors:
Q (Query): what am I looking for?
K (Key): what do I contain?
V (Value): what information do I pass on?

Score = how similar is my Query to your Key?
High score -> I borrow a lot of your Value.

Self-Attention Step by Step

1. Word -> multiply by 3 weight matrices -> Q, K, V vectors
2. Score = Q . K^T (dot product of query with all keys)
3. Divide by √(dimension) to keep gradients stable
4. Softmax -> weights that sum to 1 (how much to attend to each word)
5. Weighted sum of all Values = output for this word

Analogy: Google search. Q=your query. K=page keywords. V=page content. Score=relevance.

Multi-Head Attention

Run attention H times in parallel with different learned projections.
Each "head" learns a different type of relationship:
• Head 1 might learn subject-verb agreement
• Head 2 might learn pronoun reference
• Head 3 might learn verb tense

Concatenate all heads' outputs -> project back to model dimension.

Positional Encoding

Attention is order-agnostic (treats "dog bites man" same as "man bites dog").
Fix: add a positional encoding vector to each word embedding.
This encodes the position (1st word, 2nd word...) into the representation.

Sine/cosine formula or learned embeddings are both used.

Transformer Architecture
Two halves: Encoder (reads input, builds understanding) and Decoder (generates output word by word). Some models use only encoder (BERT), some only decoder (GPT), some both (T5).

Encoder Block

Each encoder block has:
1. Multi-Head Self-Attention
2. Add & Norm (residual connection + layer norm)
3. Feed Forward Network (2 linear layers + ReLU)
4. Add & Norm again

Stacked N times (BERT uses 12 or 24 blocks).

Decoder Block

Each decoder block has:
1. Masked Self-Attention (can't look at future words)
2. Add & Norm
3. Cross-Attention (attends to encoder output)
4. Add & Norm
5. Feed Forward
6. Add & Norm

3 Transformer Families

Encoder-only (BERT family):
Best for: understanding, classification, NER

Decoder-only (GPT family):
Best for: text generation, LLMs

Encoder-Decoder (T5, BART):
Best for: translation, summarisation 

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModel
import torch

# ── Load a pre-trained transformer ─────────────
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained('bert-base-uncased')  # load tokenizer (converts text to numbers)
model = AutoModel.from_pretrained('bert-base-uncased')          # load pre-trained BERT model

text = "The cat sat on the mat."
inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors='pt')     # convert text to token IDs as PyTorch tensors
outputs = model(**inputs)                          # run through all transformer layers

# outputs.last_hidden_state has shape [batch, seq_len, 768]
# each token gets a 768-dimensional vector representing its meaning IN CONTEXT
cls_embedding = outputs.last_hidden_state[:, 0, :]  # [CLS] token = sentence-level embedding
print(cls_embedding.shape)                            # torch.Size([1, 768])
NLP
BERT & NLP Models
BERT . RoBERTa . DistilBERT . Fine-tuning . Tokenisation . NLP Pipelines
What is BERT?
Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (Google, 2018).
Before BERT, NLP models read text LEFT-TO-RIGHT or right-to-left -- not both at once. BERT reads the ENTIRE sentence simultaneously, understanding each word in the context of all surrounding words.

"I went to the bank" -- is bank a river bank or a financial bank? BERT looks at the WHOLE sentence to decide. GPT-2 only looks left.

Pre-Training Tasks

BERT was trained on Wikipedia + BookCorpus with TWO self-supervised tasks:

1. Masked Language Model (MLM):
15% of words are hidden. BERT must predict them from context.
"The [MASK] sat on the mat" -> predict "cat"

2. Next Sentence Prediction (NSP):
Given two sentences, predict if sentence B follows sentence A.

Fine-Tuning BERT

Pre-trained BERT knows language deeply. Fine-tune it for your specific task:

Add a classification head on top of the [CLS] token output.
Train for a few epochs on your labeled data.

Tasks: Sentiment analysis, spam detection, NER (named entity recognition), question answering, text similarity, document classification.

BERT Variants

RoBERTa: BERT trained more carefully (no NSP, more data) -- usually better
DistilBERT: 40% smaller, 60% faster, 97% accuracy of BERT
ALBERT: Parameter sharing makes it much smaller
BioBERT: Fine-tuned on medical literature
FinBERT: Fine-tuned on financial text
Multilingual BERT: Supports 104 languages

Tokenisation

Text -> Token IDs -> Embeddings.
BERT uses WordPiece tokenisation:
"playing" -> ["play", "##ing"] (## means continuation)

Special tokens:
[CLS] -- start of sequence (classification token)
[SEP] -- separator between sentences
[PAD] -- padding to make same length
[MASK] -- used during pre-training 

from transformers import pipeline, AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForSequenceClassification
import torch

# ── Quick way: use a pipeline ───────────────────
sentiment = pipeline('sentiment-analysis')          # load a pre-built sentiment model
result = sentiment("I love this course!")           # run on text
print(result)                                       # [{'label': 'POSITIVE', 'score': 0.999}]

# ── Fine-tuning BERT for classification ─────────
from transformers import BertForSequenceClassification, BertTokenizer
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, TensorDataset

tokenizer = BertTokenizer.from_pretrained('bert-base-uncased')   # load tokenizer
model = BertForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(
    'bert-base-uncased', num_labels=2)              # 2 output classes (positive/negative)

# Tokenize your dataset
encoded = tokenizer(texts, padding=True,           # pad shorter sentences to same length
                    truncation=True,               # cut sentences longer than max_length
                    max_length=128,                # maximum number of tokens
                    return_tensors='pt')           # return PyTorch tensors

# Fine-tune for 3 epochs
optimizer = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=2e-5)  # very small learning rate for fine-tuning
for epoch in range(3):                             # loop over data 3 times
    outputs = model(**encoded, labels=labels)      # forward pass with true labels
    loss = outputs.loss                            # cross-entropy loss
    loss.backward()                                # backpropagate gradients
    optimizer.step()                               # update weights
    optimizer.zero_grad()                          # clear gradients for next step
GEN
Generative AI
GANs . VAEs . Diffusion Models . Stable Diffusion . DALL-E . How GenAI creates images
What is Generative AI?
Discriminative AI answers "what category?" (spam or not?). Generative AI answers "can you CREATE something new?" -- text, images, audio, code, video.

Three main families: GANs (2014), VAEs (2013), Diffusion Models (2020). Today, Diffusion + Transformers dominate everything.

GAN -- Generative Adversarial Network

Two networks compete against each other:

Generator (G): tries to create fake images that look real
Discriminator (D): tries to tell real from fake

Training: G gets better at fooling D. D gets better at detecting G. Both improve.
Analogy: A counterfeiter (G) vs police (D). Each makes the other better.
Products: DeepFakes, face generation, style transfer.

VAE -- Variational Autoencoder

Encoder: compress input into a small "latent space" (z)
Decoder: reconstruct from latent space

Key trick: latent space is a continuous probability distribution, so you can SAMPLE from it to generate new things.

Analogy: Compress a photo to a description ("tall woman, red hair"), then regenerate. Change the description -> new image.

Diffusion Models -- How they work

Forward process: Gradually add random noise to an image over T steps until it's pure noise.
Reverse process: Train a neural network to UNDO each noise step.

At inference: start from pure noise -> repeatedly denoise -> get a new image.

Text conditioning: Use a text encoder (CLIP) to guide the denoising toward the description. Products: Stable Diffusion, DALL-E 3, Midjourney.

Text-to-Image Pipeline

1. User types prompt: "astronaut riding a horse on mars"
2. Text encoder (CLIP/T5) converts prompt to embedding vector
3. Latent diffusion: start from noise in compressed space
4. Denoising U-Net runs T steps, guided by text embedding
5. Decoder (VAE) upscales latent to full image

The whole thing runs in seconds on a GPU. 

from diffusers import StableDiffusionPipeline      # bring in Stable Diffusion model
import torch

# ── Generate image from text ────────────────────
pipe = StableDiffusionPipeline.from_pretrained(    # load the pre-trained diffusion model
    "runwayml/stable-diffusion-v1-5",
    torch_dtype=torch.float16)                     # use half precision to save GPU memory
pipe = pipe.to("cuda")                             # move model to GPU for faster generation

image = pipe(
    "A data scientist presenting charts at sunrise",  # text prompt describing the image
    num_inference_steps=50,                        # how many denoising steps (more = better quality)
    guidance_scale=7.5                             # how strictly to follow the prompt (7-9 is typical)
).images[0]

image.save("generated.png")                        # save the generated image to disk
LLM
Large Language Models
GPT . Claude . Llama . RLHF . RAG . Prompting . Tokenisation . Hallucination
What is an LLM?
A Large Language Model is a massive Transformer (decoder-only) trained to predict the next word given all previous words. That's it -- but doing this at SCALE (billions of parameters, trillions of words) produces an emergent ability to reason, code, translate, summarise and more.

GPT-4, Claude, Gemini, Llama -- all LLMs. Different training data, sizes, and alignment methods.

How LLMs are Trained -- 3 Phases

Phase 1 -- Pre-training:
Train on internet-scale text (trillions of tokens). Next-token prediction. Model learns language, facts, reasoning. Very expensive ($millions).

Phase 2 -- Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT):
Show the model good examples of prompt + ideal response. Teaches it to be helpful.

Phase 3 -- RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback):
Humans rank model responses. A reward model is trained. Model is optimised to produce higher-ranked responses. Makes it safe and aligned.

Key Concepts

Tokens: words or sub-words the model processes (~4 chars each)
Context Window: max tokens the model can "see" at once (GPT-4: 128k)
Temperature: 0 = deterministic, 1 = creative, >1 = random
Top-p sampling: only pick from tokens summing to top p% probability
Hallucination: model confidently states false facts -- biggest problem
Parameters: the "weights" learned during training (GPT-4: ~1.8 trillion)

Prompting Techniques

Zero-shot: just ask -- "Classify as positive/negative: I love it"
Few-shot: give 2-3 examples before asking
Chain-of-Thought (CoT): "Let's think step by step..." -- improves reasoning
System prompt: define the model's role/persona
Role prompting: "You are a senior data scientist..."
Structured output: "Reply only in JSON with keys: sentiment, score"

RAG -- Retrieval Augmented Generation

LLMs have a fixed knowledge cutoff and hallucinate facts.
RAG fix:
1. Store your documents as vector embeddings in a vector DB (Pinecone, Chroma)
2. User asks question -> embed the question
3. Find the most similar document chunks
4. Stuff the chunks into the prompt as context
5. LLM answers using REAL retrieved facts

Result: factually grounded answers from YOUR data. 

from openai import OpenAI                           # bring in OpenAI client library

client = OpenAI(api_key="your-api-key")            # connect to OpenAI with your key

# ── Zero-shot prompting ─────────────────────────
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o",                                # which model to use
    messages=[
        {"role": "system",                         # sets the model persona
         "content": "You are a helpful data science tutor."},
        {"role": "user",                           # the user question
         "content": "Explain overfitting in simple terms."}
    ],
    temperature=0.3,                               # low temp = focused and factual
    max_tokens=500                                 # maximum length of response
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)         # print the model answer

# ── Few-shot prompting ──────────────────────────
few_shot_prompt = (
    "Classify sentiment as POSITIVE or NEGATIVE.\n"
    "Text: I love it! -> POSITIVE\n"
    "Text: Terrible service -> NEGATIVE\n"
    "Text: Best purchase ever! -> "               # model will complete with POSITIVE
)

# ── RAG with LangChain ──────────────────────────
from langchain.vectorstores import Chroma           # bring in vector database
from langchain.embeddings import OpenAIEmbeddings   # bring in embedding model
from langchain.chains import RetrievalQA            # bring in RAG chain

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings()                     # convert text to vector numbers
vectorstore = Chroma.from_documents(docs,           # store your documents as searchable vectors
                                    embeddings)
retriever = vectorstore.as_retriever(               # set up search over your documents
    search_kwargs={"k": 3})                         # retrieve top 3 most relevant chunks

qa_chain = RetrievalQA.from_chain_type(            # build the full RAG pipeline
    llm=ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o"),                # which LLM to use for answering
    retriever=retriever)                            # which retriever to use for context

answer = qa_chain.run("What is our refund policy?") # ask question -- gets answer from YOUR docs
OPS
MLOps -- Production ML
Experiment tracking . Serving . Monitoring . CI/CD . Feature Store . Orchestration
What is MLOps?
MLOps = ML + DevOps. Getting models out of Jupyter notebooks and into PRODUCTION systems that actually work reliably, 24/7, for real users.

The ML Lifecycle: Data -> Experiment -> Train -> Evaluate -> Deploy -> Monitor -> Re-train -> Repeat.
MLOps tools automate and standardize every step of this loop.

1. Experiment Tracking -- MLflow

Every time you train a model, log everything:
• Hyperparameters (learning_rate=0.01, n_estimators=100)
• Metrics (accuracy=0.92, F1=0.89)
• Model artifacts (the saved model file)

Why: Without tracking, you can't reproduce your best model.
Tool: MLflow -- open source, runs locally or on cloud.

2. Data Versioning -- DVC

Like Git for data and models. Track every version of your dataset.

Why: Model accuracy depends on training data. If data changes and you don't track it, you can't debug regressions.

DVC: stores metadata in Git, actual data files in S3/GDrive/etc.

3. Model Serving -- FastAPI

Wrap your model in an API so applications can use it.
User sends a request -> API calls model.predict() -> returns prediction as JSON.

FastAPI: Python web framework. Async. Auto-generates docs.
Docker: Package your API into a container that runs anywhere.
Kubernetes: Manage many containers at scale.

4. Monitoring -- Data & Model Drift

Data drift: the distribution of incoming data changes (new customer types)
Concept drift: the relationship between features and target changes (pandemic changes buying patterns)
Model drift: accuracy degrades over time

Tools: Evidently AI, WhyLabs, Prometheus + Grafana
Action: alert -> investigate -> retrain

5. Feature Store

Centralized repository of computed features shared across teams.

Problem it solves: Team A computes "customer lifetime value" one way, Team B computes it differently -- models see different signals.

Feature store: one definition, computed once, available to all.
Tools: Feast (open source), Tecton, Vertex AI Feature Store

6. Pipeline Orchestration

Automate the whole ML pipeline: ingest -> preprocess -> train -> evaluate -> deploy.
Schedule it to run daily/weekly automatically.

Apache Airflow: most popular, DAG-based workflows
Prefect / ZenML: Python-native, easier to learn
Kubeflow: Kubernetes-native ML pipelines
AWS SageMaker Pipelines: if you're on AWS 

import mlflow                                       # bring in experiment tracking library
import mlflow.sklearn                             # bring in sklearn model logging
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score

# ── Full MLOps experiment run ───────────────────
mlflow.set_experiment("customer_churn_model")     # name the experiment in MLflow

with mlflow.start_run():                          # start tracking this training run
    # Hyperparameters
    n_estimators = 100
    max_depth = 10

    mlflow.log_param("n_estimators", n_estimators)  # save the setting to MLflow
    mlflow.log_param("max_depth", max_depth)         # save another setting

    # Train
    model = RandomForestClassifier(               # create the model
        n_estimators=n_estimators,
        max_depth=max_depth)
    model.fit(X_train, y_train)                   # teach the model using training data

    # Evaluate
    preds = model.predict(X_val)                  # predict on validation data
    acc = accuracy_score(y_val, preds)            # measure how often we were right
    mlflow.log_metric("val_accuracy", acc)        # save the score to MLflow

    # Save model
    mlflow.sklearn.log_model(model, "model")      # save the trained model as an artifact

# ── Serve with FastAPI ──────────────────────────
from fastapi import FastAPI
import joblib

app = FastAPI()                                   # create the web application
loaded_model = joblib.load("model.pkl")           # load saved model from disk

@app.post("/predict")                             # create a POST endpoint at /predict
def predict(data: dict):                          # function runs when endpoint is called
    features = [data["age"], data["salary"],      # extract features from incoming request
                data["tenure"]]
    prediction = loaded_model.predict([features])[0]  # run model on the features
    return {"prediction": int(prediction),        # return result as JSON
            "label": "churn" if prediction else "stay"}
CI/CD for Machine Learning
Continuous Integration/Continuous Deployment -- automatically test and deploy your model whenever you push new code or data.

CI -- Test your model

On every code push:
• Run unit tests on data pipelines
• Check model performance ≥ baseline
• Validate data schema
• Check for training/serving skew

Tools: GitHub Actions, Jenkins, GitLab CI

CD -- Deploy automatically

When tests pass:
• Build Docker image with model
• Push to container registry
• Deploy to staging -> run smoke tests
• Blue/green deploy to production

Tools: GitHub Actions + ECS/GKE

Model Registry

Central store for all model versions with metadata.
State transitions: None -> Staging -> Production -> Archived

MLflow Model Registry: free, local or hosted
Weights & Biases: beautiful UI
SageMaker Model Registry: AWS native

SF
Snowflake -- Cloud Data Warehouse
Architecture . SQL . Loading Data . Time Travel . Cloning . Streams . Snowpark . Security
What is Snowflake?
Snowflake is a cloud data warehouse -- a database built for ANALYTICS at massive scale. Unlike traditional databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL) designed for transactions (one row at a time), Snowflake processes MILLIONS of rows in seconds for analytical queries.

Cloud-native: Runs on AWS, Azure, or GCP. You don't manage any servers.
Unique architecture: Separates STORAGE (what you pay per TB) from COMPUTE (what you pay per query time). Scale each independently.

3-Layer Architecture

Cloud Services layer:
Authentication, query compilation, metadata, optimizer.
Always running. Snowflake manages it.

Compute layer (Virtual Warehouses):
Your query engine. Can have MANY warehouses. Each auto-scales.
You pay per second of compute. Pause when idle = $0.

Storage layer:
Columnar compressed files in S3/GCS/Azure Blob. Shared across all warehouses.

Key Concepts

Database: highest level container (like a folder)
Schema: groups related tables (like a subfolder)
Table: structured data with rows and columns
View: saved query treated like a table
Stage: location where files are loaded from (S3 bucket etc.)
Warehouse: compute engine (XS, S, M, L, XL... 2XL)
Time Travel: query data as it was at any past point (up to 90 days) 

-- ── Step 1: Create a database and schema ──────────
CREATE DATABASE my_company_db;                   -- create a new database
USE DATABASE my_company_db;                      -- switch to this database for all commands

CREATE SCHEMA analytics;                         -- create a schema inside the database
USE SCHEMA analytics;                            -- switch to this schema

-- ── Step 2: Create a virtual warehouse ─────────
CREATE WAREHOUSE my_wh                           -- create a compute engine named my_wh
  WAREHOUSE_SIZE = 'X-SMALL'                     -- start small (1 credit/hour)
  AUTO_SUSPEND = 60                              -- auto-pause after 60 seconds of no queries
  AUTO_RESUME = TRUE;                            -- automatically wake up when query arrives
USE WAREHOUSE my_wh;                             -- use this warehouse for compute

-- ── Step 3: Create a table ──────────────────────
CREATE TABLE customers (
  customer_id   INT PRIMARY KEY,                 -- unique identifier for each customer
  name          VARCHAR(100),                    -- customer name up to 100 characters
  email         VARCHAR(200),                    -- email address
  signup_date   DATE,                            -- date they joined
  country       VARCHAR(50),                     -- country code
  total_spent   DECIMAL(10,2)                    -- total amount spent, 2 decimal places
);

-- ── Step 4: Load data from S3 ───────────────────
CREATE STAGE my_s3_stage                         -- create a pointer to your S3 bucket
  URL = 's3://my-bucket/data/'
  CREDENTIALS = (AWS_KEY_ID='xxx' AWS_SECRET_KEY='yyy');

COPY INTO customers                              -- load CSV files from S3 into the table
FROM @my_s3_stage/customers.csv
FILE_FORMAT = (TYPE = 'CSV'                      -- file type is CSV
               FIELD_OPTIONALLY_ENCLOSED_BY='"'  -- fields may be quoted
               SKIP_HEADER = 1);                 -- skip the header row

-- ── Step 5: Query ───────────────────────────────
SELECT country,                                  -- pick country column
       COUNT(*) AS customer_count,               -- count customers per country
       AVG(total_spent) AS avg_spent             -- average spend per country
FROM customers
WHERE signup_date >= '2024-01-01'               -- only customers who joined in 2024
GROUP BY country                                 -- group results by country
ORDER BY avg_spent DESC                          -- sort by highest spenders first
LIMIT 10;                                        -- show top 10 only

Time Travel

Query data as it looked at ANY past point in time (up to 90 days back).

Use cases:
• Accidentally deleted data? Recover it.
• Debug: what did the table look like yesterday?
• Audit: prove what data said on a specific date. 

-- Query table as it was 1 hour ago
SELECT * FROM customers
  AT(OFFSET => -3600);                           -- 3600 seconds = 1 hour ago

-- Query at a specific timestamp
SELECT * FROM customers
  AT(TIMESTAMP => '2024-06-01 12:00:00'::TIMESTAMP);

-- Restore deleted data
CREATE TABLE customers_restored AS              -- make a new table
  SELECT * FROM customers
  BEFORE(STATEMENT => '<query_id>');          -- before a specific query ran

Zero-Copy Cloning

Clone an entire database/schema/table INSTANTLY at zero cost.
No data is copied -- it uses the same storage until one diverges.

Use cases:
• Create a dev copy of production instantly
• Test migrations safely (clone -> test -> drop)
• Give analysts their own sandbox 

-- Clone a table instantly (no cost, no data copied)
CREATE TABLE customers_dev
  CLONE customers;                               -- exact copy of table, instant, free

-- Clone an entire database for dev environment
CREATE DATABASE prod_clone
  CLONE my_company_db;                           -- all tables, views, stages cloned instantly

-- Safe migration workflow
CREATE SCHEMA new_schema CLONE analytics;       -- clone schema
-- ... make changes to new_schema ...
-- if happy: drop old, rename new
DROP SCHEMA analytics;
ALTER SCHEMA new_schema RENAME TO analytics;

Streams & Tasks -- CDC

Stream: tracks INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE changes on a table (Change Data Capture).
Task: scheduled SQL query (like a cron job inside Snowflake).

Together: auto-process new records as they arrive -- no ETL code needed. 

-- Create a stream on the source table
CREATE STREAM customers_stream
  ON TABLE customers;                            -- track all changes to this table

-- Create a task to process changes every 5 minutes
CREATE TASK process_new_customers
  WAREHOUSE = my_wh
  SCHEDULE = '5 MINUTE'                          -- run every 5 minutes
AS
  INSERT INTO customers_clean                    -- insert clean rows into target table
  SELECT * FROM customers_stream
  WHERE METADATA$ACTION = 'INSERT';             -- only new rows (not updates/deletes)

ALTER TASK process_new_customers RESUME;        -- start the task (tasks start paused)

Snowpark -- Python in Snowflake

Run Python/Java/Scala code INSIDE Snowflake -- no need to move data out.
Use pandas-like API that translates to SQL under the hood.

Perfect for: Data science, ML feature engineering, complex transformations. 

from snowflake.snowpark import Session               # bring in Snowpark library

# Connect to Snowflake
session = Session.builder.configs({               # create a connection to Snowflake
    "account": "your_account",
    "user": "your_user",
    "password": "your_password",
    "warehouse": "my_wh",
    "database": "my_company_db",
    "schema": "analytics"
}).create()

# Use pandas-like DataFrame API
df = session.table("customers")                   # load the customers table as a DataFrame
result = (df
    .filter(df["country"] == "IN")               # keep only Indian customers
    .group_by("signup_date")                      # group by date
    .agg({"total_spent": "sum"})                  # sum spending per date
    .sort("signup_date"))                         # sort by date

result.show()                                     # display first 10 rows
result.write.save_as_table("india_daily_spend")  # save result back to Snowflake
Snowflake Role Hierarchy (RBAC)
ACCOUNTADMIN > SYSADMIN > SECURITYADMIN > PUBLIC
Best practice: Give analysts a custom ANALYST role with read-only access to specific schemas. Never use ACCOUNTADMIN for daily work. 
-- Role-Based Access Control
CREATE ROLE analyst_role;                        -- create a role for analysts
GRANT USAGE ON DATABASE my_company_db TO ROLE analyst_role;   -- allow seeing the DB
GRANT USAGE ON SCHEMA analytics TO ROLE analyst_role;          -- allow seeing the schema
GRANT SELECT ON ALL TABLES IN SCHEMA analytics TO ROLE analyst_role;  -- read-only on all tables

CREATE USER john_analyst
  PASSWORD = 'SecurePass123'
  DEFAULT_ROLE = analyst_role;                   -- set analyst role as default
GRANT ROLE analyst_role TO USER john_analyst;   -- give the role to the user

-- Cost control: limit credits used per day
ALTER RESOURCE MONITOR my_monitor SET
  CREDIT_QUOTA = 100                             -- alert when 100 credits used today
  NOTIFY_USERS = ('admin@company.com');
